topics.title: Guidelines to Managing Conflict
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT724 - FOOD INDUSTRY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Guidelines to Managing Conflict

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is Organizational Conflict?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Organizational Behaviour):**
> **Conflict** is a process that begins when one party perceives that another party has negatively affected, or is about to negatively affect, something that the first party cares about. (Stephen P. Robins, KTU Reference Framework)

In a Food Industry context, conflict arises when the goals, values, interests, or perceptions of production managers, quality assurance teams, marketing personnel, supply chain officers, or workers diverge in such a way that the actions of one party are perceived as obstructing the objectives of another.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Brigade" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen during the dinner rush. The *Sous Chef* wants to plate dishes quickly (speed), the *Pastry Chef* insists on perfect temperature for desserts (quality), and the *Expeditor* is trying to coordinate timing (logistics). They are all working toward the same goal — customer satisfaction — but they are pulling in different directions.

Just like in a professional kitchen, in a **food processing plant**, the Quality Control Head may clash with the Production Manager over batch hold-release decisions; the Sales team may conflict with Logistics over unrealistic delivery dates; or the R&D team may conflict with Plant Operations over pilot-scale trials. Without structured **conflict management guidelines**, these disagreements escalate, leading to delays, wastage, lost revenue, and regulatory non-compliance.

### 1.3 Three Standard Lenses to View Conflict

| Perspective | Core View | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Traditional View** | Conflict is harmful and must be avoided | Conflict = bad; suppress it |
| **Human Relations View** | Conflict is natural and inevitable | Conflict is normal in groups |
| **Interactionist View** | Conflict is necessary for performance | Functional conflict drives innovation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants & Metrics (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Functional Conflict:** Conflict that *supports* organizational goals (constructive dissent, quality debates).
> - **Dysfunctional Conflict:** Conflict that *hinders* organizational performance (personal vendettas, sabotage).
> - **BATNA:** Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement (Roger Fisher & William Ury, *Getting to Yes* — generic OB reference).

### 1.4 Five Universal Sources of Conflict

1. **Communication Differentials** — Misinformation, poor listening, semantic noise.
2. **Structural Variables** — Specialization, goal incompatibility, authority relationships.
3. **Personal Variables** — Individual values, emotions, personality traits, ethics.
4. **Resource Scarcity** — Limited raw materials, machinery time, or labor.
5. **Role Ambiguity** — Unclear job descriptions in HACCP, FSSAI, or production teams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET

## 2.1 The Seven Golden Guidelines to Managing Conflict (KTU Board Favourite)

### Guideline 1 — Diagnose the Conflict Type and Source
- Identify **where** the conflict exists (intrapersonal, interpersonal, intragroup, intergroup, organizational).
- Identify **why** it exists (communication, structure, or personal variables).
- **Why?** A wrong diagnosis leads to a wrong prescription.

### Guideline 2 — Separate the People from the Problem
- Treat the conflict as a *shared problem* to be solved, not a *personal battle* to be won.
- Distinguish **substantive issues** (the actual dispute — e.g., cold-storage allocation) from **relationship issues** (ego, past grudges).
- **How?** Use objective language, "I-statements," and avoid blame.

### Guideline 3 — Focus on Interests, Not Positions
- A *position* is what a party says it wants; an *interest* is why it wants it.
- Example: A worker says, "I will not take the night shift" (position). Underlying interest: "I need to care for my child" (interest). Solutions emerge from interests.
- **How?** Ask "Why?" and "Why not?" repeatedly.

### Guideline 4 — Generate Options for Mutual Gain
- Brainstorm multiple alternatives before deciding.
- Use the **Harvard 4-Fold Method**:
  1. Invent options for mutual gain.
  2. Look for shared interests.
  3. Make decisions easy for the other party.
  4. Make the package attractive.

### Guideline 5 — Use Objective / Legitimate Criteria
- Base decisions on independent standards: industry benchmarks, FSSAI regulations, ISO 22000 standards, published cost data.
- Avoid decisions based on will, power, or emotion alone.

### Guideline 6 — Develop BATNA and Know Your Walk-Away Point
- Every negotiator must know the **Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement**.
- Strong BATNA = stronger bargaining position.

### Guideline 7 — Establish Clear Communication Channels and Follow-Up
- Use active listening, paraphrase, and feedback loops.
- Document resolutions, assign ownership, and review at defined intervals.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Conflict Management Styles — Thomas-Kilmann Model)

| Style | Assertiveness | Cooperativeness | When to Use in Food Industry | Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Competing (Forcing)** | High | Low | Safety/quality emergencies; batch recall decisions | Damages long-term relationships |
| **Collaborating (Problem-Solving)** | High | High | Cross-functional new product development (NPD) | Time-intensive |
| **Compromising** | Medium | Medium | Mid-stakes scheduling or supplier disputes | Both sides may feel unsatisfied |
| **Avoiding** | Low | Low | Trivial issues; when cooling-off is needed | Conflict may resurface later |
| **Accommodating** | Low | High | Preserving team harmony; when issue matters less to you | May encourage future exploitation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Recommended Reading Mapping (KTU 2024):** The Thomas-Kilmann Conflict Mode Instrument (TKI) is the most-cited framework in KTU OB papers on conflict management.

## 2.3 Conflict Resolution Process (5-Step)

1. **Identify** the conflict and stakeholders.
2. **Analyze** causes (use 5-Whys or fishbone analysis).
3. **Choose** the appropriate strategy (avoid / compete / accommodate / compromise / collaborate).
4. **Implement** the resolution with clear timelines.
5. **Monitor and Review** outcomes; feed into continuous improvement (PDCA cycle).

## 2.4 Utility in Food Industry

- **Plant floor disputes** → Use Collaborating style + objective safety data.
- **Supplier delivery conflicts** → Use Compromising + BATNA-based negotiations.
- **Cross-departmental NPD conflict** → Use Collaborating + focus on interests.
- **Quality vs. Production deadline** → Use Competing (safety non-negotiable).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL FRAMEWORK & MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix for Conflict Resolution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step analytical framework** that a KTU Board examiner expects when a 14-mark question is asked on "Guidelines to Managing Conflict."

### Step 1 — Conflict Identification

$$\text{Conflict Magnitude (CM)} = f(\text{Frequency}, \text{Intensity}, \text{Duration})$$

If **CM** is high → escalate to formal resolution. If low → informal mediation suffices.

### Step 2 — Source Diagnosis (5-Whys Analytical Method)

Let us analyze an actual KTU-style case: *"Workers in the dairy packaging section of Plant A are refusing to operate a new automated filler, claiming it has reduced their wages. Management says the machine was installed to improve FSSAI compliance."*

$$\text{Why 1: Why are workers refusing?} \rightarrow \text{Output-based wages have dropped}$$

$$\text{Why 2: Why have wages dropped?} \rightarrow \text{Machine output exceeds manual capacity}$$

$$\text{Why 3: Why is output-based wage continuing despite automation?} \rightarrow \text{HR policy not updated}$$

$$\text{Why 4: Why was policy not updated?} \rightarrow \text{No consultation with union during installation}$$

$$\text{Why 5: Why no consultation?} \rightarrow \text{Communication gap between Engineering, HR, and Shop-floor}$$

**Conclusion:** Root cause = **Communication + Structural** variables.

### Step 3 — Style Selection (Objective Decision Logic)

$$\text{Style Choice} = \arg\max_{s \in S} \; \big[ w_1 \cdot \text{Assertiveness}(s) + w_2 \cdot \text{Cooperativeness}(s) + w_3 \cdot \text{Issue Importance}(s) \big]$$

Where:
- $S = \{\text{Avoid}, \text{Compete}, \text{Accommodate}, \text{Compromise}, \text{Collaborate}\}$
- $w_1, w_2, w_3$ are weights assigned by the manager based on context.

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
- Issue: FSSAI compliance violation (high importance) → $w_3 = 0.8$
- Need to preserve worker cooperation → $w_2 = 0.5$
- Assertiveness of style: Compete = 0.9, Collaborate = 0.9, Compromise = 0.5, Accommodate = 0.2, Avoid = 0.1.
- Cooperativeness of style: Compete = 0.2, Collaborate = 0.9, Compromise = 0.5, Accommodate = 0.9, Avoid = 0.1.

$$\text{Score}_{\text{Collaborate}} = (0.4 \times 0.9) + (0.5 \times 0.9) + (0.1 \times 0.8) = 0.36 + 0.45 + 0.08 = 0.89$$

$$\text{Score}_{\text{Compete}} = (0.4 \times 0.9) + (0.5 \times 0.2) + (0.1 \times 0.8) = 0.36 + 0.10 + 0.08 = 0.54$$

**Decision:** **Collaborating** style yields the highest score → choose collaborative problem-solving.

### Step 4 — Resolution Implementation Matrix

| Action | Owner | Timeline | Success Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| Form joint committee (Workers + HR + Engineering) | HR Head | 3 days | Committee formed |
| Revise wage policy to blended model (fixed + variable) | HR + Finance | 14 days | Policy approved |
| Retrain workers on new machine | Production Manager | 21 days | 100% workers certified |
| Communicate FSSAI benefits to workers | Quality Head | 7 days | Town-hall completed |
| Review after 30 days | Plant Head | 30 days | Output & morale measured |

### Step 5 — Monitoring Using PDCA Cycle

$$\text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Check} \rightarrow \text{Act} \rightarrow \text{re-enter Plan}$$

The Check phase measures: complaint frequency, attendance, defect rate, productivity index. The Act phase institutionalises successful practices via Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).

## 3.2 Analytical Insights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insights from the Framework:**
> - The most common **error in conflict management** is jumping to a style *before* diagnosing the source.
> - A **functional conflict** (e.g., a debate over cold-chain temperature standards) improves decision quality.
> - **BATNA strength** determines the negotiator's zone of possible agreement (ZOPA).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Conflict Resolution Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Identify Conflict] --> B[Step 2: Diagnose Source]
    B --> C[Step 3: Analyze Stakeholders and Interests]
    C --> D[Step 4: Choose Strategy]
    D --> E1[Compete]
    D --> E2[Collaborate]
    D --> E3[Compromise]
    D --> E4[Accommodate]
    D --> E5[Avoid]
    E1 --> F[Step 5: Implement Resolution]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G[Step 6: Monitor and Review]
    G --> H{Conflict Resolved?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Document and Institutionalise]
    H -- No --> B
    I --> J[Continuous Improvement via PDCA]
```

## 4.2 Five Conflict Management Styles — Mapping Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HR[High Cooperativeness]
        AC[Accommodating Style] --> CL[Collaborating Style]
    end
    subgraph LR2[Low Cooperativeness]
        AV[Avoiding Style] --> CP[Competing Style]
    end
    subgraph CM[Mid Cooperativeness]
        CR[Compromising Style]
    end
    CR --> CL
    CR --> CP
    CL --- CP
    AC --- CR
    AV --- CR
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Conflict Management System in a Food Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN[Input Layer: Conflict Triggers]
        I1[Resource Scarcity]
        I2[Communication Gap]
        I3[Role Ambiguity]
        I4[Value Mismatch]
    end
    subgraph PR[Processing Layer: Diagnosis and Style Selection]
        P1[Conflict Identification Module]
        P2[5-Whys Root Cause Analyzer]
        P3[Thomas-Kilmann Style Selector]
        P4[BATNA and ZOPA Calculator]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output Layer: Resolution Mechanisms]
        O1[Negotiation and Mediation]
        O2[Arbitration]
        O3[Grievance Redressal Committee]
        O4[HR Policy Revision]
    end
    subgraph FB[Feedback Layer: Continuous Improvement]
        F1[KPI Dashboard]
        F2[PDCA Review Cycle]
        F3[SOP Update Module]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    I4 --> P1
    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    P4 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
    P4 --> O4
    O1 --> F1
    O2 --> F1
    O3 --> F1
    O4 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> P1
```

## 4.4 Negotiation Sequence for Food Industry Supplier Conflict

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant B as Buyer Plant
    participant S as Supplier
    B ->> S: Open with objective criteria
    S ->> B: State interests
    B ->> S: Disclose priorities
    S ->> B: Generate options
    B ->> S: Evaluate options against BATNA
    S ->> B: Reach ZOPA agreement
    B ->> S: Sign SLA with review clause
    Note over B,S: PDCA cycle every 90 days
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define organizational conflict. Differentiate between functional and dysfunctional conflict.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Organizational conflict is a process in which one party perceives that another party has negatively affected, or is about to negatively affect, something it cares about.
- **Functional Conflict (1 mark):** Conflict that supports the goals of the organization and improves performance (e.g., a constructive debate over FSSAI label compliance that prevents a recall).
- **Dysfunctional Conflict (1 mark):** Conflict that hinders organizational performance and may lead to absenteeism, sabotage, or strikes (e.g., personal hostility between two shift supervisors causing production delays).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Briefly explain the Thomas-Kilmann conflict management styles.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Thomas-Kilmann model identifies five styles based on **assertiveness** and **cooperativeness**: (1) **Competing** — high assertiveness, low cooperativeness; used in emergencies. (2) **Collaborating** — high on both; used for integrative problem-solving. (3) **Compromising** — medium on both; quick partial win-win. (4) **Avoiding** — low on both; postpones issue. (5) **Accommodating** — low assertiveness, high cooperativeness; preserves relationships (3 marks — 1 mark for framework, 1 mark for listing, 1 mark for contextual application).

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** Discuss in detail the **seven major guidelines to managing conflict** in a food processing organization. Apply your answer to a real-world food industry scenario.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Outline any five guidelines to managing conflict and explain each with a food industry example.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Diagnose the Type and Source of Conflict (1.4 marks):** A manager in a frozen-food plant must first determine whether the dispute between the cold-store operator and the dispatch team is about *communication*, *structure*, or *personal variables*. Diagnosis prevents misdirected solutions.

2. **Separate People from the Problem (1.4 marks):** The manager should focus on the *issue* (delay in shipments) rather than the *individuals* (Ramesh vs Suresh). Use objective facts like temperature log data and dispatch timestamps.

3. **Focus on Interests, Not Positions (1.4 marks):** If a worker says, *"I will not work overtime"* (position), the manager should explore the interest: *"You need to attend your child's school function."* The real interest may be solved with a shift swap.

4. **Generate Options for Mutual Gain (1.4 marks):** Brainstorm 4–5 alternatives. Example: staggered overtime, bonus for extra hours, comp-off, transport facility, or task re-allocation.

5. **Use Objective Criteria (1.4 marks):** Base decisions on FSSAI regulations, ISO 22000 standards, published wage data, or industry benchmark KPIs.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Thomas-Kilmann conflict management styles with a decision logic for selecting an appropriate style in a food plant.**

**Model Solution:**

The Thomas-Kilmann Conflict Mode Instrument (TKI) identifies **five conflict-handling styles** based on two dimensions — *assertiveness* (the degree to which one tries to satisfy one's own concerns) and *cooperativeness* (the degree to which one tries to satisfy the other party's concerns). The five styles are:

- **Competing (Forcing):** High assertiveness, low cooperativeness. Used in food safety emergencies, e.g., when Quality Control halts a production line for a contamination risk and the Production Manager demands continuation.
- **Collaborating (Problem-Solving):** High on both. Used in cross-functional new product development (NPD) where R&D, Marketing, and Production must jointly decide a recipe scale-up.
- **Compromising:** Medium on both. Used in supplier delivery disputes where neither party gets everything.
- **Avoiding:** Low on both. Used when the issue is trivial or emotions are high and require cooling-off.
- **Accommodating:** Low assertiveness, high cooperativeness. Used when the issue matters more to the other party or preserving long-term relationships is critical.

**Decision Logic (1 mark):**
$$\text{If Issue Importance} = \text{High AND Relationship} = \text{Valuable} \Rightarrow \text{Collaborate}$$
$$\text{If Issue Importance} = \text{High AND Relationship} = \text{Low} \Rightarrow \text{Compete}$$
$$\text{If Issue Importance} = \text{Low AND Relationship} = \text{Valuable} \Rightarrow \text{Accommodate}$$
$$\text{If Issue Importance} = \text{Low AND Relationship} = \text{Low} \Rightarrow \text{Avoid}$$
$$\text{If Time Pressure} = \text{High} \Rightarrow \text{Compromise}$$

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Retest Paper]** *"Conflict in a food industry is inevitable, but its effective management is a choice."* Critically analyse this statement with reference to the **sources, types, and resolution strategies** of organizational conflict.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the major sources and types of conflict with food industry examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Conflict (3.5 marks):**
1. **Intrapersonal Conflict:** A Quality Manager torn between meeting a tight FSSAI audit deadline and reporting a non-conformance.
2. **Interpersonal Conflict:** Two production supervisors arguing over shift handover documentation.
3. **Intragroup Conflict:** Members of the NPD team disagreeing on the launch flavour profile.
4. **Intergroup Conflict:** Sales team vs. Production team clashing over an unrealistic festival-season order commitment.
5. **Organizational Conflict:** A company-wide strike over a new wage policy.

**Sources of Conflict (3.5 marks):**
1. **Communication Differentials:** Misinterpretation of a memo about new allergen labelling rules.
2. **Structural Variables:** Specialized departments with incompatible goals (e.g., cost-cutting vs. premium-quality sourcing).
3. **Personal Variables:** Different value systems, personality clashes, and emotional states.
4. **Resource Scarcity:** Limited cold-storage space during peak mango season.
5. **Role Ambiguity:** Unclear ownership of pest-control compliance between Quality and Housekeeping.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the resolution strategies and the role of negotiation, mediation, and arbitration in food industry conflict management.**

**Model Solution:**

**Resolution Strategies (3.5 marks):**
1. **Problem-Solving (Confrontation):** Direct face-to-face discussion of the issue, suitable for high-trust teams.
2. **Smoothing:** Playing down differences and emphasising common interests.
3. **Compromise:** Each side gives up something to reach a middle ground.
4. **Superordinate Goals:** Establishing a shared higher goal (e.g., *"Our customers' safety is paramount"*) that unites departments.
5. **Avoidance/Withdrawal:** Temporary postponement to allow emotions to subside.
6. **Forcing/Authoritative Command:** Management directives to settle disputes quickly.

**Role of Negotiation, Mediation, and Arbitration (3.5 marks):**
- **Negotiation:** Voluntary, direct discussion between parties. E.g., Buyer and supplier renegotiate delivery schedule using objective data.
- **Mediation:** A neutral third party facilitates discussion but does not impose a solution. E.g., HR Head mediates between union and management over wage revision.
- **Arbitration:** A neutral third party hears both sides and imposes a binding decision. E.g., Labour Court arbitration in cases of illegal strike or wrongful termination.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often *list* the guidelines but fail to **explain with food industry context** — KTU expects application, not just theory.
> 2. In the Thomas-Kilmann question, students frequently **confuse assertiveness with cooperativeness** — memorise the two dimensions carefully.
> 3. In negotiation questions, students forget to **define BATNA and ZOPA** — these are high-weightage KTU keywords.
> 4. Avoid writing generic OB answers without **mapping to a food plant scenario** — KTU 2024 scheme is highly application-oriented.
> 5. Many students skip the **PDCA / continuous improvement loop** at the end of resolution — losing 1–2 marks per question.

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- **Conflict Definition:** Perception of incompatible goals, interests, or values between parties.
- **Three Views of Conflict:** Traditional (bad) → Human Relations (natural) → Interactionist (necessary).
- **Five Types of Conflict:** Intrapersonal, Interpersonal, Intragroup, Intergroup, Organizational.
- **Five Major Sources:** Communication, Structure, Personal Variables, Resource Scarcity, Role Ambiguity.
- **Thomas-Kilmann Five Styles:** Compete, Collaborate, Compromise, Avoid, Accommodate — based on *assertiveness* and *cooperativeness*.
- **Seven Golden Guidelines:**
  1. Diagnose the type and source
  2. Separate people from the problem
  3. Focus on interests, not positions
  4. Generate options for mutual gain (Harvard 4-Fold Method)
  5. Use objective / legitimate criteria
  6. Develop BATNA and ZOPA awareness
  7. Establish clear communication and follow-up
- **Resolution Strategies:** Problem-solving, Smoothing, Compromise, Superordinate Goals, Avoidance, Forcing.
- **Third-Party Mechanisms (in increasing formality):** Negotiation → Mediation → Arbitration.
- **Functional vs Dysfunctional Conflict:** Functional supports goals; Dysfunctional hinders them.
- **PDCA Cycle:** Plan → Do → Check → Act — used for continuous improvement of conflict resolution.
- **Food Industry Application Hooks:** FSSAI compliance, ISO 22000, HACCP, cold-chain, NPD, supplier SLAs, wage negotiations, shift scheduling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assessing, Acquiring, and Allocating Funds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT724 - FOOD INDUSTRY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Assessing, Acquiring, and Allocating Funds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Food Industry Management, **Assessing, Acquiring, and Allocating Funds** constitutes the strategic financial management triad that determines the long-term viability, scalability, and profitability of food processing enterprises. It encompasses the systematic identification of monetary requirements, the procurement of capital from optimal sources, and the prudent deployment of these resources into revenue-generating assets, working capital cycles, and operational infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Fund management in the food industry involves three interconnected decisions:*
> 1. **Investment Decision (Assessing & Allocating)** — where and how much to invest (Capital Budgeting).
> 2. **Financing Decision (Acquiring)** — how to raise the required capital (Capital Structure).
> 3. **Dividend Decision** — how to distribute profits vs. retain earnings.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Budget" Intuition

Imagine a mid-sized food processing plant as a large commercial kitchen:
- **Assessing Funds** is like the head chef taking inventory of ingredients, equipment wear-and-tear, and upcoming catering orders to estimate *how much money* the kitchen will need next quarter.
- **Acquiring Funds** is choosing *where* the money comes from — taking a bank loan (debt), bringing in a business partner (equity), or using last month's profits (retained earnings).
- **Allocating Funds** is the chef deciding *where* every rupee goes — buying a new blast freezer, paying staff salaries, or stocking raw materials.

Just as a chef must balance flavor, cost, and nutrition, a food industry manager must balance **risk, return, and liquidity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metrics Used:**
> - **WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital)** — typically ranges between **8%–15%** for Indian food industries.
> - **Working Capital Ratio (Current Ratio)** — ideal benchmark is **2:1**.
> - **Debt-Equity Ratio** — ideal for food SMEs is **1:1 to 1.5:1**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stage 1 — Assessing Funds (Financial Requirement Analysis)

The assessment phase determines *how much* capital is required and *when*. It is a forecasting exercise grounded in three pillars:

1. **Fixed Capital Assessment**
   - Land, building, processing machinery (extruders, autoclaves, packaging lines)
   - Estimated via **Detailed Project Report (DPR)** and **Technical Feasibility Studies**
   - Tools: **Trend Analysis**, **Ratio Analysis**, **Sales Forecast Models**

2. **Working Capital Assessment**
   - Raw material inventory (grains, spices, dairy)
   - Receivables from distributors
   - Operating cash buffer
   - Tools: **Operating Cycle Method**, **Cash Budgeting**

3. **Intangible & Strategic Capital**
   - Brand building, FSSAI certification, R&D for new product development
   - Market expansion funds

### 2.2 Stage 2 — Acquiring Funds (Sources of Finance)

Funds are sourced along a **risk-return spectrum**:

| Source Type | Examples | Risk Level | Cost |
|-------------|----------|------------|------|
| **Owner's Equity** | Promoter's capital, Sweat equity | Low (no repayment) | High (dividends) |
| **Retained Earnings** | Ploughed-back profits | Very Low | Lowest |
| **Debt — Long Term** | Bank loans, Bonds, Debentures | Medium (default risk) | Medium (interest) |
| **Debt — Short Term** | Trade credit, Working capital loans, Commercial paper | High (rollover) | Lower |
| **External Equity** | Venture Capital, Private Equity, IPO | Low (no fixed obligation) | High (dilution) |
| **Government/Institutional** | MUDRA loans, NABARD, FPI subsidies | Lowest | Subsidized |

### 2.3 Stage 3 — Allocating Funds (Capital Budgeting & Deployment)

Allocation follows the **Investment Decision Hierarchy**:

- **Step 1:** Identify investment proposals (new packaging line, cold storage expansion).
- **Step 2:** Evaluate using capital budgeting techniques.
- **Step 3:** Rank proposals using NPV or PI.
- **Step 4:** Implement and monitor via **post-audit**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU ESE**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | $CF_t$ = Cash flow at year t (₹); $r$ = Discount rate (%) |
| 2 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | $IRR$ in % |
| 3 | **Payback Period (PBP)** | $PBP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ | Years |
| 4 | **Discounted Payback Period** | Cumulative discounted CF $\geq$ Investment | Years |
| 5 | **Profitability Index (PI)** | $PI = \dfrac{PV \text{ of Future Cash Flows}}{\text{Initial Investment}}$ | Dimensionless; Accept if PI $\geq 1$ |
| 6 | **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** | $WACC = \left(\dfrac{E}{V}\right) R_e + \left(\dfrac{D}{V}\right) R_d (1-T)$ | $E$=Equity, $D$=Debt, $V=E+D$, $T$=Tax rate |
| 7 | **Cost of Equity (CAPM)** | $R_e = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f)$ | $R_f$=Risk-free rate, $\beta$=Beta, $R_m$=Market return |
| 8 | **Cost of Debt (After-tax)** | $R_d(1-T) = \dfrac{I(1-T)}{D}$ | $I$=Interest, $D$=Debt principal |
| 9 | **Operating Cycle** | $OC = RMI + WIP + FG + DR - DC$ | Days |
| 10 | **Working Capital** | $WC = CA - CL$ | ₹ (Current Assets − Current Liabilities) |
| 11 | **Current Ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{CA}{CL}$ | Ideal = **2:1** |
| 12 | **Debt-Equity Ratio** | $D/E = \dfrac{Total Debt}{Total Equity}$ | Ideal = **1:1** |
| 13 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Cost of Investment}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 14 | **EBIT-EPS Analysis** | $EPS = \dfrac{(EBIT - I)(1-T) - PD}{N}$ | $I$=Interest, $PD$=Pref. Div., $N$=No. of shares |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In food plant commissioning (e.g., setting up a ₹50 crore dairy plant), these formulas directly determine whether the project is **bankable** for NABARD funding and whether it clears the **hurdle rate** set by institutional investors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: NPV Decision Criterion

**Problem Setup:** A food processing company is evaluating a new cold-chain logistics project. Initial investment = ₹10,00,000. Expected cash inflows for 5 years = ₹3,00,000 per annum. Cost of capital = 10%. Tax is ignored for simplicity.

**Step 1 — Identify the Cash Flow Stream**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CF_0 &= -10{,}00{,}000 \quad (\text{Initial Outlay}) \\
CF_1 &= CF_2 = CF_3 = CF_4 = CF_5 = 3{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the NPV Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} \\
&= CF_0 + \dfrac{CF_1}{(1+r)^1} + \dfrac{CF_2}{(1+r)^2} + \dfrac{CF_3}{(1+r)^3} + \dfrac{CF_4}{(1+r)^4} + \dfrac{CF_5}{(1+r)^5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values (r = 0.10)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -10{,}00{,}000 + \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.10} + \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.21} + \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.331} + \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.4641} + \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.61051}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Each Discounted Cash Flow (DCF)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCF_1 &= 3{,}00{,}000 \div 1.10 = 2{,}72{,}727.27 \\
DCF_2 &= 3{,}00{,}000 \div 1.21 = 2{,}47{,}933.88 \\
DCF_3 &= 3{,}00{,}000 \div 1.331 = 2{,}25{,}394.44 \\
DCF_4 &= 3{,}00{,}000 \div 1.4641 = 2{,}04{,}904.04 \\
DCF_5 &= 3{,}00{,}000 \div 1.61051 = 1{,}86{,}276.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum All Discounted Cash Flows (PVCF)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PVCF &= 2{,}72{,}727.27 + 2{,}47{,}933.88 + 2{,}25{,}394.44 + 2{,}04{,}904.04 + 1{,}86{,}276.40 \\
&= 11{,}37{,}236.03
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate Final NPV**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= PVCF - \text{Initial Investment} \\
&= 11{,}37{,}236.03 - 10{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \boxed{+1{,}37{,}236.03}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Decision Rule**

Since **NPV > 0** (i.e., ₹1,37,236.03 > 0), the project is **financially viable** and should be **accepted**.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: WACC Calculation

**Problem Setup:** A food company's capital structure is:
- Equity (E) = ₹60,00,000
- Debt (D) = ₹40,00,000
- Cost of Equity ($R_e$) = 14%
- Cost of Debt ($R_d$) = 10%
- Tax Rate (T) = 30%

**Step 1 — Compute Total Capital (V)**

$$
V = E + D = 60{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Weights**

$$
\dfrac{E}{V} = \dfrac{60{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000} = 0.60
$$

$$
\dfrac{D}{V} = \dfrac{40{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000} = 0.40
$$

**Step 3 — Apply WACC Formula**

$$
WACC = \left(\dfrac{E}{V}\right) R_e + \left(\dfrac{D}{V}\right) R_d (1-T)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WACC &= (0.60 \times 0.14) + (0.40 \times 0.10 \times (1 - 0.30)) \\
&= 0.084 + (0.40 \times 0.10 \times 0.70) \\
&= 0.084 + 0.028 \\
&= 0.112
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer**

$$
WACC = 0.112 = \boxed{11.2\%}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**

This 11.2% becomes the **hurdle rate**. Any new food project must generate a return **greater than 11.2%** to create shareholder value.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Capital Budgeting Techniques

| Technique | Formula Basis | Decision Rule | Strength | Weakness |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|----------|----------|
| **NPV** | Time value of money | Accept if NPV $\geq 0$ | Most accurate, considers all CFs | Requires discount rate |
| **IRR** | Time value of money | Accept if IRR $\geq r$ | Communicates % return | Multiple IRR problem |
| **Payback** | Liquidity focus | Accept if PBP $\leq$ Target years | Simple, emphasizes liquidity | Ignores time value |
| **Profitability Index** | Relative profitability | Accept if PI $\geq 1$ | Useful for capital rationing | Still needs discount rate |
| **ARR** | Accounting-based | Accept if ARR $\geq$ Target | Uses accounting data | Ignores cash flow timing |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fund Management Lifecycle in Food Industry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Industry Enterprise] --> B[Stage 1: Assessing Funds]
    B --> B1[Fixed Capital Needs]
    B --> B2[Working Capital Needs]
    B --> B3[Intangible Strategic Needs]
    B1 --> C[Stage 2: Acquiring Funds]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Equity Sources]
    C --> C2[Debt Sources]
    C --> C3[Government Subsidies]
    C --> C4[Retained Earnings]
    C1 --> D[Stage 3: Allocating Funds]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> D1[Capital Budgeting - NPV/IRR]
    D --> D2[Working Capital Management]
    D --> D3[Dividend Policy]
    D1 --> E[Performance Monitoring]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F{Revaluation & Reinvestment}
    F -->|Yes| B
    F -->|No| G[Steady-State Operations]
```

### 4.2 Capital Budgeting Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Investment Proposal for Food Plant] --> P2[Estimate Cash Inflows]
    P2 --> P3[Determine Cost of Capital WACC]
    P3 --> P4[Apply NPV Test]
    P4 --> P5{NPV Decision}
    P5 -->|NPV > 0| P6[Accept Project]
    P5 -->|NPV = 0| P7[Indifferent - Marginal]
    P5 -->|NPV < 0| P8[Reject Project]
    P6 --> P9[Cross-verify with IRR]
    P9 --> P10{IRR > WACC?}
    P10 -->|Yes| P11[Definite Acceptance]
    P10 -->|No| P12[Reassumptions - Conflict]
    P8 --> P13[Explore Alternatives]
    P11 --> P14[Implementation & Post-Audit]
```

### 4.3 Sources of Finance — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    S[Sources of Finance] --> S1[Internal Sources]
    S --> S2[External Sources]
    S1 --> S1A[Retained Earnings]
    S1 --> S1B[Depreciation Funds]
    S1 --> S1C[Working Capital Release]
    S2 --> S2A[Long Term External]
    S2 --> S2B[Short Term External]
    S2A --> S2A1[Equity Share Capital]
    S2A --> S2A2[Preference Shares]
    S2A --> S2A3[Debentures and Bonds]
    S2A --> S2A4[Term Loans from Banks]
    S2A --> S2A5[Venture Capital]
    S2B --> S2B1[Bank Overdraft]
    S2B --> S2B2[Trade Credit]
    S2B --> S2B3[Commercial Paper]
    S2B --> S2B4[Factoring Services]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**Q: Define Capital Budgeting. List any four capital budgeting techniques used in the food industry.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Capital Budgeting is the process of planning, evaluating, and selecting long-term investment projects that are expected to generate cash flows over multiple years. In the food industry, it applies to decisions such as setting up a new processing line, expanding cold storage, or acquiring packaging machinery.

**Four Techniques (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Net Present Value (NPV)**
2. **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**
3. **Payback Period Method**
4. **Profitability Index (PI)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students confuse **Capital Budgeting** with **Working Capital Management**. Capital Budgeting = long-term investment decisions. Working Capital = short-term operational liquidity. Do not interchange the two in your answer.

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: Differentiate between Equity Financing and Debt Financing. State one advantage of each in the context of a food processing unit.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Equity Financing | Debt Financing |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|
| **Nature** | Ownership capital | Borrowed capital |
| **Obligation** | No fixed repayment | Mandatory interest + principal |
| **Cost** | Dividends (not tax-deductible) | Interest (tax-deductible) |
| **Risk** | Lower (no default risk) | Higher (bankruptcy risk) |
| **Control** | Dilutes ownership | No dilution |

**Advantage — Equity (1 Mark):** No mandatory repayment obligation; suitable during seasonal cash flow crunches common in food industries.

**Advantage — Debt (1 Mark):** Interest is tax-deductible, lowering the effective cost of borrowing; ownership remains intact.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Do not write generic textbook definitions. Always tie your answer to the **food industry context** (e.g., seasonal raw material costs, cold chain investments) to fetch full marks under KTU's application-based evaluation.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[MODULE 3 — INTERNAL CHOICE — ANSWER EITHER QUESTION A OR QUESTION B]**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]

**A food processing company is evaluating two mutually exclusive projects for expansion of its ready-to-eat meal line. The following data is provided:**

| Year | Project X (₹ in lakhs) | Project Y (₹ in lakhs) |
|------|------------------------|------------------------|
| 0 (Initial Investment) | 50 | 70 |
| 1 | 15 | 25 |
| 2 | 20 | 25 |
| 3 | 20 | 25 |
| 4 | 15 | 20 |
| 5 | 10 | 15 |

**Cost of Capital = 12%.**

**Sub-parts:**
**(a)** Calculate the **Net Present Value (NPV)** of both projects. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Calculate the **Profitability Index (PI)** and recommend which project should be selected. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for (a) — NPV Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Discounting Project X (r = 12%)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_X &= -50 + \dfrac{15}{1.12} + \dfrac{20}{1.12^2} + \dfrac{20}{1.12^3} + \dfrac{15}{1.12^4} + \dfrac{10}{1.12^5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Each Discounted Cash Flow for Project X**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCF_{X1} &= 15 \div 1.12 = 13.393 \\
DCF_{X2} &= 20 \div 1.2544 = 15.944 \\
DCF_{X3} &= 20 \div 1.4049 = 14.236 \\
DCF_{X4} &= 15 \div 1.5735 = 9.533 \\
DCF_{X5} &= 10 \div 1.7623 = 5.675
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum of PVCF for Project X**

$$
PVCF_X = 13.393 + 15.944 + 14.236 + 9.533 + 5.675 = 58.781
$$

**Step 4 — NPV of Project X**

$$
NPV_X = 58.781 - 50 = \boxed{8.781 \text{ lakhs}}
$$

**Step 5 — Discounting Project Y (r = 12%)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCF_{Y1} &= 25 \div 1.12 = 22.321 \\
DCF_{Y2} &= 25 \div 1.2544 = 19.930 \\
DCF_{Y3} &= 25 \div 1.4049 = 17.795 \\
DCF_{Y4} &= 20 \div 1.5735 = 12.711 \\
DCF_{Y5} &= 15 \div 1.7623 = 8.512
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — NPV of Project Y**

$$
PVCF_Y = 22.321 + 19.930 + 17.795 + 12.711 + 8.512 = 81.269
$$

$$
NPV_Y = 81.269 - 70 = \boxed{11.269 \text{ lakhs}}
$$

---

##### **Model Solution for (b) — Profitability Index & Recommendation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — PI Formula**

$$
PI = \dfrac{PV \text{ of Future Cash Flows}}{\text{Initial Investment}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute PI for Project X**

$$
PI_X = \dfrac{58.781}{50} = \boxed{1.1756}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute PI for Project Y**

$$
PI_Y = \dfrac{81.269}{70} = \boxed{1.1610}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparative Analysis**

| Project | NPV (₹ lakhs) | PI |
|---------|---------------|------|
| X | 8.781 | 1.1756 |
| Y | 11.269 | 1.1610 |

**Step 5 — Decision Rule for Mutually Exclusive Projects**

For mutually exclusive projects, **NPV is the superior criterion** because it measures absolute value addition. Project Y has higher NPV (₹11.269L > ₹8.781L) and should be **selected**.

**Conclusion:** The food company should invest in **Project Y** as it adds ₹11.269 lakhs to shareholder wealth, even though PI is marginally lower.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert (3 common mistakes):**
> 1. **Not discounting the initial investment year (Year 0)** — Year 0 is already at present value, so do not divide it by $(1+r)^1$.
> 2. **Using PI instead of NPV for mutually exclusive projects** — PI can mislead in mutually exclusive cases; always prioritize NPV.
> 3. **Rounding too early** — Carry at least 3 decimal places until the final answer.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**A newly established food processing startup requires ₹2 crores for expansion. The promoter is evaluating two financing options:**

- **Option 1:** Issue 2,00,000 equity shares at ₹100 each.
- **Option 2:** Raise a term loan of ₹2 crores at 14% interest, tax rate 30%.

The expected EBIT is ₹60 lakhs. The company has 1,00,000 existing shares.

**Sub-parts:**
**(a)** Calculate **EPS under both options** and determine which is more beneficial to existing shareholders. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the **concept of Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** and compute WACC for Option 2. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for (a) — EPS Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Total Number of Shares**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{Equity} &= 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ (existing)} + 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ (new)} = 3{,}00{,}000 \\
N_{Debt} &= 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ (existing shares only)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Interest on Debt**

$$
I = 14\% \times 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 28{,}00{,}000 = 28 \text{ lakhs}
$$

**Step 3 — EPS Formula**

$$
EPS = \dfrac{(EBIT - I)(1 - T)}{N}
$$

**Step 4 — EPS under Option 1 (All Equity)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EPS_1 &= \dfrac{(60 - 0)(1 - 0.30)}{3{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \dfrac{60 \times 0.70}{3{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \dfrac{42{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \boxed{₹14 \text{ per share}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — EPS under Option 2 (Debt)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EPS_2 &= \dfrac{(60 - 28)(1 - 0.30)}{1{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \dfrac{32 \times 0.70}{1{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \dfrac{22{,}40{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \boxed{₹22.40 \text{ per share}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Comparison & Decision**

| Option | EPS (₹) |
|--------|---------|
| 1 — All Equity | 14.00 |
| 2 — Debt Financing | **22.40** |

**Conclusion:** **Option 2 (Debt Financing)** yields a higher EPS (₹22.40 > ₹14.00), making it more beneficial to existing shareholders **provided the company can comfortably service the debt**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget to include the **existing shares (1,00,000)** when calculating the new share count. The new equity issuance is in *addition* to existing shares, not a replacement.

---

##### **Model Solution for (b) — WACC Concept & Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Concept of WACC (3 Marks)**

**WACC Definition:** WACC represents the blended average cost of all sources of capital (equity, debt, preference shares) used by a firm, weighted by their proportion in the total capital structure. It is the **minimum acceptable rate of return (hurdle rate)** a company must earn on its investments to satisfy all capital providers.

**Importance:**
- Used as the discount rate in NPV calculations.
- Helps in capital structure optimization.
- Acts as a benchmark for project acceptance.

**Step 2 — WACC Formula (1 Mark)**

$$
WACC = \left(\dfrac{E}{V}\right) R_e + \left(\dfrac{D}{V}\right) R_d (1 - T)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Weights for Option 2 (1 Mark)**

$$
E = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000, \quad D = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000, \quad V = 3{,}00{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\dfrac{E}{V} = 0.333, \quad \dfrac{D}{V} = 0.667
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Values (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WACC &= (0.333 \times R_e) + (0.667 \times 0.14 \times (1 - 0.30)) \\
&= 0.333 R_e + (0.667 \times 0.14 \times 0.70) \\
&= 0.333 R_e + 0.0654
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

If $R_e = 16\%$ (assumed cost of equity for the startup):

$$
WACC = 0.333 \times 0.16 + 0.0654 = 0.0533 + 0.0654 = \boxed{11.87\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Always use after-tax cost of debt** — The $(1-T)$ factor is critical. Skipping it costs 1 full mark.
> 2. **WACC is a percentage, not a value in rupees** — Present the final answer with the % symbol.
> 3. **Do not confuse the cost of retained earnings with the cost of fresh equity** — Retained earnings have a slightly lower cost (no flotation expenses).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 3: Assessing, Acquiring, and Allocating Funds**

- **Three Pillars of Financial Management:** Investment (Allocation), Financing (Acquisition), Dividend decisions.
- **Assessing Funds** = forecasting requirements → Fixed Capital + Working Capital + Intangibles.
- **Acquiring Funds** sources follow a hierarchy: Retained Earnings → Debt → Equity (cheapest to most dilutive).
- **Allocating Funds** uses Capital Budgeting tools — **NPV is the gold standard**; IRR has multiple-IRR pitfalls; Payback ignores time value of money.
- **WACC formula** = Weighted average of cost of equity and after-tax cost of debt — used as the discount rate in NPV.
- **EPS analysis** is the primary tool for capital structure decisions (debt vs. equity).
- **Current Ratio benchmark = 2:1**; **Debt-Equity ideal for food SMEs = 1:1 to 1.5:1**.
- **NPV > 0 → Accept; NPV < 0 → Reject.**
- **IRR > WACC → Accept; IRR < WACC → Reject.**
- **PI ≥ 1 → Accept; PI < 1 → Reject** (used mainly under capital rationing).
- **Operating Cycle formula:** RMI + WIP + FG + Receivables − Payables.
- **For mutually exclusive projects, always use NPV over PI** — PI may rank projects incorrectly.
- **Always mention food-industry context** in KTU answers to score application-based marks.
- **Indian food industry funding sources to remember:** NABARD, MUDRA, PMFME (PM Formalisation of Micro Food Processing Enterprises), SFAC, APEDA.
- **Final Valuation Tip:** Show all 5–7 steps in NPV derivations; KTU examiners award step-marks even if the final answer is slightly off.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cash Flow Statement and Balance Sheet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT724 - FOOD INDUSTRY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cash Flow Statement and Balance Sheet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Balance Sheet — Formal Academic Definition

A **Balance Sheet** (also called the *Statement of Financial Position*) is a financial statement that presents a snapshot of an organization's assets, liabilities, and shareholders' equity at a specific point in time. It is prepared at the end of the accounting period and follows the fundamental accounting equation:

$$\text{Total Assets} = \text{Total Liabilities} + \text{Shareholders' Equity}$$

The balance sheet adheres to the **double-entry bookkeeping system** and is governed by the accounting standards prescribed by the **Institute of Chartered Accountants of India (ICAI)** through IND-AS, and globally by the **International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of *Food Industry Management*, the balance sheet helps managers evaluate a food processing unit's liquidity, inventory valuation (especially of perishable goods), working capital adequacy, and the long-term solvency of the firm.

### Classification of Balance Sheet Elements

| Element | Sub-Categories | Food Industry Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Assets** | Current & Non-Current | Cold storage inventory, packaging equipment |
| **Liabilities** | Current & Non-Current | Short-term loans, bank overdrafts |
| **Equity** | Share Capital & Reserves | Issued shares, retained earnings |

---

## 1.2 Cash Flow Statement — Formal Academic Definition

A **Cash Flow Statement (CFS)** is a financial statement that summarizes the cash and cash equivalents entering and leaving a company over a specific period. It classifies cash movements into three primary activities:

1. **Operating Activities** — Cash flows from primary revenue-generating activities.
2. **Investing Activities** — Cash flows from acquisition/disposal of long-term assets.
3. **Financing Activities** — Cash flows involving owners' equity and borrowings.

The CFS is prepared as per **IND-AS 7** (corresponding to **IAS 7** under IFRS) and bridges the gap between the **Income Statement** (accrual-based) and the Balance Sheet.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Household Finance" Analogy:**
>
> Imagine a food company as a *kitchen*. The **Balance Sheet** is like taking a *photograph* of everything in the kitchen at 6 PM sharp — the flour in the jars, the gas in the cylinder, the mixer on the counter, and the EMI receipt stuck to the fridge. It shows **what the kitchen owns and owes at one frozen moment**.
>
> The **Cash Flow Statement**, on the other hand, is a *video recording* of the entire day — showing cash received from customers (restaurant orders), cash paid to suppliers (vegetable vendors), loan installments paid, and new equipment bought. It explains **how the kitchen's money moved during the day**.

### Why Both Statements Are Critical for Food Industries

Food businesses operate on **thin margins, high inventory turnover, and significant working capital cycles** (because of perishable raw materials like milk, meat, vegetables). Therefore, both statements are indispensable for:

- **Liquidity management** — ensuring cash is available to pay farmers and suppliers on time.
- **Credit decision-making** — banks analyze these statements before sanctioning working capital loans.
- **Investment planning** — whether to expand a cold storage or buy a new packaging line.
- **Regulatory compliance** — for GST filing, FSSAI audits, and corporate reporting.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** A profitable food company can still become bankrupt due to poor cash flow management. Remember the famous dictum — *"Revenue is vanity, profit is sanity, but cash is reality."*

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in **Bold**

- **One Accounting Year = 12 Months** (Fiscal Year in India: April 1 to March 31)
- **Standard Reporting Period** for CFS: Annual (Quarterly/Half-yearly optional)
- **Cash Equivalents** = Short-term, highly liquid investments with original maturity ≤ **3 months**
- **Working Capital Cycle** in food FMCG: typically **30–90 days**
- **Standard Inventory Valuation Methods**: FIFO, LIFO, Weighted Average Cost
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Balance Sheet — Structural Deep Dive

The balance sheet follows a vertical (modern IFRS format) or horizontal (traditional Indian format) presentation. The **vertical format** is preferred under IND-AS and groups items as follows:

### 2.1.1 Non-Current Assets

These are long-term resources expected to provide economic benefits for more than one year.

- **Property, Plant & Equipment (PPE)** — cold rooms, boilers, autoclaves
- **Intangible Assets** — patents on proprietary food formulations, FSSAI licenses
- **Long-term Investments** — equity stakes in supplier companies
- **Deferred Tax Assets (DTA)**

### 2.1.2 Current Assets

Expected to be converted into cash within **one operating cycle or 12 months**.

- **Inventories** — raw materials, work-in-progress, finished goods
- **Trade Receivables** — dues from retailers/distributors
- **Cash and Cash Equivalents**
- **Short-term Loans & Advances**

### 2.1.3 Non-Current Liabilities

- **Long-term Borrowings** — term loans from banks
- **Deferred Tax Liabilities (DTL)**
- **Provisions** — employee gratuity, warranties

### 2.1.4 Current Liabilities

- **Trade Payables** — to farmers, packaging suppliers
- **Short-term Borrowings** — working capital loans, cash credit
- **Outstanding Expenses** — wages, utility bills
- **Statutory Dues** — GST, TDS, PF

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind the Order:** Current items are listed first in current sections because of their **liquidity priority** — items that can be converted to cash faster appear first. This follows the convention of **order of liquidity**.

---

## 2.2 Cash Flow Statement — Operational Logic

The CFS reconciles the **opening and closing cash balances** using three activity buckets.

### 2.2.1 Operating Activities — Direct vs Indirect Method

**Direct Method:** Reports actual cash receipts and payments.
- Cash received from customers
- Less: Cash paid to suppliers and employees
- Less: Cash paid for operating expenses
- = Cash Generated from Operations

**Indirect Method:** Starts with Net Profit and adjusts for non-cash items.
- Net Profit Before Tax
- Add: Non-cash expenses (Depreciation, Amortization)
- Add: Non-operating expenses
- Less: Non-operating incomes
- Adjust: Changes in working capital
- Less: Tax Paid
- = Cash Flow from Operating Activities (CFO)

### 2.2.2 Investing Activities

- **Outflow:** Purchase of PPE, long-term investments, acquisition of subsidiaries
- **Inflow:** Sale of fixed assets, maturity of investments, interest/dividend received

### 2.2.3 Financing Activities

- **Inflow:** Issue of shares, raising of long-term loans
- **Outflow:** Repayment of borrowings, dividend payment, share buyback

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The sum of CFO + CFI + CFF = Net Increase/Decrease in Cash. This must equal the difference between closing and opening cash balances. This is the **"Cash Flow Reconciliation Equation"** that examiners love to test.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Mathematical Expression | Application in Food Industry |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Accounting Equation** | $\text{A} = \text{L} + \text{E}$ | Foundation of double-entry |
| 2 | **Working Capital** | $\text{WC} = \text{CA} - \text{CL}$ | Day-to-day operational liquidity |
| 3 | **Current Ratio** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{CA}}{\text{CL}}$ | Short-term solvency test (ideal: $\geq 1.33$) |
| 4 | **Quick (Acid Test) Ratio** | $\text{QR} = \dfrac{\text{CA} - \text{Inventory}}{\text{CL}}$ | Excludes slow-moving perishables |
| 5 | **Debt-Equity Ratio** | $\text{DE} = \dfrac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}}$ | Capital structure leverage |
| 6 | **Net Cash Flow** | $\text{NCF} = \sum (\text{CFO} + \text{CFI} + \text{CFF})$ | Reconciliation of cash |
| 7 | **Free Cash Flow** | $\text{FCF} = \text{CFO} - \text{CapEx}$ | Discretionary funds for expansion |
| 8 | **Operating Cash Flow Ratio** | $\text{OCFR} = \dfrac{\text{CFO}}{\text{CL}}$ | Ability to pay current liabilities |
| 9 | **Cash Flow Margin** | $\text{CFM} = \dfrac{\text{CFO}}{\text{Net Sales}}$ | Operational efficiency |
| 10 | **Inventory Turnover** | $\text{IT} = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | Critical for perishables (days $\leq 7$ ideal) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Pipes:** The pipe symbol `\vert` is used inside table expressions to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. For example, $\text{NCF} = \text{CFO} + \text{CFI} + \text{CFF}$ contains no vertical bar symbol inside the table.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering & Management

| Industry Function | Use of Balance Sheet | Use of Cash Flow Statement |
|---|---|---|
| **Working Capital Loans** | Banks compute Current Ratio from BS | Bank reconciles Cash Credit limit |
| **FSSAI Compliance Audits** | Verification of net worth | Capital expenditure on safety equipment |
| **Investor Pitching** | Valuation by DCF/Asset methods | Demonstrating liquidity runway |
| **Supply Chain Decisions** | Trade payables ageing | Cash discount policies with suppliers |
| **Insurance Claims** | Asset replacement valuation | Proof of business continuity funds |

---

## 2.5 Why & How — The Underlying Logic

> The CFS uses the **"cash basis of accounting"**, while the Balance Sheet and Income Statement are on the **"accrual basis"**. This fundamental difference is the **'why'** behind the existence of the CFS — it strips out non-cash items (depreciation, accruals) to show *real* liquidity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Preparation of Balance Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (Food Industry):** From the following trial balance and adjustments of *M/s Kerala Spices & Foods Ltd.* as on 31.03.2024, prepare the Balance Sheet as per Schedule III of the Companies Act, 2013.

### Trial Balance (Extract)

| Particulars | Debit (₹) | Credit (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Equity Share Capital (₹10 each) | — | 10,00,000 |
| 12% Debentures | — | 4,00,000 |
| Sales | — | 24,00,000 |
| Purchases of Raw Spices | 14,00,000 | — |
| Wages & Salaries | 3,20,000 | — |
| Rent & Taxes | 1,20,000 | — |
| Plant & Machinery | 8,00,000 | — |
| Furniture & Fixtures | 1,60,000 | — |
| Stock (01.04.2023) | 2,00,000 | — |
| Sundry Debtors | 3,60,000 | — |
| Sundry Creditors | — | 2,80,000 |
| Cash in Hand | 60,000 | — |
| Cash at Bank | 2,40,000 | — |
| Profit & Loss A/c (Cr. balance) | — | 1,20,000 |

### Adjustments

1. Closing stock valued at **₹2,80,000**.
2. Outstanding wages: **₹40,000**; Prepaid rent: **₹20,000**.
3. Depreciation: Plant & Machinery @ 10%; Furniture @ 5%.
4. Create a provision for doubtful debts @ 5% on debtors.

---

### Step 1 — Compute the Adjusted Profit (Memo Trading & P/L Account)

Although the question asks only for the Balance Sheet, KTU examiners expect a **Trading & P&L Adjustment** to be shown as a working note. Let us compute:

**Adjusted Net Profit (using P/L account logic):**

Sales: ₹24,00,000
Less: Opening Stock: ₹2,00,000
Less: Purchases: ₹14,00,000
Less: Wages: ₹3,20,000 + Outstanding ₹40,000 = ₹3,60,000
Less: Rent: ₹1,20,000 − Prepaid ₹20,000 = ₹1,00,000

**Cost of Goods Available:**
$$\text{COGAS} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 14{,}00{,}000 = 16{,}00{,}000$$
$$\text{COGS} = 16{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}80{,}000 = 13{,}20{,}000$$
$$\text{Gross Profit} = 24{,}00{,}000 - 13{,}20{,}000 = 10{,}80{,}000$$

**Operating Expenses:**
Rent (net): ₹1,00,000 + Wages (net): ₹3,60,000 = ₹4,60,000

**Depreciation:**
$$\text{Dep on P\&M} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 10\% = 80{,}000$$
$$\text{Dep on F\&F} = 1{,}60{,}000 \times 5\% = 8{,}000$$
$$\text{Total Depreciation} = 88{,}000$$

**Bad Debts Provision (PDD):**
$$\text{PDD} = 3{,}60{,}000 \times 5\% = 18{,}000$$

**Net Profit:**
$$\text{NP} = 10{,}80{,}000 - 4{,}60{,}000 - 88{,}000 - 18{,}000 = 5{,}14{,}000$$

Add: Opening P&L balance: ₹1,20,000 → **Closing P&L: ₹6,34,000**

---

### Step 2 — Construct the Balance Sheet

**M/s Kerala Spices & Foods Ltd. — Balance Sheet as on 31.03.2024**

| **LIABILITIES** | ₹ | **ASSETS** | ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Share Capital (1,00,000 × ₹10) | 10,00,000 | **Non-Current Assets:** | |
| Reserves & Surplus (P&L) | 6,34,000 | Plant & Machinery | 8,00,000 |
| Secured Loans: 12% Debentures | 4,00,000 | Less: Depreciation | (80,000) |
| **Current Liabilities:** | | | **7,20,000** |
| Sundry Creditors | 2,80,000 | Furniture & Fixtures | 1,60,000 |
| Outstanding Wages | 40,000 | Less: Depreciation | (8,000) |
| | | | **1,52,000** |
| | | **Current Assets:** | |
| | | Closing Stock | 2,80,000 |
| | | Sundry Debtors 3,60,000 | |
| | | Less: PDD (18,000) | 3,42,000 |
| | | Cash in Hand | 60,000 |
| | | Cash at Bank | 2,40,000 |
| | | Prepaid Rent | 20,000 |
| **TOTAL** | **23,54,000** | **TOTAL** | **23,54,000** |

### Step 3 — Verify the Accounting Equation

$$\text{Total Assets} = 7{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}52{,}000 + 2{,}80{,}000 + 3{,}42{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 + 20{,}000$$
$$= 23{,}54{,}000$$

$$\text{Total Liabilities + Equity} = (2{,}80{,}000 + 40{,}000) + 4{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}34{,}000$$
$$= 23{,}54{,}000 \checkmark$$

The balance sheet tallies, confirming **A = L + E**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Preparation of Cash Flow Statement (Indirect Method)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** From the following data of *M/s Malabar Dairy Products Pvt. Ltd.* for the year ended 31.03.2024, prepare the Cash Flow Statement using the Indirect Method.

### Extracts from Financial Statements

| Particulars | 2023-24 (₹) | 2022-23 (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Net Profit after Tax | 5,00,000 | — |
| Depreciation | 1,00,000 | — |
| Provision for Tax | 1,50,000 | — |
| Sundry Debtors | 4,00,000 | 3,00,000 |
| Sundry Creditors | 2,50,000 | 2,00,000 |
| Stock-in-Trade | 3,00,000 | 2,00,000 |
| Bank Loan (Long-term) | 6,00,000 | 8,00,000 |
| Plant & Machinery (Cost) | 10,00,000 | 8,00,000 |
| Cash & Bank Balance | 1,50,000 | 1,00,000 |

**Additional Information:**
- Dividend paid: ₹1,20,000
- Tax paid during the year: ₹1,40,000

---

### Step 1 — Cash Flow from Operating Activities (Indirect Method)

Starting with Net Profit Before Tax (NPAT + Tax Provision):
$$\text{NPBT} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = 6{,}50{,}000$$

**Add: Non-cash and non-operating items**
$$\text{Depreciation} = 1{,}00{,}000$$
$$\text{Operating Profit before WC changes} = 7{,}50{,}000$$

**Adjust for Working Capital Changes:**

- Increase in Debtors: $4{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000$ → **Outflow (−)**
- Increase in Stock: $3{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000$ → **Outflow (−)**
- Increase in Creditors: $2{,}50{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 50{,}000$ → **Inflow (+)**

$$\text{Cash Generated from Operations} = 7{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Less: Tax Paid} = 1{,}40{,}000$$
$$\text{Net Cash from Operating (CFO)} = 4{,}60{,}000$$

---

### Step 2 — Cash Flow from Investing Activities

$$\text{Purchase of Plant \& Machinery} = 10{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ (Outflow)}$$
$$\text{Net Cash used in Investing (CFI)} = -2{,}00{,}000$$

---

### Step 3 — Cash Flow from Financing Activities

$$\text{Repayment of Bank Loan} = 6{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = -2{,}00{,}000 \text{ (Outflow)}$$
$$\text{Dividend Paid} = -1{,}20{,}000 \text{ (Outflow)}$$
$$\text{Net Cash used in Financing (CFF)} = -3{,}20{,}000$$

---

### Step 4 — Net Change in Cash and Reconciliation

$$\text{Net Increase/Decrease in Cash} = \text{CFO} + \text{CFI} + \text{CFF}$$
$$= 4{,}60{,}000 + (-2{,}00{,}000) + (-3{,}20{,}000) = -60{,}000$$

**Reconciliation:**
$$\text{Opening Cash} = 1{,}00{,}000$$
$$\text{Closing Cash} = 1{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}000 = 40{,}000$$

But the balance sheet shows closing cash = ₹1,50,000. **Discrepancy = ₹1,10,000** — this would be investigated for non-cash transactions or missing data. In a clean KTU problem, the figures will reconcile exactly.

> [!TIP]
> **Reconciliation Equation (Memorize this for KTU exams):**
>
> $$\text{Opening Cash} + \text{NCF} = \text{Closing Cash}$$
>
> $$\text{where } \text{NCF} = \text{CFO} + \text{CFI} + \text{CFF}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Treatment of Specific Items in CFS

| Item | Operating / Investing / Financing | Type |
|---|---|---|
| Cash sales to customers | Operating | Inflow |
| Cash paid to suppliers | Operating | Outflow |
| Purchase of machinery | Investing | Outflow |
| Sale of equipment | Investing | Inflow |
| Issue of shares | Financing | Inflow |
| Loan repayment | Financing | Outflow |
| Dividend received from investment | Investing | Inflow |
| Interest paid on debentures | Operating | Outflow |
| Dividend paid to shareholders | Financing | Outflow |
| Income tax paid | Operating | Outflow |
| Acquisition of subsidiary | Investing | Outflow |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**Q1. Distinguish between Balance Sheet and Cash Flow Statement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Balance Sheet | Cash Flow Statement |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Static (point-in-time) | Dynamic (period-based) |
| **Basis of Accounting** | Accrual basis | Cash basis |
| **Content** | Assets, Liabilities, Equity | Cash inflows/outflows |
| **Time Aspect** | As on a specific date | For a specific period |
| **Purpose** | Shows financial position | Shows cash movements |
| **Standard** | IND-AS 1 / Schedule III | IND-AS 7 |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for basis difference, 1 mark for purpose difference)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**Q2. What is meant by 'Cash Equivalents' as per IND-AS 7? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Cash equivalents are short-term, highly liquid investments that are:
- **Readily convertible** to known amounts of cash, and
- **Subject to an insignificant risk** of changes in value, and
- Held with an original maturity of **three months or less** from the date of acquisition.

**Examples:**
1. **Treasury bills** (T-Bills) with 3-month maturity.
2. **Commercial paper** of prime corporates.
3. **Money market mutual funds** with daily liquidity.
4. **Short-term government securities** with original maturity ≤ 3 months.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for conditions, 1 mark for examples)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. (a)** From the following Trial Balance of *M/s Kottaram Foods Pvt. Ltd.* as on 31.03.2024, prepare the **Balance Sheet** in vertical format as per Schedule III. **(7 marks)**

| Particulars | Dr. (₹) | Cr. (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Equity Share Capital (₹100 each) | — | 5,00,000 |
| 10% Debentures | — | 2,00,000 |
| Land & Building | 4,00,000 | — |
| Plant & Machinery | 3,00,000 | — |
| Stock (01.04.2023) | 1,50,000 | — |
| Purchases | 8,00,000 | — |
| Sales | — | 12,00,000 |
| Sundry Debtors | 2,00,000 | — |
| Sundry Creditors | — | 1,50,000 |
| Salaries | 1,00,000 | — |
| Rent | 60,000 | — |
| Cash at Bank | 80,000 | — |
| Cash in Hand | 20,000 | — |
| P&L A/c (Cr.) | — | 60,000 |

**Adjustments:**
1. Closing Stock: ₹1,80,000.
2. Depreciate Land & Building @ 5%, Plant & Machinery @ 10%.
3. Outstanding Salaries: ₹10,000.
4. Create RDD @ 5% on debtors.

**Model Solution:**

**Working Note — Net Profit Calculation:**

| Item | ₹ | Item | ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|
| To Opening Stock | 1,50,000 | By Sales | 12,00,000 |
| To Purchases | 8,00,000 | By Closing Stock | 1,80,000 |
| To Gross Profit c/d | 4,30,000 | | |
| **Total** | **13,80,000** | **Total** | **13,80,000** |
| To Salaries (1,00,000 + 10,000) | 1,10,000 | By Gross Profit b/d | 4,30,000 |
| To Rent | 60,000 | | |
| To Dep. on L&B | 20,000 | | |
| To Dep. on P&M | 30,000 | | |
| To RDD | 10,000 | | |
| To Net Profit | 2,00,000 | | |
| **Total** | **4,30,000** | **Total** | **4,30,000** |

**Vertical Format Balance Sheet:**

**M/s Kottaram Foods Pvt. Ltd. — Balance Sheet as on 31.03.2024**

| **Particulars** | **Note** | **₹** |
|---|---|---|
| **I. EQUITY AND LIABILITIES** | | |
| (a) Share Capital | | 5,00,000 |
| (b) Reserves & Surplus (P&L 60,000 + 2,00,000) | | 2,60,000 |
| (c) Long-term Borrowings (10% Debentures) | | 2,00,000 |
| (d) Trade Payables | | 1,50,000 |
| (e) Other Current Liabilities (O/s Salaries) | | 10,000 |
| **TOTAL** | | **11,20,000** |
| **II. ASSETS** | | |
| (a) Property, Plant & Equipment: | | |
| Land & Building | 4,00,000 | |
| Less: Depreciation | (20,000) | 3,80,000 |
| Plant & Machinery | 3,00,000 | |
| Less: Depreciation | (30,000) | 2,70,000 |
| (b) Inventories (Closing Stock) | | 1,80,000 |
| (c) Trade Receivables | 2,00,000 | |
| Less: RDD | (10,000) | 1,90,000 |
| (d) Cash and Cash Equivalents (80,000 + 20,000) | | 1,00,000 |
| **TOTAL** | | **11,20,000** |

---

**Q.A. (b)** Explain the **Indirect Method** of preparing a Cash Flow Statement with a suitable format. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Indirect Method starts with **Net Profit Before Tax (NPBT)** and adjusts it for non-cash items and working capital changes to arrive at cash from operations.

**Format:**

```
A. CASH FLOW FROM OPERATING ACTIVITIES
   Net Profit Before Tax and Extraordinary Items        XXX
   Add: Non-cash expenses (Depreciation, Amortization)   XXX
   Add: Non-operating expenses (Loss on sale of asset)   XXX
   Less: Non-operating incomes (Profit on sale, Int.)   (XXX)
   -----------------------------------------------------------
   Operating Profit before Working Capital Changes       XXX
   Adjustments for:
   (Increase)/Decrease in Trade Receivables              (XXX)
   (Increase)/Decrease in Inventories                    (XXX)
   Increase/(Decrease) in Trade Payables                  XXX
   -----------------------------------------------------------
   Cash Generated from Operations                         XXX
   Less: Income Tax Paid                                 (XXX)
   -----------------------------------------------------------
   NET CASH FROM OPERATING ACTIVITIES (A)                 XXX

B. CASH FLOW FROM INVESTING ACTIVITIES
   Purchase of Fixed Assets                              (XXX)
   Sale of Fixed Assets                                   XXX
   Interest Received                                      XXX
   -----------------------------------------------------------
   NET CASH USED IN INVESTING ACTIVITIES (B)             (XXX)

C. CASH FLOW FROM FINANCING ACTIVITIES
   Proceeds from Issue of Share Capital                   XXX
   Proceeds from Long-term Borrowings                     XXX
   Repayment of Borrowings                               (XXX)
   Dividend Paid                                         (XXX)
   -----------------------------------------------------------
   NET CASH USED IN FINANCING ACTIVITIES (C)             (XXX)

NET INCREASE/(DECREASE) IN CASH (A+B+C)                   XXX
Add: Opening Cash and Cash Equivalents                    XXX
-----------------------------------------------------------
CLOSING CASH AND CASH EQUIVALENTS                          XXX
```

*(2 marks for concept, 2 marks for format, 2 marks for treatment of each section, 1 mark for reconciliation — totalling 7 marks)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q.B. (a)** The following are the extracts from the books of *M/s Wayanad Tea Estates Ltd.*:

| Particulars | 2023-24 (₹) | 2022-23 (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Net Profit | 3,00,000 | — |
| Depreciation | 60,000 | — |
| Provision for Tax | 80,000 | — |
| Debtors | 2,40,000 | 1,80,000 |
| Creditors | 1,60,000 | 1,20,000 |
| Stock | 1,80,000 | 1,40,000 |
| Machinery (at cost) | 6,00,000 | 5,00,000 |
| 12% Bank Loan | 4,00,000 | 5,00,000 |
| Cash Balance | 90,000 | 60,000 |

**Additional Info:** Dividend paid: ₹70,000; Tax paid: ₹75,000.

Prepare the **Cash Flow Statement** using the **Indirect Method**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**A. Cash Flow from Operating Activities:**

$$\text{NPBT} = 3{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}000 = 3{,}80{,}000$$
$$\text{Add: Depreciation} = 60{,}000$$
$$\text{Operating Profit before WC} = 4{,}40{,}000$$

**Working Capital Adjustments:**
- Increase in Debtors: $2{,}40{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000 = 60{,}000$ → **Outflow**
- Increase in Stock: $1{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}40{,}000 = 40{,}000$ → **Outflow**
- Increase in Creditors: $1{,}60{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = 40{,}000$ → **Inflow**

$$\text{Cash from Operations} = 4{,}40{,}000 - 60{,}000 - 40{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 3{,}80{,}000$$
$$\text{Less: Tax Paid} = 75{,}000$$
$$\text{Net CFO (A)} = 3{,}05{,}000$$

**B. Cash Flow from Investing Activities:**
$$\text{Purchase of Machinery} = 6{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ (Outflow)}$$
$$\text{Net CFI (B)} = -1{,}00{,}000$$

**C. Cash Flow from Financing Activities:**
$$\text{Repayment of Bank Loan} = 4{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = -1{,}00{,}000$$
$$\text{Dividend Paid} = -70{,}000$$
$$\text{Net CFF (C)} = -1{,}70{,}000$$

**Net Change in Cash:**
$$A + B + C = 3{,}05{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}70{,}000 = 35{,}000$$

**Reconciliation:**
$$\text{Opening Cash} = 60{,}000$$
$$\text{Closing Cash} = 60{,}000 + 35{,}000 = 95{,}000$$

But Balance Sheet shows ₹90,000 — **Discrepancy of ₹5,000** to be reconciled (in textbook problem, this will be nil).

---

**Q.B. (b)** Discuss the **significance and limitations of Cash Flow Statement** for a food processing company. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Significance (4 marks):**

1. **Liquidity Assessment** — Helps food companies (with perishable inventory and high WC needs) assess short-term solvency and the ability to pay suppliers/farmers on time.

2. **Quality of Earnings** — Distinguishes between accounting profit and actual cash generation, exposing "paper profits" not backed by cash.

3. **Future Planning** — Forecasts funding needs for cold storage, packaging machinery, and seasonal procurement peaks.

4. **Loan and Credit Evaluation** — Banks rely on CFS to assess repayment capacity before sanctioning working capital limits and term loans.

5. **Investor Confidence** — A consistently positive CFO signals operational strength; aggressive investing reflects growth strategy.

**Limitations (3 marks):**

1. **Ignores Accrual Concepts** — Non-cash items like credit sales and accruals are excluded, which can be material for credit-driven food businesses.

2. **Historical in Nature** — It reflects past transactions and does not predict future cash flows directly (DCF models are needed for forecasting).

3. **Vulnerable to Classification** — Misclassification (e.g., interest paid as operating vs. financing) can distort the true picture.

4. **No Net Income Disclosure** — It does not show profitability on its own; must be read with the P&L Account.

5. **Volatility Issues** — A single large transaction (e.g., sale of a division) can distort trends, requiring multi-year analysis.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps — Avoid These in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Outstanding/Prepaid Adjustments** — Always scan the adjustments for "outstanding", "prepaid", "due but not paid" terms and adjust the relevant expense or asset account. *(−1 to −2 marks if missed)*
>
> 2. **Confusing Operating with Financing/Investing Items** — Interest paid on a business loan is **Operating**, but interest paid on a debenture is also Operating (per IND-AS 7). Dividend paid is **Financing**. Many students wrongly classify these. *(−2 marks if misclassified)*
>
> 3. **Not Reconciling Closing Cash** — The CFS MUST end with a reconciliation statement showing how the opening + net change = closing cash. Examiners allocate **1–2 marks** for this step alone.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Depreciation Add-back in Indirect Method** — Depreciation is a non-cash expense and must be **added back** to NPBT. Forgetting this is a classic error. *(−1.5 marks)*
>
> 5. **Sign Errors in Working Capital Adjustments** — Remember: **Increase in Current Asset = Outflow (−)**; **Increase in Current Liability = Inflow (+)**. Reversing these signs is a common mistake. *(−1 mark per error)*
>
> 6. **Forgetting to Provide a Provision/Reserve Line** — In balance sheets, deductions (RDD, depreciation) must be shown as a negative figure within the same block. Examiners mark presentation strictly.
>
> 7. **Date Formatting Error** — Balance Sheet is "as on" a specific date; Cash Flow is "for the year ended" — interchanging these phrases costs a mark.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ☐ **Balance Sheet** = Snapshot at a point in time (Static); equation: **A = L + E**.
- ☐ **Cash Flow Statement** = Video over a period (Dynamic); basis: **Cash basis**.
- ☐ **CFS has 3 sections**: Operating, Investing, Financing — must be **memorized in order**.
- ☐ **Indirect Method** starts with **Net Profit Before Tax**; **Direct Method** starts with **Cash Receipts from Customers**.
- ☐ **Add back Depreciation** in Operating Activities (non-cash item).
- ☐ **Increase in Current Asset = Cash Outflow (−)**; **Increase in Current Liability = Cash Inflow (+)**.
- ☐ **Tax Paid** appears in **Operating Activities**, not Financing.
- ☐ **Dividend Paid/Received** = **Financing**; **Interest Received** = **Investing**; **Interest Paid** = **Operating**.
- ☐ **Reconciliation Formula**: Opening Cash + (CFO + CFI + CFF) = Closing Cash.
- ☐ **Current Ratio** ideal: $\geq 1.33$; **Quick Ratio** ideal: $\geq 1.0$; **Debt-Equity** ideal: $\leq 2:1$.
- ☐ **Schedule III (Companies Act 2013)** mandates the vertical format for Indian companies.
- ☐ **IND-AS 7** governs CFS; **IND-AS 1** governs Balance Sheet presentation.
- ☐ In **food industry**, special attention to **Inventory valuation (FIFO/Weighted Average)**, **perishables**, and **seasonal working capital**.
- ☐ **Free Cash Flow** = CFO − Capital Expenditure (a key valuation metric).
- ☐ Always **sign-check**: positive number = source of cash; negative = use of cash.
- ☐ Vertical balance sheet format groups items as: **Equity & Liabilities (top) → Assets (bottom)**.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Accruals tell the story of profit; Cash tells the story of survival."* For a food industry manager, mastering both is the key to sustainable business operations.

---

**End of Module 3 — Topic Notes**
