topics.title: Marketing Environment and Market Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT724 - FOOD INDUSTRY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Marketing Environment and Market Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Marketing Environment — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The **Marketing Environment** refers to the internal and external forces, actors, and conditions that surround and influence an organization's ability to build and maintain successful relationships with its target customers. It comprises both the **Microenvironment** (close actors — suppliers, customers, competitors, intermediaries, publics) and the **Macroenvironment** (broader societal forces — Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, Legal — often abbreviated as **PESTEL**).

In the food industry specifically, the marketing environment is uniquely volatile because:
- Products are **perishable** (short shelf life).
- Consumers are increasingly **health-conscious**.
- Regulatory frameworks (FSSAI, Codex Alimentarius) are **stringent**.
- Raw material supply is **seasonal and climate-dependent**.

## 1.2 Market Segmentation — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Market Segmentation** is the strategic process of dividing a broad consumer or business market — normally consisting of existing and potential customers — into sub-groups of consumers (known as **segments**) based on some shared characteristics, such as demographic traits, psychographic profiles, geographic location, or behavioral patterns. The goal is to design and deliver offerings that precisely match the unique needs of each segment.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Restaurant Menu"**
>
> Imagine you are the owner of a restaurant in Kerala. You do **not** cook the same single dish and serve it to *everyone* on Earth. Instead:
>
> 1. You **observe** the local environment (Malabar spice lovers vs. Travancore coconut-preferring diners = *PESTEL*).
> 2. You **understand your immediate actors** (fish suppliers in the morning market, nearby competitor hotels, food delivery apps as intermediaries = *Microenvironment*).
> 3. You then **divide** your customers — young college students wanting cheap biryani, families wanting wholesome meals, health-conscious office-goers wanting low-oil options, and NRIs craving authentic *payasam* = **Market Segmentation**.
> 4. You finally craft a **menu (targeting & positioning)** that speaks to each segment.
>
> The **Marketing Environment** is the *kitchen's surroundings* (suppliers, weather, rules), and **Market Segmentation** is the *art of cutting the large market cake* into slices that each customer can enjoy.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics to Remember

- **STP Framework** — Segmentation, Targeting, Positioning (the strategic heart of marketing).
- **TAM, SAM, SOM** — Total Addressable Market, Serviceable Available Market, Serviceable Obtainable Market.
- **5 Cs Framework** — Company, Customers, Competitors, Collaborators, Context.
- **Market Share %** = $\frac{\text{Company Sales}}{\text{Total Industry Sales}} \times 100$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microenvironment (Internal + Immediate External Actors)

The microenvironment consists of forces **close to the company** that directly affect its ability to serve customers.

| Actor | Role in Food Industry | Strategic Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Suppliers** | Provide raw materials — milk, grains, spices, vegetables | Supply chain disruptions instantly affect product output |
| **Marketing Intermediaries** | Distributors, wholesalers, retailers, logistics firms | Critical for last-mile reach of FMCG food products |
| **Customers** | End consumers — households, HoReCa (Hotels/Restaurants/Catering) | Different buying behaviors across B2B vs. B2C |
| **Competitors** | Direct (e.g., Amul vs. Nandini) and Indirect (e.g., soy milk) | Pricing, packaging, and innovation battles |
| **Publics** | Media, government, local community, financial groups | Reputational asset; CSR strengthens public image |
| **Internal Environment** | Company departments — R&D, HR, Finance, Production | Coordination determines marketing effectiveness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Microenvironment Matters:** These actors are *partially controllable* — the firm can negotiate with suppliers, train employees, or partner with intermediaries.

## 2.2 Macroenvironment — The PESTEL Framework

The macroenvironment consists of **larger societal forces** that are largely **uncontrollable** but must be **monitored and adapted to**.

### 2.2.1 P — Political Factors
- Government stability, trade policies, taxation, subsidies.
- *Food industry example:* PMGKAY (Pradhan Mantri Garib Kalyan Anna Yojana) impacts demand for grains; FSSAI regulations govern labeling.

### 2.2.2 E — Economic Factors
- GDP growth, inflation, disposable income, exchange rates, interest rates.
- *Food industry example:* During inflation, consumers shift from premium dairy to economy brands.

### 2.2.3 S — Social Factors
- Demographics, culture, lifestyle, education, religion.
- *Food industry example:* Kerala's high literacy drives demand for **organic & clean-label** foods.

### 2.2.4 T — Technological Factors
- Automation, cold chain logistics, AI-based demand forecasting, e-commerce.
- *Food industry example:* Cold storage tech extends shelf life of seafood; IoT sensors in dairy supply chain.

### 2.2.5 E — Environmental Factors
- Climate change, sustainability, packaging waste, water scarcity.
- *Food industry example:* Push toward biodegradable packaging; carbon footprint labels.

### 2.2.6 L — Legal Factors
- Consumer protection, food safety laws, advertising standards, labor laws.
- *Food industry example:* FSSAI Act 2006, Legal Metrology Act (packaging weight), Advertising Standards Council of India (ASCI) guidelines.

## 2.3 Market Segmentation — Detailed Bases

Market segmentation is the **first step of the STP process**. It uses four principal bases:

### 2.3.1 Geographic Segmentation
Divides the market based on **location-based variables**.
- **Variables:** Country, region, state, city density (urban/rural), climate.
- *Food industry example:* ITC's *Aashirvaad* Atta offers Kerala-specific *parotta* atta variants.

### 2.3.2 Demographic Segmentation
Divides the market based on **demographic and socio-economic variables**.
- **Variables:** Age, gender, income, education, occupation, family size, religion, generation.
- *Food industry example:* Complan targets children; Protinex targets adults; Horlicks targets both with separate SKUs.

### 2.3.3 Psychographic Segmentation
Divides the market based on **lifestyle, values, personality, and social class**.
- **Variables:** Lifestyle (health-conscious, busy), values (eco-friendly), personality (adventurous eaters), AIO (Activities, Interests, Opinions).
- *Food industry example:* Organic Tattva targets LOHAS (Lifestyles of Health and Sustainability) consumers.

### 2.3.4 Behavioral Segmentation
Divides the market based on **consumer behavior patterns** toward the product.
- **Variables:** Occasion (regular, festive, gifting), benefits sought, usage rate, loyalty status, buyer readiness.
- *Food industry example:* Cadbury Celebrations peaks during Diwali/Ramadan/Easter (occasion-based).

## 2.4 The STP Process — Step-by-Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Sequential Steps of Strategic Marketing**
>
> 1. **S — Segmentation:** Divide the heterogeneous market into homogeneous sub-groups.
> 2. **T — Targeting:** Evaluate each segment's attractiveness and select one or more to enter.
> 3. **P — Positioning:** Design the firm's offering and image to occupy a distinct place in the target customer's mind.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Model | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Market Share (%)** | $\text{MS} = \left(\dfrac{\text{Company Sales}}{\text{Total Market Sales}}\right) \times 100$ | Competitive position tracking |
| **Market Growth Rate (%)** | $\text{MGR} = \left(\dfrac{\text{Sales}_{t} - \text{Sales}_{t-1}}{\text{Sales}_{t-1}}\right) \times 100$ | Segment attractiveness |
| **BCG Matrix Quadrant** | $\text{Relative Market Share} = \dfrac{\text{Business Unit Share}}{\text{Largest Competitor Share}}$ | Portfolio decisions in food company |
| **Segment Attractiveness Score (SAS)** | $\text{SAS} = \sum (w_i \times s_i)$, where $w_i$ is weight and $s_i$ is score on factor $i$ | Targeting decision |
| **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{\text{Profit}_t}{(1+d)^t}$ | Loyalty segmentation |
| **Penetration Rate (%)** | $\text{PR} = \left(\dfrac{\text{Customers Using Product}}{\text{Total Population in Segment}}\right) \times 100$ | Segment reach analysis |
| **PESTEL Scoring** | Sum of weighted scores across 6 factors | Macro-environment analysis |

> **Notation Convention:** $d$ = discount rate, $t$ = time period, $n$ = customer lifespan in years.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering Fields

- **Strategic Planning:** Helps food companies prioritize R&D investment.
- **Product Development:** Identifies niche segments (e.g., gluten-free, vegan, keto).
- **Supply Chain Design:** Geographic segmentation informs distribution center placement.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** PESTEL analysis flags upcoming food safety laws.
- **Investment Decisions:** CLV and SAS help allocate marketing budgets efficiently.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Macro-Environment onto a Food Company

> **Scenario:** A Kerala-based coconut oil brand (e.g., KLF Nirmal Industries) evaluating its marketing environment.

| PESTEL Factor | Force Identified | Impact on Firm | Strategic Response |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Political** | FSSAI mandates saturated fat labeling | Compliance cost ↑ | Redesign packaging with FSSAI logo |
| **Economic** | Recession in Gulf countries → remittance ↓ | NRI demand for premium oil ↓ | Launch economy pack at ₹99 |
| **Social** | Rising veganism in metros | Demand for cold-pressed virgin coconut oil ↑ | Launch cold-pressed variant |
| **Technological** | E-commerce growth in Tier-2 cities | Distribution channel expansion | Partner with Amazon Pantry, BigBasket |
| **Environmental** | Ban on single-use plastic | Packaging redesign needed | Switch to glass bottles & tin containers |
| **Legal** | Legal Metrology Act — strict weight norms | Need precision filling | Invest in automated volumetric fillers |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Segment Attractiveness Score (SAS)

Suppose a food company wants to evaluate **three potential market segments** — Organic Foods, Ready-to-Eat Meals, and Traditional Snacks — using four weighted criteria.

### Step 1: Define the Criteria and Weights

| Criterion | Weight ($w_i$) | Organic | RTE Meals | Traditional Snacks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Market Size | 0.30 | 8 | 9 | 6 |
| Growth Rate | 0.25 | 9 | 8 | 5 |
| Competition Intensity | 0.20 | 6 (low) | 5 (low) | 3 (high) |
| Alignment with Brand | 0.25 | 9 | 6 | 8 |
| **Sum of Weights** | **1.00** | | | |

### Step 2: Apply the SAS Formula

For each segment:

$$
\text{SAS}_{j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (w_i \times s_{ij})
$$

where $j$ indexes the segment and $i$ indexes the criterion.

### Step 3: Compute for Organic Foods

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAS}_{\text{Organic}} &= (0.30 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 9) \\
&= 2.40 + 2.25 + 1.20 + 2.25 \\
&= 8.10
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute for Ready-to-Eat Meals

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAS}_{\text{RTE}} &= (0.30 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.25 \times 6) \\
&= 2.70 + 2.00 + 1.00 + 1.50 \\
&= 7.20
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute for Traditional Snacks

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAS}_{\text{Snacks}} &= (0.30 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.25 \times 8) \\
&= 1.80 + 1.25 + 0.60 + 2.00 \\
&= 5.65
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Compare and Select

Ranking: Organic (8.10) > RTE (7.20) > Snacks (5.65).

**Decision:** The company should prioritize **Organic Foods** as its primary target segment, with **RTE Meals** as a secondary segment.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical — Market Share & Growth Rate

> **Problem:** A regional dairy company in Kerala had total industry sales of ₹500 crores last year. The company itself posted sales of ₹75 crores. This year, industry sales grew to ₹550 crores, and the company's sales rose to ₹82.5 crores. Compute (a) current market share, (b) prior market share, and (c) comment on the trend.

### Part (a): Current Market Share

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MS}_{\text{current}} &= \left(\frac{82.5}{550}\right) \times 100 \\
&= 0.15 \times 100 \\
&= 15.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (b): Prior Market Share

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MS}_{\text{prior}} &= \left(\frac{75}{500}\right) \times 100 \\
&= 0.15 \times 100 \\
&= 15.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (c): Trend Commentary

Although the company's absolute sales grew from ₹75 crores to ₹82.5 crores (a **10% growth**), the market share remained constant at **15%**. This implies the company grew *in line with the industry* and failed to **outpace competitors**. Strategic action is required to gain share through innovation, pricing, or distribution expansion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Explained:** The denominator in market share is the *total industry sales*, not the prior year. Each year's market share is calculated using that year's own industry sales figure, allowing direct year-on-year comparison.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> **Note on Diagram Convention:** Solid arrows (`-->`) represent **strategic action** by the firm, while dashed arrows (`-.->`) represent the **influence direction** of external forces upon the company. This visual convention helps separate *agent* from *patient* roles in the marketing environment schema.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the marketing microenvironment. List its key components.**

**Model Answer:**
The marketing microenvironment consists of forces **close to the company** that directly influence its ability to serve customers. Its key components are:
1. **Suppliers** — providers of raw materials and inputs.
2. **Customers** — the consumer markets (B2C) and business markets (B2B).
3. **Competitors** — direct and indirect rivals.
4. **Marketing Intermediaries** — distributors, retailers, logistics providers.
5. **Publics** — media, government, financial, citizen-action groups.
6. **Internal Departments** — R&D, HR, Finance, Operations.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for list with brief relevance)*

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is psychographic segmentation? Give one example from the food industry.**

**Model Answer:**
Psychographic segmentation divides buyers into groups based on **psychological traits** — lifestyle, values, personality, and social class. Unlike demographics, it captures the *why* behind purchase behavior.

> **Example from food industry:** Organic Tattva and 24 Mantra Organic target the **LOHAS (Lifestyles of Health and Sustainability)** consumer segment, whose values emphasize health, environmental responsibility, and clean eating — even at premium prices.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for concept + 1.5 for example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **PESTEL framework** in detail with specific examples from the **Indian food industry**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **four major bases of market segmentation** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a): PESTEL Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** For full 7 marks, students must cover **all six letters of PESTEL**, give **one example per factor**, and link the example to the **Indian food industry** specifically.

The **PESTEL framework** analyzes the **macro-environmental forces** that shape opportunities and threats for any organization. The acronym stands for **Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, and Legal** factors.

**1. Political Factors (P)**
Government policies, political stability, trade restrictions, and subsidies directly shape the food industry. Example: The Indian government's **Pradhan Mantri Garib Kalyan Anna Yojana (PMGKAY)** distributes free food grain to 80 crore beneficiaries, creating sustained demand for wheat and rice from FCI godowns. Similarly, the **Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme** for the food processing industry encourages capacity expansion.

**2. Economic Factors (E)**
Macroeconomic variables such as GDP growth, inflation, disposable income, and exchange rates influence food consumption patterns. Example: During **high inflation in 2023-24**, Indian consumers shifted from premium dairy brands to economy SKUs — sales of Amul's economy pack of butter surged. **Remittance inflows from the Gulf** sustain Kerala's food retail growth, especially premium coconut oil, banana chips, and payasam mixes.

**3. Social Factors (S)**
Demographic shifts, cultural values, lifestyle changes, and education levels affect food preferences. Example: Rising **health consciousness and veganism** in metros has driven demand for plant-based proteins (e.g., GoodDot, Imagine Foods). Kerala's **high literacy rate (~96%)** correlates with strong demand for **organic and clean-label** products. Festive occasions like **Onam, Diwali, and Ramadan** trigger occasion-based food demand spikes.

**4. Technological Factors (T)**
Innovation in production, logistics, and communication alters industry dynamics. Example: **Cold chain logistics** innovations (reefer trucks, IoT temperature sensors) have extended the shelf life of seafood and dairy exports. **AI-based demand forecasting** used by BigBasket and JioMart helps reduce food waste. **E-commerce platforms** (Swiggy, Zomato, Blinkit) have created the **quick commerce (Q-commerce)** food delivery category.

**5. Environmental Factors (E)**
Climate change, sustainability concerns, and ecological regulations pressure food companies. Example: India's **single-use plastic ban** (effective 2022) forced companies like ITC and Hindustan Unilever to redesign packaging. Carbon footprint labeling is emerging. **Climate-induced crop failure** in 2023 (deficient monsoon) caused tomato and onion price spikes, impacting processed food input costs.

**6. Legal Factors (L)**
Laws and regulations governing food safety, labeling, and advertising. Example: The **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 (FSSAI)** mandates licensing, hygiene, and labeling. The **Legal Metrology (Packaged Commodities) Rules, 2011** require accurate net weight declaration. **ASCI guidelines** prohibit misleading food advertisements — recent action against Maggi's "No MSG" claim illustrates enforcement.

**Conclusion:** PESTEL analysis is a **continuous strategic activity** that helps food firms anticipate change, comply with regulations, and identify emerging market opportunities.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b): Four Bases of Market Segmentation

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** 7 marks = 1 mark per base for definition + 1 mark per base for food industry example + 1 mark for overall summary table/comparison.

Market segmentation is the process of dividing a **heterogeneous market** into **homogeneous sub-groups** that share similar needs, characteristics, or behaviors. The four major bases are:

**1. Geographic Segmentation**
Divides the market by **location-based variables** such as country, region, state, city size, and climate.
> *Food industry example:* **ITC Aashirvaad Atta** offers Kerala-specific variants like *Aashirvaad Kerala Parotta Mix* tailored to regional taste. **Britannia** distributes different biscuit variants in North vs. South India based on local flavor preferences.

**2. Demographic Segmentation**
Divides the market by **demographic and socio-economic variables** — age, gender, income, education, occupation, family size, religion, and generation.
> *Food industry example:* **Horlicks** (by GSK) segments by age — Horlicks for children, Women's Horlicks for adult women, Protein Plus for seniors. **Nestlé Cerelac** targets infants (6–24 months). **Campa Cola** during its 2023 relaunch targeted the **youth segment** through low pricing.

**3. Psychographic Segmentation**
Divides the market by **psychological traits** — lifestyle, values, personality, social class, and AIO (Activities, Interests, Opinions).
> *Food industry example:* **Organic Tattva**, **24 Mantra**, and **Conscious Food** target the **LOHAS segment** (Lifestyles of Health and Sustainability). **McDonald's** in India offers **McAloo Tikki** aligned with vegetarian values. **Sattviko** positions itself on the **Sattvic diet philosophy**.

**4. Behavioral Segmentation**
Divides the market by **consumer behavior patterns** — occasion, benefits sought, usage rate, loyalty status, and buyer readiness.
> *Food industry example:* **Cadbury Celebrations** is heavily occasion-segmented (Diwali, Rakhi, Eid, Christmas). **Amul** offers loyalty benefits through its **Amul Parlour network** for repeat purchasers. **Red Bull** targets the **high-usage, energy-seeking** segment.

| Basis | Key Variables | Food Industry Example |
|---|---|---|
| Geographic | Region, climate, urban/rural | Kerala-specific parotta mixes |
| Demographic | Age, income, family | Horlicks, Cerelac |
| Psychographic | Lifestyle, values | Organic Tattva (LOHAS) |
| Behavioral | Occasion, loyalty, usage | Cadbury Celebrations (festive) |

**Conclusion:** A robust segmentation strategy uses **multiple bases in combination** (e.g., a product might be segmented by age + lifestyle + occasion simultaneously), enabling the firm to develop targeted offerings that resonate with each sub-group.

---

### **Question B** (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** What is **Market Segmentation**? Explain the **process of market segmentation** in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Differentiate between **Targeting** and **Positioning** strategies. Illustrate with a real-time **Indian food brand** example. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a): Market Segmentation & Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** 7 marks = 1 mark for definition + 5 marks for process steps (1 per step) + 1 mark for food industry linkage.

**Definition:** Market segmentation is the strategic process of dividing a broad market into **distinct sub-sets of buyers** with common needs, characteristics, or behaviors, who might require separate marketing strategies.

**The Process of Market Segmentation (7 stages):**

**Step 1 — Define the Market**
Identify the broad product-market the firm operates in. Example: The market for **packaged breakfast cereals in India**.

**Step 2 — Identify Segmentation Variables**
Select the relevant bases — geographic, demographic, psychographic, behavioral.

**Step 3 — Develop Segment Profiles**
Profile each segment in terms of size, growth, profitability, and accessibility.

**Step 4 — Evaluate Segment Attractiveness**
Use the Segment Attractiveness Score (SAS) framework to rank segments.

**Step 5 — Select Target Segments**
Choose one or more segments based on fit with company strengths and resources.

**Step 6 — Develop Positioning**
Craft a unique value proposition for each chosen target.

**Step 7 — Design Marketing Mix (4Ps)**
Tailor Product, Price, Place, Promotion to each target.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b): Targeting vs Positioning with Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** 7 marks = 2 marks for each of 4 differentiation points + 3 marks for Indian food brand example.

**Brand Chosen for Illustration: Amul**

| Dimension | Targeting | Positioning |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Choosing which customer segments to serve | Designing the brand's image in the target's mind |
| **Objective** | Decide *who* to serve | Decide *how* to be perceived |
| **Focus** | Customer selection | Customer perception |
| **Outcome** | List of target segments | Unique value proposition |
| **Example: Amul** | Targets **mass-market Indian households** (Tier 1, 2, 3 cities) — both B2C (dairy, butter) and B2B (HoReCa, bakeries) | Positioned as **"The Taste of India"** — affordable, trustworthy, high-quality dairy from **Gujarat's cooperative farmer network** |

Amul's targeting choice: *serve every Indian household across income levels*. Amul's positioning: *wholesome, value-for-money, farmer-owned cooperative dairy brand*. This two-step combination is what makes Amul a category leader in Indian dairy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing Microenvironment with Macroenvironment** — Many students mix the two. Remember: *Micro = controllable, close actors*; *Macro = uncontrollable, PESTEL forces*.
> 2. **Memorizing PESTEL but skipping examples** — Generic definitions earn 2-3 marks; **food industry-specific examples** fetch full marks.
> 3. **Skipping the "process"** in segmentation — Simply listing 4 bases is not enough. The examiner expects **sequence** (define market → pick variables → profile → evaluate → target → position).
> 4. **Ignoring STP linkage** — Segmentation is incomplete without targeting and positioning. Always link back to STP.
> 5. **Writing vague "lifestyle" answers** — For psychographic segmentation, name specific Indian food brands and explain *why* (values, social class, AIO).
> 6. **Formula errors in SAS computation** — Weight × Score must be summed, not averaged. Each weight ≤ 1, and weights must sum to 1.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Marketing Environment & Segmentation**
>
> - [x] **Marketing Environment** has TWO layers: **Microenvironment** (suppliers, customers, competitors, intermediaries, publics, internal) and **Macroenvironment** (**PESTEL**).
> - [x] **PESTEL** = Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, Legal.
> - [x] **Microenvironment = close & partially controllable**; **Macroenvironment = distant & uncontrollable**.
> - [x] **STP** = Segmentation → Targeting → Positioning.
> - [x] **Four segmentation bases:** Geographic, Demographic, Psychographic, Behavioral.
> - [x] **Demographic** variables: age, income, gender, education, occupation, family.
> - [x] **Psychographic** variables: lifestyle, values, personality, social class, AIO.
> - [x] **Behavioral** variables: occasion, usage rate, benefits sought, loyalty, readiness.
> - [x] **Market Share** = (Company Sales / Industry Sales) × 100.
> - [x] **Segment Attractiveness Score (SAS)** = $\sum (w_i \times s_i)$; weights sum to 1.
> - [x] **TAM / SAM / SOM** model: Total / Serviceable / Obtainable Market.
> - [x] **Effective segmentation criteria** (Kotler): **Measurable, Accessible, Substantial, Differentiable, Actionable** (acronym: **MADSA**).
> - [x] **Targeting strategies:** Undifferentiated, Differentiated, Concentrated, Micromarketing.
> - [x] **Positioning strategies** build on a **Unique Value Proposition (UVP)** and a **Perceptual Map**.
> - [x] **Food industry examples** to memorize: Amul, ITC Aashirvaad, Cadbury Celebrations, Horlicks, Organic Tattva, Maggi.
> - [x] **Indian regulatory body** for food: **FSSAI** (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India).
> - [x] **Key Indian food laws:** FSSAI Act 2006, Legal Metrology Act 2009, ASCI Advertising Code.

> **Final Tip for KTU Board Exam:** In 14-mark answers, **structure with headings, sub-points, tables, and examples**. The examiner allocates 1 mark for clear structure, 2-3 marks for definitions, 5-6 marks for explanations, and 2-3 marks for food industry examples. **Never write a 14-mark answer as a wall of text.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Market Measurement, Forecasting, Advertisement, and Publicity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT724 - FOOD INDUSTRY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Market Measurement, Forecasting, Advertisement, and Publicity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 3 — MARKET MEASUREMENT, FORECASTING, ADVERTISEMENT & PUBLICITY

## 1.1 Market Measurement — Core Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Market Measurement** is the qualitative and quantitative assessment of the size, structure, characteristics, and growth potential of a market — typically expressed through **market size**, **market share**, **market potential**, and **sales potential** figures relevant to a food business.

In the context of food industry management, market measurement is the act of *putting numbers* on:
1. **How big** is the addressable market for a particular food product category (e.g., packaged dairy, breakfast cereals, ready-to-eat snacks)?
2. **How much** of that market does our company capture?
3. **How fast** is the market growing?
4. **Who** are the buyers, what do they consume, and how frequently?

### Core Terminology (KTU Syllabus Anchors)

| Term | Definition | Unit / Metric |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Total Market Potential (TMP)** | The total sales volume/value of a product class expected in a defined geographic area during a stated period. | **₹ (Rupees) / Volume in tonnes / Litres** |
| **Company Sales Potential (CSP)** | The share of TMP that a particular firm could capture under ideal marketing conditions. | **₹ or % of TMP** |
| **Market Share** | The proportion of total market sales earned by one company. | **Percentage (%)** |
| **Market Forecast** | Expected demand for a product category over a specific future period under a given marketing environment. | **₹ or Units** |
| **Sales Forecast** | Expected company-level sales over a specific period at a given marketing-effort level. | **₹ or Units** |

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a Kerala breakfast market.** The total breakfast market in Ernakulam district = every morning meal consumed by ~3.5 lakh households + eateries. That is the *Market Potential*. Of this, if *Amul* sells breakfast curd to 70,000 households, its *Market Share* = 70,000 / 3,50,000 ≈ **20%**. Now, by adding ₹50 lakh in local advertising and 200 new retailers, the company *forecasts* it could push share to 25% next year — this forecast becomes the *Sales Forecast*.

The point of market measurement is to replace guesswork ("our product is doing okay, I think") with data-grounded decisions ("our Q2 sales were ₹2.4 Cr, market grew 8%, so we under-performed by 3 points — corrective action: distributor expansion in Thrissur").

---

## 1.2 Market Forecasting — Core Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Market Forecasting** is the systematic process of estimating future demand for a product or service under stated conditions, using historical data, market intelligence, statistical models, and managerial judgment.

Forecasting answers the question: *"How much will our customers want to buy tomorrow, next month, next season, next year?"*

In the food industry, forecasting is especially critical because of:
- **Perishability** of raw materials (milk, fresh produce, meat).
- **Seasonality** (ice cream peaks in summer, sweets in festive season, palm oil demand spikes during Onam).
- **Short shelf-life** that demands tight production planning.

### Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a forecasting model like a **GPS navigation system** for a delivery truck. The GPS doesn't *guarantee* zero traffic tomorrow, but it uses past route data, current speed, and traffic patterns to give you the *most likely* arrival time. Similarly, forecasting doesn't guarantee demand — it gives a *best estimate* with a confidence range.

The two broadest families of forecasting techniques are:

1. **Qualitative Forecasting** — based on judgment, opinion, surveys, expert panels. Useful when historical data is absent (e.g., launching a new functional beverage).
2. **Quantitative Forecasting** — based on mathematical/statistical models applied to numerical historical data. Used when stable historical data exists.

---

## 1.3 Advertisement & Publicity — Core Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advertisement (Advertising)** is a *paid, non-personal* communication of marketing messages by an identified sponsor, delivered through mass media channels, to inform, persuade, or remind target audiences about a product, service, or idea.
>
> **Publicity** is *unpaid, non-personal* communication about an organization, product, or service, generated and disseminated through third-party channels (press, media, influencers, public) — usually without the sponsor paying for the airtime/space.

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you are launching a new Kerala-style banana chips brand. **Advertising** is when you *pay* ₹2 lakh to Mathrubhumi newspaper to print a colourful full-page ad — you control the message, layout, and timing. **Publicity** is when a popular food vlogger in Kochi tries your chips on camera and posts it to 50,000 YouTube subscribers — you don't pay the vlogger (usually), and the message reaches the audience via a *third party* perceived as more credible.

The AIDA framework underpins most advertising strategy:

$$A \rightarrow I \rightarrow D \rightarrow A$$

- **A** — **Attention** (capture the buyer's interest).
- **I** — **Interest** (build curiosity about the product).
- **D** — **Desire** (convert interest into wanting).
- **A** — **Action** (trigger the actual purchase).

---

## 1.4 Why These Topics Matter in Food Industry Management

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation, examiners frequently test whether students can:
> 1. Distinguish *qualitative* vs *quantitative* forecasting.
> 2. Apply a basic moving-average or trend-line forecast.
> 3. Choose the right advertising medium for a given food product category.
> 4. Differentiate advertisement from publicity using *paid vs unpaid*, *sponsor control*, and *message credibility* criteria.
> 5. Calculate market share and forecast next-period sales using simple time-series methods.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# MODULE 3 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 Methods of Market Measurement (Detailed Breakdown)

Market measurement is not a single number — it is a *layered diagnostic*. The principal components and methods are:

### A. Market Size Estimation

**Definition:** Total units or revenue generated by a product category in a defined geography and period.

**Common measurement approaches:**

1. **Top-Down (Macro) Approach**
   - Start with industry reports (Nielsen, IMARC, Euromonitor), government statistics (FSSAI, NSSO Consumer Expenditure Surveys, APEDA, MPEDA, NDDB), to estimate the total category size.
   - Filter down to geography, demographic, and channel of interest.

2. **Bottom-Up (Build-Up) Approach**
   - Start with the *number of buyers* × *average quantity bought per period* × *frequency of purchase*.
   - Often more accurate for hyper-local FMCG food markets.

3. **Analog Method (Analogy)**
   - Apply known market size in a comparable market (e.g., a similar district) to estimate the target market.

4. **Volume × Value Method**
   - Estimate both physical volume (tonnes, litres) and monetary value (₹) for a balanced view.

### B. Market Share Calculation

$$\text{Market Share (\%)} = \frac{\text{Company Sales}}{\text{Total Market Sales}} \times 100$$

- **Relative Market Share (RMS):** a company's share divided by the share of its largest competitor.

$$\text{RMS} = \frac{\text{Our Share \%}}{\text{Largest Competitor's Share \%}}$$

### C. Market Potential vs Sales Potential

$$\text{CSP} = \text{TMP} \times \text{Expected Market Share \%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Example: TMP for branded Kerala banana chips in India = ₹800 Cr. If a single firm can realistically capture 8%, CSP = ₹64 Cr.

### D. Market Growth Rate

$$\text{Growth Rate (\%)} = \frac{\text{Sales}_{\text{Current}} - \text{Sales}_{\text{Previous}}}{\text{Sales_{\text{Previous}}}} \times 100$$

---

## 2.2 Forecasting Methods — Deep Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Qualitative (Judgmental) Methods

| Method | Mechanism | When Used |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| **Sales Force Composite** | Field sales personnel submit individual territory forecasts; aggregated at HQ. | Stable channel partners; quick estimates. |
| **Jury of Executive Opinion** | Senior managers pool judgment into one number. | No historical data; new product. |
| **Delphi Method** | Structured iterative anonymous expert surveys, feedback loops until consensus. | Long-range strategic forecasts. |
| **Market Research / Consumer Survey** | Direct sampling of buyer purchase intent. | New product concept testing. |
| **Scenario Analysis** | Forecasts under optimistic / pessimistic / most-likely scenarios. | Highly volatile food categories. |

### 2.2.2 Quantitative (Statistical) Methods

#### (a) Naïve Method

$$F_{t+1} = D_t$$

> Forecast for next period equals actual demand of current period.

#### (b) Simple Moving Average (SMA)

$$\text{SMA}_{n} = \frac{D_{t} + D_{t-1} + D_{t-2} + \ldots + D_{t-(n-1)}}{n}$$

> Averages demand over the last *n* periods to smooth out random fluctuations.

#### (c) Weighted Moving Average (WMA)

$$\text{WMA} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} w_i \cdot D_{t-i}$$

with the constraint:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} w_i = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad w_{\text{recent}} > w_{\text{old}}$$

> Recent periods carry higher weight (e.g., 0.5, 0.3, 0.2).

#### (d) Exponential Smoothing

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha \cdot D_t + (1 - \alpha) \cdot F_t$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = smoothing constant (0 < α < 1)
- $D_t$ = actual demand in period *t*
- $F_t$ = forecast made for period *t*

> Recent observations get exponentially higher weight; older observations decay geometrically.

#### (e) Linear Trend Projection

$$\hat{Y}_t = a + b \cdot t$$

Using the least-squares method:

$$b = \frac{n \sum (t \cdot Y) - \sum t \cdot \sum Y}{n \sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2}, \quad a = \bar{Y} - b \bar{t}$$

where $Y_t$ = sales in period *t*, and *t* is the time index.

#### (f) Regression Analysis (Causal Model)

$$Y = a + b_1 X_1 + b_2 X_2 + \ldots + \epsilon$$

> Demand (*Y*) is modelled as a function of independent variables like price, advertising spend, income, season, competitor activity.

---

## 2.3 Advertisement — Detailed Theoretical Structure

### 2.3.1 Objectives of Advertising (food industry examples)

1. **Informative advertising** — launch of a new millet-based snack.
2. **Persuasive advertising** — promote organic ghee vs regular ghee.
3. **Reminder advertising** — repeat brand cues for established products (Amul butter, Ching's Schezwan chutney).
4. **Reinforcement advertising** — post-purchase reassurance ("you're making the right choice").
5. **Brand building** — long-term emotional equity (Britannia's "Eat Healthy, Think Better").

### 2.3.2 Advertising Media — Detailed Comparison

| Media Type | Examples | Strength | Limitation | Best Food Use |
|-----------|----------|----------|------------|---------------|
| **Print** | Newspapers, magazines, flyers | Tangible, long shelf life, local reach | Declining readership | Recipe inserts, mall open ads |
| **Broadcast — TV** | Set-top box, OTT platforms | Mass reach, audio-visual impact, emotion-building | High cost, ad-skipping | FMCG staples, packaged food launches |
| **Broadcast — Radio** | FM, community radio | Low cost, regional languages, drive-time reach | Audio only, low attention | Local dairies, bakeries |
| **Outdoor (OOH)** | Hoardings, transit, billboards | High repeat impressions, geo-targeted | No action mechanism | Restaurant menus, beverage brands |
| **Digital / Online** | Social media, search ads, YouTube, influencer | Precision targeting, measurable ROI, low entry cost | Ad fatigue, ad blockers | E-commerce packaged food, premium snacks |
| **In-store / POP** | Shelf displays, banners, sampling | Trigger point at purchase | Limited audience | All packaged food |
| **Direct Mail / SMS** | Coupons, newsletters, WhatsApp | Personal, trackable | Low response rate | Loyalty programs, offers |

### 2.3.3 Advertising Budget Methods

| Method | Logic |
|--------|-------|
| **Percentage of Sales** | A fixed % of last year's sales is allocated. |
| **Objective & Task** | Define objectives, list tasks, cost them. |
| **Affordable** | Spend only what is left after other expenses. |
| **Competitive Parity** | Match competitor's ad spend intensity. |
| **Unit Sales Based** | A fixed amount per unit sold. |

### 2.3.4 Advertising Effectiveness Metrics

- **Reach** = % of target audience exposed at least once.
- **Frequency** = average number of exposures per person.
- **Gross Rating Points (GRP)** = Reach × Frequency.
- **Cost Per Thousand (CPM)** = (Ad Cost / Audience) × 1000.
- **Return on Ad Spend (ROAS)** = (Revenue from Ads / Ad Cost) × 100.
- **Brand Recall** and **Brand Recognition** via consumer surveys.

---

## 2.4 Publicity — Detailed Theoretical Structure

### 2.4.1 Public Relations (PR) Tools in Food Industry

- **Press releases** — announcing new product launches, ISO certifications, awards.
- **Press conferences** — milestone events (e.g., factory opening, export consignment).
- **Sponsorships** — sports events, food festivals (Kerala Food Fest), cultural events.
- **Events & experiences** — cook-offs, sampling drives, food truck tours.
- **Influencer / KOL partnerships** — micro-influencers reviewing products.
- **CSR activities** — feeding programs, farmer welfare, sustainability reports.
- **Crisis communication** — managing FSSAI-related news, contamination issues, recalls.

### 2.4.2 Publicity Process

$$\text{Identify Story Idea} \rightarrow \text{Craft Message} \rightarrow \text{Select Media} \rightarrow \text{Pitch to Journalist / KOL} \rightarrow \text{Coverage / Air Time} \rightarrow \text{Monitor \& Amplify}$$

### 2.4.3 Advantages & Limitations

| Advantages of Publicity | Limitations |
|--------------------------|-------------|
| Highly credible (third-party endorsement) | No direct control over message |
| Zero direct media cost (organic) | Timing is unpredictable |
| Wider reach via media amplification | Cannot be repeated on demand |
| Strong impact on brand image | Hard to attribute to specific marketing effort |

---

## 2.5 Advertisement vs Publicity — Tabular Comparison

| Dimension | Advertisement | Publicity |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Cost** | Paid | Usually free |
| **Sponsor Identification** | Clearly identified sponsor | Sponsor may not be obvious |
| **Control over message** | Full control | Limited / no control |
| **Credibility** | Lower (audience knows it's paid) | Higher (perceived as editorial) |
| **Reach** | Targeted by media buy | Organic & viral |
| **Frequency** | Repeatable at will | Cannot be repeated at will |
| **Measurability** | High (reach, GRP, ROAS) | Difficult |
| **Speed** | Quick deployment | Slower (depends on journalist/KOL) |
| **Legal disclosure** | Required in many jurisdictions | Less formal regulation |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit / Variable Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Market Share % | $(\text{Our Sales} / \text{Total Market Sales}) \times 100$ | % |
| 2 | Relative Market Share | $\text{Our Share \%} / \text{Largest Competitor Share \%}$ | Ratio |
| 3 | Company Sales Potential | $\text{TMP} \times \text{Expected Share \%}$ | ₹ or Units |
| 4 | Growth Rate % | $(S_t - S_{t-1}) / S_{t-1} \times 100$ | % |
| 5 | Naïve Forecast | $F_{t+1} = D_t$ | Units |
| 6 | Simple Moving Average | $\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} D_{t-i} / n$ | Units |
| 7 | Weighted Moving Average | $\sum w_i D_{t-i}$, where $\sum w_i = 1$ | Units |
| 8 | Exponential Smoothing | $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ | Units |
| 9 | Linear Trend | $\hat{Y} = a + b t$ | Units |
| 10 | Trend Slope | $b = [n \sum tY - \sum t \sum Y] / [n \sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2]$ | — |
| 11 | GRP | $\text{Reach \%} \times \text{Frequency}$ | — |
| 12 | CPM | $(\text{Ad Cost} / \text{Audience Size}) \times 1000$ | ₹ per 1000 |
| 13 | ROAS | $(\text{Ad-driven Revenue} / \text{Ad Cost}) \times 100$ | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always use **\vert** or **\mid** in tables for absolute value or modulus to avoid breaking markdown pipe syntax (e.g., write $\vert Y \vert$ instead of $\vert Y \vert$ as raw text).

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering & Business

- **Production planning:** Forecast demand → schedule dairy plant batches.
- **Inventory management:** Reduce spoilage of perishable items.
- **Cold-chain sizing:** Forecasted throughput dictates cold-storage capacity.
- **Capital investment decisions:** Long-range forecasts drive new plant location & capex.
- **Marketing ROI:** CPM, ROAS, GRP guide media-mix optimization for launches.
- **Crisis containment:** Well-managed publicity protects brand equity during food-safety events.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# MODULE 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Simple Moving Average Forecast

**Problem Statement:** A bakery's quarterly sales (in thousand loaves) for the last 4 quarters are given below:

| Quarter | Sales ($D_t$) in '000 loaves |
|---------|------------------------------|
| Q1 | 18 |
| Q2 | 22 |
| Q3 | 20 |
| Q4 | 24 |

Compute the 3-quarter simple moving average forecast for Q1 of the next year.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the window size.**
We are asked for a 3-quarter moving average, so $n = 3$.

**Step 2 — List the most recent 3 quarters.**
The most recent three quarters are Q2, Q3, Q4 of the current year:

$$\text{Window} = \{D_{Q2}, D_{Q3}, D_{Q4}\} = \{22,\ 20,\ 24\}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the moving-average formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SMA}_3 &= \frac{D_{Q2} + D_{Q3} + D_{Q4}}{3} \\
&= \frac{22 + 20 + 24}{3} \\
&= \frac{66}{3} \\
&= 22
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — State the forecast.**
Forecast for next quarter (Q1 of next year) = **22 thousand loaves**.

**Interpretation:** The bakery is expected to produce approximately **22,000 loaves** in the next quarter if past demand patterns continue.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Exponential Smoothing Forecast

**Problem Statement:** Forecast sales for May using exponential smoothing with α = 0.3, given the following data:

| Month | Actual Demand ($D_t$) | Forecast ($F_t$) |
|-------|----------------------|------------------|
| January | 200 | — |
| February | 240 | 200 (assumed) |
| March | 210 | 212 |
| April | 230 | 211.4 |
| May | ? | to find |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the exponential smoothing equation.**

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha \cdot D_t + (1 - \alpha) \cdot F_t$$

**Step 2 — Verify March forecast from February data.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{Mar}} &= \alpha \cdot D_{\text{Feb}} + (1-\alpha) \cdot F_{\text{Feb}} \\
&= 0.3 \times 240 + 0.7 \times 200 \\
&= 72 + 140 \\
&= 212
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the given data.

**Step 3 — Verify April forecast from March data.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{Apr}} &= \alpha \cdot D_{\text{Mar}} + (1-\alpha) \cdot F_{\text{Mar}} \\
&= 0.3 \times 210 + 0.7 \times 212 \\
&= 63 + 148.4 \\
&= 211.4
\end{aligned}
$$

This also matches the given data.

**Step 4 — Compute May forecast from April data.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{May}} &= \alpha \cdot D_{\text{Apr}} + (1-\alpha) \cdot F_{\text{Apr}} \\
&= 0.3 \times 230 + 0.7 \times 211.4 \\
&= 69 + 147.98 \\
&= 216.98
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Forecast for May = **216.98 units** (≈ **217 units**).

> [!TIP]
> Note: A higher α (e.g., 0.7) reacts faster to recent demand swings. A lower α (e.g., 0.2) smooths demand more heavily. In food retail where promotions can spike sales sharply, a moderate α (0.3 to 0.5) is common.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Linear Trend Forecast (Least Squares)

**Problem Statement:** Annual sales of a packaged spice brand for 5 consecutive years are:

| Year ($t$) | Sales ($Y_t$) in ₹ Lakhs |
|------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | 100 |
| 2 | 120 |
| 3 | 115 |
| 4 | 130 |
| 5 | 145 |

Forecast Year 6 sales using linear trend.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Tabulate intermediate calculations.**

| $t$ | $Y_t$ | $t \cdot Y_t$ | $t^2$ |
|-----|-------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | 100 | 100 | 1 |
| 2 | 120 | 240 | 4 |
| 3 | 115 | 345 | 9 |
| 4 | 130 | 520 | 16 |
| 5 | 145 | 725 | 25 |
| **Σ** | **610** | **1930** | **55** |

**Step 2 — Compute slope (b).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{n \sum tY - \sum t \cdot \sum Y}{n \sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 1930 - 15 \times 610}{5 \times 55 - (15)^2} \\
&= \frac{9650 - 9150}{275 - 225} \\
&= \frac{500}{50} \\
&= 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute intercept (a).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{t} &= \frac{\sum t}{n} = \frac{15}{5} = 3 \\
\bar{Y} &= \frac{\sum Y}{n} = \frac{610}{5} = 122 \\
a &= \bar{Y} - b \cdot \bar{t} = 122 - 10 \times 3 = 92
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Formulate the trend equation.**

$$\hat{Y}_t = 92 + 10 t$$

**Step 5 — Forecast Year 6 ($t = 6$).**

$$\hat{Y}_6 = 92 + 10 \times 6 = 152 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Answer:** Forecast sales for Year 6 = **₹ 152 lakhs**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Derivation 4 — Market Share and Growth Rate

**Problem Statement:** Total Kerala market for branded ready-to-drink (RTD) fruit juices in 2024 was estimated at ₹ 1,200 Cr. A company called "FreshKerala Co." had revenues of ₹ 144 Cr from this segment. In 2023, the total market was ₹ 1,000 Cr and FreshKerala's revenue was ₹ 110 Cr. Compute:
- (i) Market Share in 2024
- (ii) Market Share in 2023
- (iii) Market Growth Rate (2023 → 2024)
- (iv) Company Growth Rate (2023 → 2024)
- (v) Relative Market Share (RMS) if the largest competitor has 22% share in 2024.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**(i) Market Share 2024**

$$
\text{MS}_{2024} = \frac{144}{1200} \times 100 = 12\%
$$

**(ii) Market Share 2023**

$$
\text{MS}_{2023} = \frac{110}{1000} \times 100 = 11\%
$$

**(iii) Market Growth Rate**

$$
\text{GR}_{\text{Market}} = \frac{1200 - 1000}{1000} \times 100 = 20\%
$$

**(iv) Company Growth Rate**

$$
\text{GR}_{\text{Company}} = \frac{144 - 110}{110} \times 100 = \frac{34}{110} \times 100 \approx 30.91\%
$$

**(v) Relative Market Share**

$$
\text{RMS} = \frac{12}{22} \approx 0.545
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - The market grew **20%**, but the company grew **30.91%**, so FreshKerala *outperformed* the market — it gained 1 share point (from 11% to 12%).
> - RMS < 1 means FreshKerala is *smaller* than the market leader. Goal: push RMS > 1 to become the leader.

---

## 3.5 Worked Derivation 5 — Advertising Reach, Frequency, GRP, CPM

**Problem Statement:** A TV ad campaign for a new flavoured milk targets 50 lakh households. The ad is aired 4 times to each household. Total ad cost = ₹ 80 lakh. Compute GRP, CPM.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Express Reach as a percentage.**

$$\text{Reach} = \frac{50,00,000}{\text{Total TV Households}}$$

Assume total TV households in target region = 100 lakh:

$$\text{Reach} = \frac{50}{100} \times 100 = 50\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute GRP.**

$$
\text{GRP} = \text{Reach (\%)} \times \text{Frequency} = 50 \times 4 = 200
$$

**Step 3 — Compute CPM.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPM} &= \frac{\text{Ad Cost}}{\text{Audience}} \times 1000 \\
&= \frac{80,00,000}{50,00,000} \times 1000 \\
&= 1.6 \times 1000 \\
&= ₹1600 \text{ per thousand households}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** ₹ 1,600 to reach 1,000 households — useful for benchmarking across media.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Forecasting Method When?

| Situation | Recommended Method | Reason |
|-----------|--------------------|--------|
| No historical data, new product | Qualitative: Market research / Jury / Delphi | Past data is irrelevant for novel demand |
| Stable mature product, 24+ periods of data | Quantitative: Linear trend or Regression | Long-term trend well established |
| Highly seasonal food product | Time-series with seasonal indices | Captures peaks & troughs (e.g., Onam, Christmas) |
| Short-term operational planning | Naïve or Simple Moving Average | Quick, easy, minimal data |
| Sharp recent change | Exponential Smoothing (high α) or WMA | Reacts to recent shifts |
| Many causal drivers (price, ad spend, season) | Multiple regression | Accounts for multiple independent variables |
| Strategic 5-10 year horizon | Delphi / Scenario analysis | Long-term expert judgment |

---

## 3.7 Advertisement vs Publicity Decision Flow

```
Decision Start
     ↓
Is the message paid for by the company?
     ↓
   YES → ADVERTISEMENT
     ↓
   NO → Is the company identifiable as sponsor?
            ↓
          YES → PUBLICITY (sponsored message via media)
          ↓
          NO  → WORD OF MOUTH / EARNED MEDIA
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# MODULE 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the following terms with one suitable food-industry example each:**
(a) Total Market Potential (TMP)
(b) Company Sales Potential (CSP)
(c) Market Share

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Total Market Potential (TMP)** is the maximum total sales volume or monetary value of a defined product category that could be sold in a specified geographic area during a stated period under assumed marketing conditions.

*Food-industry example:* The estimated total sales of all branded ready-to-drink (RTD) fruit juices sold in Kerala in 2024 = ₹1,200 Cr.

(b) **Company Sales Potential (CSP)** is the share of the TMP that an individual firm could reasonably expect to capture under ideal marketing-effort conditions.

*Food-industry example:* If the Kerala RTD juice market is ₹1,200 Cr and FreshKerala Co. could realistically capture 12%, its CSP = ₹144 Cr.

(c) **Market Share** is the proportion (in %) of total category sales earned by one company.

*Food-industry example:* FreshKerala's market share = (144 / 1200) × 100 = 12%.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Advertisement and Publicity. Mention any three distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Advertisement | Publicity |
|---------|---------------|-----------|
| (i) **Cost** | Paid by sponsor | Usually unpaid (earned media) |
| (ii) **Control** | Full control over message | Limited control; third-party filters message |
| (iii) **Credibility** | Lower (audience knows it's paid) | Higher (perceived as editorial / third-party) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]
**(a)** Explain the qualitative methods of sales forecasting in the food industry. Discuss any **two** methods in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bakery's monthly sales (in units of a particular cake) for the last 5 months are: 120, 135, 150, 145, 160. Using a 3-month simple moving average, forecast the sales for Month 6. If the actual Month 6 sales turn out to be 175 units, compute the forecast error and the Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD) over all 6 periods assuming the forecast for Month 4 onward was based on the 3-month SMA. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Qualitative Forecasting Methods

Qualitative forecasting techniques are used when historical numerical data is **insufficient, unreliable, or irrelevant** — for example, launching a new exotic fruit pulp in Kerala, or planning 5 years ahead in an emerging health-food category.

**Two methods discussed in detail:**

**(1) Jury of Executive Opinion**
- A panel of senior managers (marketing, sales, finance, production) each independently submits a sales estimate.
- Estimates are reviewed in a joint meeting, differences are discussed, and a single consensus forecast is finalized.
- *Strength:* Fast, low cost, leverages diverse expertise.
- *Limitation:* Subject to groupthink, anchoring bias, and dominance by senior voices.
- *Food example:* Forecast for festive-season sales of a brand of premium Kerala banana chips by combining Marketing, Distribution, and Production heads.

**(2) Delphi Method**
- A structured, iterative, *anonymous* process. Experts respond to repeated rounds of questionnaires, controlled by a coordinator.
- After each round, aggregated responses are shared (without identifying individuals), and experts revise their estimates.
- The process converges to a consensus or reveals strong dissent.
- *Strength:* Avoids groupthink and dominant personalities; rich expert reasoning.
- *Limitation:* Slow (multiple rounds); dependent on expert panel quality.
- *Food example:* Long-range (5–10 year) forecast for the demand of plant-based meat alternatives in South India using a 12-member panel of nutritionists, food technologists, and retail executives.

*(Other methods briefly: Sales Force Composite, Consumer Surveys, Scenario Analysis.)*

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Moving Average Forecast & MAD

**Given Data:**

| Month ($t$) | Actual Sales ($D_t$) |
|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | 120 |
| 2 | 135 |
| 3 | 150 |
| 4 | 145 |
| 5 | 160 |
| 6 | 175 (actual) |

**Step 1 — Compute the 3-period SMA forecast for Month 4.**

$$
F_4 = \frac{D_1 + D_2 + D_3}{3} = \frac{120 + 135 + 150}{3} = \frac{405}{3} = 135
$$

**Step 2 — Forecast for Month 5.**

$$
F_5 = \frac{D_2 + D_3 + D_4}{3} = \frac{135 + 150 + 145}{3} = \frac{430}{3} \approx 143.33
$$

**Step 3 — Forecast for Month 6.**

$$
F_6 = \frac{D_3 + D_4 + D_5}{3} = \frac{150 + 145 + 160}{3} = \frac{455}{3} \approx 151.67
$$

**Step 4 — Compute forecast errors $e_t = D_t - F_t$ for each forecast period.**

| Period | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | Error $e_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ |
|--------|-------|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| 4 | 145 | 135 | +10 | 10 |
| 5 | 160 | 143.33 | +16.67 | 16.67 |
| 6 | 175 | 151.67 | +23.33 | 23.33 |
| **Σ** | — | — | — | **50.00** |

**Step 5 — Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD).**

$$
\text{MAD} = \frac{\sum \vert e_t \vert}{n} = \frac{50}{3} \approx 16.67
$$

**Answers:**
- Forecast for Month 6 = **≈ 151.67 units** (≈ 152 units).
- MAD across 3 forecast periods = **≈ 16.67 units**.

**Interpretation:** The MAD of ~16.67 means on average our 3-month SMA forecast deviates from actual demand by about 17 units. Since actual sales are consistently *above* the forecast (positive errors), the bakery is experiencing **growing demand** — the firm should consider:
1. Switching to a **weighted moving average** or **exponential smoothing** to react faster to the upward trend.
2. Investigating **causal factors** (new competitors, marketing campaign, season) via regression.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]
**(a)** What is market measurement? Explain any **three** methods of measuring market size for a packaged food product with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data shows advertising spend (₹ Lakhs) and sales revenue (₹ Lakhs) for a regional pickle brand over 6 months:

| Month | Ad Spend ($X$) | Sales ($Y$) |
|-------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | 10 | 80 |
| 2 | 12 | 95 |
| 3 | 14 | 100 |
| 4 | 15 | 110 |
| 5 | 18 | 130 |
| 6 | 20 | 140 |

Using the linear regression equation $Y = a + bX$, predict sales when ad spend is ₹25 lakhs. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Market Measurement Methods

**Definition:** Market measurement is the systematic process of quantifying the size, structure, value, and growth potential of a market for a specific product category, in a defined geography and period, for managerial decision-making.

**Three methods:**

**(1) Top-Down (Macro) Approach**
- Start with macro statistics from industry reports (Nielsen, IMARC, Euromonitor), government sources (FSSAI, NSSO, APEDA, NDDB), and progressively filter down to the target geography and demographic.
- *Example:* To estimate the Kerala market for branded mango pickle, start with India's overall pickle market (~₹3,500 Cr from IMARC), apply Kerala's share (~3%) → ~₹105 Cr. Then refine to South Kerala (~60%) → ~₹63 Cr.

**(2) Bottom-Up (Build-Up) Approach**
- Estimate from primary data: number of buyers × average quantity bought × purchase frequency.
- *Example:* 50 lakh urban Kerala households × 1.2 kg annual pickle consumption × average price ₹200/kg = ₹120 Cr addressable market. Add HoReCa segment (~₹30 Cr) → Total ≈ ₹150 Cr.

**(3) Analog Method (Analogy)**
- Use a comparable market as a reference and adjust for size, income, taste preferences.
- *Example:* Estimate Tamil Nadu's branded pickle market by using Karnataka's market (₹400 Cr) and adjusting for population ratio (Tamil Nadu is ~1.2× Karnataka) and per-capita pickle consumption (~0.9× Karnataka) → ~₹432 Cr.

*(Other methods: Volume × Value method, Sales Force Composite, Consumer Survey.)*

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Linear Regression for Ad Spend → Sales

**Step 1 — Tabulate the data with intermediate columns.**

| $X$ | $Y$ | $X^2$ | $XY$ |
|-----|-----|-------|------|
| 10 | 80 | 100 | 800 |
| 12 | 95 | 144 | 1140 |
| 14 | 100 | 196 | 1400 |
| 15 | 110 | 225 | 1650 |
| 18 | 130 | 324 | 2340 |
| 20 | 140 | 400 | 2800 |
| **ΣX = 89** | **ΣY = 655** | **ΣX² = 1389** | **ΣXY = 10130** |

**Step 2 — Compute the slope $b$.**

$$
b = \frac{n \sum XY - \sum X \cdot \sum Y}{n \sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2} = \frac{6 \times 10130 - 89 \times 655}{6 \times 1389 - 89^2}
$$

$$
b = \frac{60780 - 58295}{8334 - 7921} = \frac{2485}{413} \approx 6.018
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the intercept $a$.**

$$
\bar{X} = \frac{89}{6} \approx 14.833, \quad \bar{Y} = \frac{655}{6} \approx 109.167
$$

$$
a = \bar{Y} - b \cdot \bar{X} = 109.167 - 6.018 \times 14.833 \approx 109.167 - 89.265 \approx 19.90
$$

**Step 4 — Regression equation.**

$$
Y = 19.90 + 6.018 \cdot X
$$

**Step 5 — Predict sales for $X = 25$ lakhs.**

$$
Y = 19.90 + 6.018 \times 25 = 19.90 + 150.45 = 170.35 \text{ lakhs}
$$

**Answer:** Predicted sales when ad spend is ₹25 lakhs ≈ **₹ 170.35 lakhs** (≈ ₹ 1.70 Cr).

**Interpretation:** Each additional ₹1 lakh in advertising spend is associated with ~₹6.02 lakh in incremental sales (in this dataset). This helps the brand manager decide on optimal ad-spend level.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in this module — Examiner's Penalty Zone:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Market Share and Relative Market Share.** Market Share is a single-firm % of the total market. RMS compares your share to the largest competitor's share — always a ratio, not a percentage.
> 2. **Confusing Market Potential with Market Forecast.** Potential = the *maximum possible* sales under ideal conditions. Forecast = the *expected* sales under planned marketing effort. Forecast ≤ Potential.
> 3. **Mistaking Publicity for Advertising.** Publicity is *not paid for* by the sponsor; if you say "the company pays the newspaper," that is advertising — even if it looks like an editorial.
> 4. **Forgetting to verify the regression line at the average point.** Always sanity-check by plugging $\bar{X}$ and getting $\bar{Y}$ — examiners reward this.
> 5. **Wrong α value in exponential smoothing.** α is between 0 and 1. Students sometimes write α > 1 or omit α, leading to nonsense forecasts.
> 6. **Calculating MAD incorrectly.** MAD = average of *absolute* errors, not signed errors. Forgetting the modulus (use $\vert e_t \vert$) is a 1-mark loss.
> 7. **Not stating units.** "Forecast = 152" is incomplete; write "₹ 152 lakhs" or "152 units." Examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units.
> 8. **Mixing time indices in moving averages.** For a 3-period SMA, ensure you are using the *immediately preceding three* periods, not skipping or repeating.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 3:**

### A. Market Measurement
- [ ] **Market Size** = total category sales in a defined geography/period.
- [ ] **Market Share %** = (Our Sales / Total Market Sales) × 100.
- [ ] **Relative Market Share (RMS)** = Our Share % / Largest Competitor's Share %.
- [ ] **Company Sales Potential (CSP)** = TMP × Expected Market Share %.
- [ ] **Growth Rate %** = (Current − Previous) / Previous × 100.
- [ ] Methods: **Top-Down, Bottom-Up, Analog, Volume × Value**.

### B. Forecasting
- [ ] **Qualitative methods**: Jury of Executive Opinion, Delphi, Sales Force Composite, Consumer Survey, Scenario Analysis — used when data is scarce.
- [ ] **Quantitative methods**: Naïve ($F_{t+1} = D_t$), Simple Moving Average, Weighted Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing ($F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$), Linear Trend, Regression.
- [ ] Choose **α** in exponential smoothing based on responsiveness vs smoothing need.
- [ ] Choose **n** in moving average based on noise level (small n = responsive, large n = smoother).
- [ ] Linear Trend equation: $\hat{Y}_t = a + b t$, derived via least squares.

### C. Advertisement
- [ ] **Paid, identified sponsor, non-personal, repeatable.**
- [ ] **AIDA model**: Attention → Interest → Desire → Action.
- [ ] **Media types**: Print, Broadcast (TV/Radio), Outdoor (OOH), Digital, In-store, Direct.
- [ ] **Metrics**: Reach (%), Frequency, GRP (Reach × Frequency), CPM (Cost per Thousand), ROAS.
- [ ] **Budget methods**: % of Sales, Objective & Task, Affordable, Competitive Parity, Unit Sales.

### D. Publicity
- [ ] **Unpaid (or earned), third-party endorsement, limited control, high credibility.**
- [ ] **PR tools**: Press release, Press conference, Sponsorship, Events, Influencers, CSR, Crisis communication.
- [ ] **Advantages**: Credibility, free media cost, virality.
- [ ] **Limitations**: No message control, unpredictable timing, hard to measure.

### E. Advertisement vs Publicity
- [ ] **Cost:** Ad = paid, Pub = unpaid (usually).
- [ ] **Sponsor identification:** Ad = clearly identified, Pub = may be disguised.
- [ ] **Message control:** Ad = full, Pub = limited.
- [ ] **Credibility:** Ad = lower, Pub = higher.
- [ ] **Measurability:** Ad = high (GRP/CPM), Pub = low.

### F. KTU Exam-Oriented Practice Tips
- [ ] Always show **formula → substitution → numerical answer → interpretation**.
- [ ] Always include **units** (₹, units, %).
- [ ] For regression, **show intermediate sums (ΣX, ΣY, ΣXY, ΣX²)** to earn method marks.
- [ ] For MAD, use **absolute values** (modulus $\vert e_t \vert$).
- [ ] For qualitative methods, **cite a food-industry example** to earn full marks.
- [ ] Memorize **AIDA** and the **Adv vs Pub** comparison table — both are favorite KTU 2-mark and 7-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Market Information System and Market Research
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT724 - FOOD INDUSTRY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Market Information System and Market Research

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Market Information System (MKIS)

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Marketing Information System (MKIS)** is a continuing and interacting structure of people, equipment, and procedures designed to gather, sort, analyze, evaluate, and distribute pertinent, timely, and accurate information for use by marketing decision-makers to improve their marketing planning, implementation, and control activities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The MKIS is *not* a one-time research project. It is a **continuous, structured, and permanent infrastructure** that supplies reliable information flow for recurring marketing decisions in the food industry (e.g., daily commodity prices, weekly sales analytics, monthly consumer sentiment).

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Smart Restaurant" Model
Imagine a chain of restaurants called *"Spice Garden Kerala."* The Managing Director needs to decide next quarter's menu, pricing, and store expansion. She cannot rely on gut feeling.

* **The Internal Cash Registers, Inventory Logs, and POS Systems** → act as the *Internal Records Subsystem* (what we sold, what was wasted, who bought).
* **The Field Officers visiting local markets, competitors, and trade fairs** → act as the *Marketing Intelligence Subsystem* (what the market is whispering).
* **The hired External Agency conducting a "Customer Preference Survey for Millet-Based Dishes"** → acts as the *Marketing Research Subsystem* (deep, specific answers to a specific question).
* **The Computer Software running "What-If" scenarios on menu price changes** → acts as the *Marketing Decision Support Subsystem* (MDSS) (logical recommendation engine).

The orchestration of all four = **MKIS**. Without it, decisions are blind; with it, they are data-driven.

## 1.2 Market Research

### Formal Academic Definition
**Market Research** is the systematic, objective, and exhaustive process of gathering, recording, and analyzing data about a specific marketing problem or opportunity, leading to insights and recommendations for managerial action. It is a *subsystem* of the larger MKIS.

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Detective at Work"
If the restaurant owner suspects *"Why is the sales of Appam declining on Mondays?"*, he needs a **Market Researcher** (a detective). The detective:
1. Goes through past sales records (secondary data).
2. Interviews Monday customers (primary qualitative data).
3. Distributes structured questionnaires to 500 regulars (primary quantitative data).
4. Compares Appam prices with three competitor restaurants (competitive analysis).
5. Reports back: *"Sales drop on Mondays because of a competing breakfast offer at Café A, plus Monday's office crowd leaves for their hometowns."*

This *focused, problem-specific investigation* is **Market Research**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Distinction (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **MKIS = Continuous nervous system** (always running, broad, feeds all managers).
> - **Market Research = A specific medical test** (one-time or periodic, narrow, deep, answers a specific question).

## 1.3 Why These Concepts Are Critical in the Food Industry
| Driver | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Perishability** | Food items have a *short shelf life*. Market intel on real-time demand is non-negotiable. |
| **Consumer Health Awareness** | Shift toward organic, gluten-free, vegan demands constant tracking. |
| **FSSAI & Regulatory Pressure** | Labelling, traceability, and ingredient disclosure require precise data systems. |
| **Volatile Commodity Prices** | Turmeric, palm oil, sugar — prices swing daily. MIS must be live. |
| **Intense Local Competition** | Kerala's food market features unbranded players; differentiation data is vital. |
| **Seasonal Demand Cycles** | Onam, Vishu, Christmas — demand spikes need predictive research. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric for Sampling Confidence (Z-value):**
> - 90% confidence → **Z = 1.645**
> - 95% confidence → **Z = 1.96**
> - 99% confidence → **Z = 2.576**

These constants are mandatory for any sample-size calculation in market research problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Subsystems of an MKIS (KTU High-Yield Architecture)

### 1) Internal Records System
The most accessible and cost-effective data source. For a food firm, it includes:
* **Sales Records** (SKU-wise, region-wise, channel-wise).
* **Inventory & Stock Movement** (FIFO, FEFO tracking for perishables).
* **Cost Accounting** (cost per kg of finished product).
* **Customer Database** (loyalty cards, CRM entries).
* **Distribution & Logistics Data** (refrigerated truck turnaround, cold-chain breaks).

> [!NOTE]
> For a Kerala-based dairy processor, **Internal Records** would include daily milk procurement volumes, batch-wise fat/SNF tests, and the "first expiry first out" (FEFO) picklist — these are the lifeblood of operational decisions.

### 2) Marketing Intelligence System
Routine, informal, *outside-in* observation of the market environment. Sources:
* Reading trade publications (*Food Industry News India*).
* Talking to **suppliers, retailers, ex-employees** of competitors.
* Monitoring social media chatter and Google reviews.
* Attending exhibitions (Annapoorna, FoodTec).
* **Mystery shopping** (sending evaluators to competitor stores).

### 3) Marketing Research System
*Project-specific* deep dives (covered in Section 2.2 below).

### 4) Marketing Decision Support System (MDSS)
A computerized toolkit that uses **models, statistical software, and "what-if" simulations** to help managers structure problems and analyze data. Examples in food industry:
* **Linear Programming** models for product-mix optimization.
* **Regression models** predicting next month's rice consumption.
* **Conjoint analysis** for new flavoured-yoghurt design.

## 2.2 Types of Market Research (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Purpose | Food Industry Example | Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Exploratory Research** | Discover insights, define the problem. | "Why are sales of *kappa* (tapioca) chips flat?" | Focus groups, in-depth interviews, secondary data scan. |
| **Descriptive Research** | Describe market characteristics or functions. | "What % of urban Kerala consumers buy millet-based snacks monthly?" | Cross-sectional surveys, observational data, panel data. |
| **Causal Research (Experimental)** | Identify cause-effect relationships. | "Will a 10% price cut on ghee increase sales by more than 10%?" | Field experiments, A/B testing in retail stores. |
| **Predictive Research** | Forecast future market conditions. | "What will be the demand for frozen seafood in 2028?" | Time-series analysis, regression forecasting, Delphi method. |

## 2.3 The Marketing Research Process (Six Logical Steps)

1. **Define the Problem & Research Objectives** — *"Kerala consumers are rejecting our 'sugar-free' jam; why?"*
2. **Develop the Research Plan** — Choose exploratory, descriptive, or causal design; specify data sources.
3. **Collect the Information** — Primary (surveys, interviews) and/or secondary data.
4. **Analyze the Information** — Tabulate, apply statistical tests (chi-square, t-test, regression).
5. **Present the Findings** — Research report with executive summary, methodology, insights.
6. **Make the Marketing Decision** — Manager acts on findings.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (CRITICAL FOR NUMERICALS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — KTU frequently tests them in Module 3 numerical questions.**

| Formula | Use Case | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Cochran's Sample Size (proportion): $n_0 = \dfrac{Z^2 \, p \, q}{e^2}$ | Estimating required sample size when $p$ is known or assumed. | $Z$ = confidence Z-value; $p$ = est. proportion; $q = 1-p$; $e$ = margin of error. |
| Adjusted for finite population: $n = \dfrac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{(n_0 - 1)}{N}}$ | When population $N$ is small. | $N$ = total population; $n_0$ = initial Cochran estimate. |
| Sample size for the mean: $n = \left(\dfrac{Z \, \sigma}{E}\right)^2$ | When estimating an average (e.g., average monthly spend). | $\sigma$ = standard deviation; $E$ = tolerable error. |
| Market Share: $MS = \dfrac{\text{Company Sales}}{\text{Total Market Sales}} \times 100$ | Industry competition tracking. | Expressed as a percentage. |
| Relative Market Share: $RMS = \dfrac{\text{Brand Sales}}{\text{Largest Competitor's Sales}} \times 100$ | BCG matrix input. | Percentage. |
| Customer Satisfaction Index: $CSI = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Attribute Rating})}{\text{Max Possible Score}} \times 100$ | Composite satisfaction metric. | 0–100 scale. |
| Net Promoter Score: $NPS = \% \text{Promoters} - \% \text{Detractors}$ | Loyalty/word-of-mouth health. | Range $-100$ to $+100$. |
| Chi-square test statistic: $\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O - E)^2}{E}$ | Testing association between categorical variables (e.g., brand preference vs. age group). | $O$ = observed; $E$ = expected. |
| Margin of Error: $e = Z \, \sqrt{\dfrac{p \, q}{n}}$ | Designing the precision of an estimate. | Same as Cochran variables. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering/Industry Utility:** In a real **food processing company**, the Cochran formula decides the survey size for "How many consumers must we poll before launching our new cold-pressed coconut oil?" An under-sized sample → unreliable launch decision → commercial loss. An over-sized sample → wasteful expenditure. Cochran's formula provides the *exact* balance.

## 2.5 Sources of Data — Primary vs Secondary

| Aspect | Primary Data | Secondary Data |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Collected *fresh* for the current problem. | Already existing, gathered for *other* purposes. |
| Cost | High (time, money, manpower). | Low (publications, reports). |
| Time | Long lead time. | Quick to access. |
| Relevance | Highly specific to the problem. | May not perfectly fit the current question. |
| Food Industry Examples | Consumer taste-test of a new masala blend, in-store observation. | FSSAI annual reports, APEDA export data, NSSO consumption surveys. |

## 2.6 Research Instruments

* **Questionnaire** — structured set of questions (open-ended / closed-ended / Likert scale).
* **Interview schedule** — face-to-face or telephonic.
* **Observation checklist** — for in-store shopper behavior.
* **Mechanical instruments** — eye-trackers, neuromarketing tools (advanced).

## 2.7 Sampling Techniques (Most Tested KTU Sub-Topic)

* **Probability Sampling**:
  * *Simple Random Sampling* — every unit has equal chance.
  * *Stratified Sampling* — divide population into strata (e.g., age groups), sample within each.
  * *Systematic Sampling* — pick every $k$-th unit.
  * *Cluster Sampling* — select whole clusters (e.g., 5 districts of Kerala).
* **Non-Probability Sampling**:
  * *Convenience Sampling* — whoever is available.
  * *Judgmental / Purposive* — expert-driven.
  * *Snowball* — referrals lead to more respondents.
  * *Quota* — fixed category numbers.
