# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Cochran's Sample Size Formula

This derivation is a *guaranteed* KTU Module 3 numerical favorite. We derive it from first principles.

### Setup — The Underlying Statistical Principle
For a *finite or infinite* population where we wish to estimate a **proportion** $p$ (e.g., fraction of consumers preferring *organic rice*), the **confidence interval** for the true proportion $p$ based on sample proportion $\hat{p}$ is:

$$
\hat{p} \;\pm\; Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p \, q}{n}}
$$

where:
* $\hat{p}$ = sample proportion (point estimator of $p$).
* $Z_{\alpha/2}$ = the standard normal critical value (e.g., 1.96 at 95% confidence).
* $p \, q$ = variance of the Bernoulli proportion estimator ($q = 1 - p$).
* $n$ = sample size.

### Step 1 — Identify the Margin of Error Term
The half-width of the confidence interval is the **margin of error** $e$:

$$
e \;=\; Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p \, q}{n}}
$$

This represents the maximum tolerable deviation between the sample estimate $\hat{p}$ and the true population proportion $p$, at the chosen confidence level.

### Step 2 — Square Both Sides to Remove the Square Root
$$
e^2 \;=\; Z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot \frac{p \, q}{n}
$$

This isolates $n$ in a single rational expression.

### Step 3 — Cross-Multiply to Isolate $n$
$$
n \cdot e^2 \;=\; Z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot p \cdot q
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $n$ (Cochran's Initial Formula)
$$
n_0 \;=\; \frac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p \cdot q}{e^2}
$$

This is **Cochran's initial sample size formula** — used when the population is large (effectively infinite, e.g., $N \to \infty$).

### Step 5 — Apply Finite Population Correction (FPC)
When the population $N$ is *small* (e.g., only 600 retail outlets in Ernakulam), the sample cannot exceed $N$. We apply the FPC:

$$
n \;=\; \frac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{(n_0 - 1)}{N}}
$$

### Step 6 — Decide $p$ when Unknown
If $p$ is unknown (no prior estimate), use the **most conservative** value:

$$
p = 0.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p \cdot q = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25
$$

This maximizes the variance term and hence the required sample size — guaranteeing a *safe* estimate.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style, Step-by-Step)

> **Problem:** A Kerala-based organic food company wishes to estimate the proportion of urban consumers in Thiruvananthapuram who are willing to pay a premium for **cold-pressed coconut oil**. The city has **3,200 regular grocery buyers** of the brand. Past studies suggest $p = 0.40$. The management requires a **95% confidence level** with a **margin of error of 5%**. Determine the required sample size.

### Step A — Identify the Variables
* $Z = 1.96$ (for 95% confidence — **standard constant**)
* $p = 0.40$
* $q = 1 - p = 0.60$
* $e = 0.05$ (5% as a proportion)
* $N = 3{,}200$

### Step B — Apply Cochran's Initial Formula
$$
n_0 = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot q}{e^2} = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot (0.40) \cdot (0.60)}{(0.05)^2}
$$

### Step C — Compute Numerator
$$
(1.96)^2 = 3.8416
$$
$$
3.8416 \times 0.40 = 1.53664
$$
$$
1.53664 \times 0.60 = 0.921984
$$

### Step D — Compute Denominator
$$
(0.05)^2 = 0.0025
$$

### Step E — Divide
$$
n_0 = \frac{0.921984}{0.0025} = 368.7936
$$

Since sample size must be a whole number, **round up**:

$$
n_0 \approx 369
$$

### Step F — Apply Finite Population Correction
$$
n = \frac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{(n_0 - 1)}{N}} = \frac{369}{1 + \dfrac{369 - 1}{3{,}200}}
$$

### Step G — Compute the Inner Term
$$
\frac{368}{3{,}200} = 0.115
$$

### Step H — Add 1 and Divide
$$
n = \frac{369}{1 + 0.115} = \frac{369}{1.115} \approx 330.94
$$

### Step I — Round Up
$$
\boxed{n = 331 \text{ consumers}}
$$

### Step J — Managerial Interpretation
> The marketing team should survey **331 consumers** out of the 3,200 base to obtain a proportion estimate with $\pm 5\%$ accuracy at 95% confidence. The result is *internally valid* and statistically defensible for a board-level go/no-go decision on the cold-pressed coconut oil launch.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Research Type (Decision Tool)

| Research Trigger | Best-Fit Research Type | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Management says "Sales are dropping, but we don't know why." | Exploratory | Problem is fuzzy; we need hypothesis generation. |
| Management says "Find out how many consumers buy monthly." | Descriptive | We need a quantified snapshot. |
| Management says "Test if a price cut raises unit sales." | Causal | Need controlled manipulation. |
| Management says "Forecast Q3 demand for mango pulp." | Predictive | Time-series / regression. |
| Management says "Compare our brand perception vs. 3 competitors." | Descriptive (Comparative) | Cross-sectional, attribute-based. |

---

## 3.4 Sample Size for the Mean — Quick Derivation

For estimating a *mean* (e.g., average monthly spend ₹X on breakfast cereals):

### Step 1 — Confidence Interval for a Mean
$$
\bar{x} \;\pm\; Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

### Step 2 — Margin of Error
$$
E = Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

### Step 3 — Square
$$
E^2 = Z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot \frac{\sigma^2}{n}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $n$
$$
\boxed{n = \left( \frac{Z \, \sigma}{E} \right)^2}
$$

> **Example:** With $\sigma = 50$ rupees, $Z = 1.96$, $E = 5$ rupees:

$$
n = \left( \frac{1.96 \times 50}{5} \right)^2 = (19.6)^2 = 384.16 \;\Rightarrow\; n = 385
$$

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OEFTT724]`
**Define a Marketing Information System (MKIS). List its four major components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Marketing Information System (MKIS)** is a continuing and interacting structure of people, equipment, and procedures designed to gather, sort, analyse, evaluate, and distribute pertinent, timely, and accurate information to marketing decision-makers (1 mark).

**The four components are (1 mark each — naming 4 = 2 marks):**
1. **Internal Records System** — sales, inventory, cost, and customer data.
2. **Marketing Intelligence System** — continuous external observation of competitors, suppliers, and trade environment.
3. **Marketing Research System** — project-specific systematic studies.
4. **Marketing Decision Support System (MDSS)** — analytical tools, models, and software for decision-making.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, OEFTT724]`
**Differentiate between Market Research and Market Information System.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Tabular Form Recommended):**

| Parameter | Market Information System (MKIS) | Market Research |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** (1 mark) | Broad, continuous, ongoing. | Narrow, project-specific, often one-time. |
| **Purpose** (1 mark) | Supplies routine information for all marketing decisions. | Solves a specific research problem in depth. |
| **Time Frame** (0.5 mark) | Permanent feature of the organization. | Bounded by project duration. |
| **Output** (0.5 mark) | Continuous stream of marketing intelligence. | Specific report / recommendation for a defined issue. |

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern 2024 Scheme:** Each 14-mark question has **two sub-parts**, typically (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. You must answer **either Option A or Option B** in full.

---

### ✅ OPTION A — Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

#### (a) Explain the six-step Marketing Research Process in detail. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Define the Problem and Research Objectives (1 mark)**
The researcher meets management to convert the vague concern (e.g., "sales of our ready-to-eat *idiyappam* are dropping") into a sharp research question ("Is the decline driven by price, taste, or competitor entry?"). Clear objectives — *exploratory*, *descriptive*, or *causal* — are set.

**Step 2 — Develop the Research Plan (1 mark)**
This step specifies (i) data sources (primary, secondary, or both), (ii) research approach (survey, experiment, observation), (iii) research instrument (questionnaire, interview guide), (iv) sampling plan (technique + size), and (v) contact method (online, telephone, in-person).

**Step 3 — Collect the Information (1.5 marks)**
Fieldwork is executed. For a food company, this could mean deploying 10 enumerators to 50 supermarkets across Kerala to gather shelf-share and price data, while simultaneously running an online consumer survey via Google Forms.

**Step 4 — Analyse the Information (1.5 marks)**
Raw data is tabulated, cleaned, and subjected to statistical tests. Tools include cross-tabulation, chi-square test for independence, regression analysis, and conjoint analysis. Modern food firms use SPSS, R, or Python notebooks for deeper analytics.

**Step 5 — Present the Findings (1 mark)**
A formal research report is drafted with: executive summary, methodology, key insights, limitations, and recommendations. Effective presentations use visualisations (bar charts, heat-maps) so a non-technical marketing head can grasp the implications in minutes.

**Step 6 — Make the Marketing Decision (1 mark)**
Management uses the report to act. The decision may involve reformulating the recipe, repackaging, repricing, or withdrawing the SKU. Crucially, the **outcomes of the decision** must feed back into the MKIS for organisational learning.

---

#### (b) Discuss the various methods of data collection in market research, with at least one food-industry example for each. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Method | Description | Food Industry Example (1 mark each sub-point) |
|---|---|---|
| **Personal Interviews** (1 mark) | Face-to-face structured or semi-structured dialogue. | Surveying 200 housewives in Kochi about their breakfast cereal preferences and willingness-to-pay. |
| **Telephone Interviews** (1 mark) | Quick, geographically dispersed data gathering. | Calling 500 retailers across Kerala to check stock-out rates of a new *payasam* mix. |
| **Online / Email Surveys** (1 mark) | Low-cost, fast, large reach. | A Google Form sent to loyalty-card members of a baker chain, evaluating a new *kuzhalappam* flavour. |
| **Focus Groups** (1 mark) | 8–12 participants, moderator-led discussion. | Tasting session with 10 mothers in Thrissur to compare a new baby food's texture and aroma. |
| **Observation** (1 mark) | Watching actual behaviour, not stated. | Trained observers recording how long shoppers spend at the *chilli-powder* shelf in supermarkets. |
| **Experiments** (1 mark) | Manipulating one variable to see its effect. | Testing two price points (₹99 vs ₹119) of ghee in identical retail stores to measure sales response. |
| **Secondary Data** (1 mark) | Using existing reports. | Analysing APEDA export data and FSSAI compliance lists to understand the organic spice export opportunity. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Sub-Part b):**
> - Students often **list methods without food-industry examples** — this loses up to **3 marks**. Always tie to a *concrete food business scenario*.
> - Do **not** confuse "data source" (primary vs secondary) with "data collection method" (interview vs observation). They are different layers; the examiner checks for this.

---

### ✅ OPTION B — Question B `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

#### (a) Explain the four components of a Marketing Information System (MKIS) with suitable food-industry examples. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Component 1 — Internal Records System (1.75 marks)**
This is the company's *own* transactional database.
*Examples for a dairy cooperative in Kottayam:*
* Daily milk procurement volumes from 800 farmers.
* Batch-wise fat and SNF (Solid-Not-Fat) test logs.
* SKU-wise sales returns due to spoilage.
* Route-wise delivery time and refrigeration break-points.

**Component 2 — Marketing Intelligence System (1.75 marks)**
A *passive* system that gathers ongoing information about the external marketing environment.
*Examples:*
* Field officers visiting competitors' outlets to note shelf positions and prices.
* A junior executive attending the *Annapoorna Trade Fair* in Mumbai and summarising new snack launches.
* Setting up **Google Alerts** for the keywords "Kerala banana chips export."
* A dedicated WhatsApp group where salesmen share competitor activities weekly.

**Component 3 — Marketing Research System (1.75 marks)**
*Project-specific* studies to solve well-defined problems.
*Example:* A *kappa* (tapioca) chips manufacturer in Kollam commissions a formal **conjoint study** to determine the optimal combination of price, crispness, saltiness, and pack size that maximises consumer preference.

**Component 4 — Marketing Decision Support System (MDSS) (1.75 marks)**
Computerised tools that use models and statistical analysis to support decision-making.
*Examples:*
* **Linear Programming (LP)** model selecting the cheapest combination of raw materials meeting the nutritional spec of a protein bar.
* **Regression model** predicting ice-cream sales based on temperature and festival indicators.
* **Simulation model** answering "What if we raise the price of our cold-pressed oil by ₹20?"

---

#### (b) A Kerala-based spice brand wants to estimate the proportion of households in Thrissur that purchase branded *cardamom* powder. The brand's customer base in the city is approximately **8,500 households**. Past research indicates $p = 0.35$. The marketing team wants **99% confidence** with a **margin of error of 3%**. Calculate the required sample size, applying the finite population correction. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify Variables (1 mark)**
* $Z$ at 99% confidence = **2.576**
* $p = 0.35$, hence $q = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65$
* $e = 0.03$
* $N = 8{,}500$

**Step 2 — Apply Cochran's Initial Formula (1.5 marks)**
$$
n_0 = \frac{Z^2 \, p \, q}{e^2} = \frac{(2.576)^2 \times 0.35 \times 0.65}{(0.03)^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Numerator (1.5 marks)**
$$
(2.576)^2 = 6.635776
$$
$$
0.35 \times 0.65 = 0.2275
$$
$$
\text{Numerator} = 6.635776 \times 0.2275 = 1.509614
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Denominator (0.5 marks)**
$$
(0.03)^2 = 0.0009
$$

**Step 5 — Divide (0.5 marks)**
$$
n_0 = \frac{1.509614}{0.0009} \approx 1{,}677.35
$$

Round up:
$$
n_0 = 1{,}678
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Finite Population Correction (1.5 marks)**
$$
n = \frac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{(n_0 - 1)}{N}} = \frac{1{,}678}{1 + \dfrac{1{,}678 - 1}{8{,}500}}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute and Finalise (1 mark)**
$$
\frac{1{,}677}{8{,}500} \approx 0.1973
$$
$$
n = \frac{1{,}678}{1.1973} \approx 1{,}401.49
$$
$$
\boxed{n = 1{,}402 \text{ households}}
$$

> **Managerial Note:** The team must survey **1,402 households** out of 8,500 to estimate the brand-cardamom purchase proportion within $\pm 3\%$ accuracy at 99% confidence — a robust, board-defensible figure for a market-entry decision.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Sub-Part b):**
> - **Z-value error:** Students often use $Z = 1.96$ (95%) when the question states 99% — this single substitution drops **0.5 marks**.
> - **Forgetting FPC:** If the question mentions a finite population, the examiner **expects** the correction. Skipping it loses **0.5 to 1 mark**.
> - **Rounding direction:** Always **round UP** the sample size. Using 1,401 instead of 1,402 sacrifices the required precision and is marked wrong.
> - **Unit slip:** The answer must be in *households*, not rupees or packets.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this checklist for the night before the exam.**

- **MKIS** is a *continuous* system; **Market Research** is a *project-specific* subsystem. Always state the distinction first when asked.
- The **four MKIS components** are: *Internal Records, Marketing Intelligence, Marketing Research, MDSS*. Memorise the spelling and order.
- **Three primary research types** for descriptive exams: *Exploratory* (fuzzy problem), *Descriptive* (quantify), *Causal* (cause-effect). Add *Predictive* for full marks.
- **Six-step research process** = *Problem definition → Plan → Collect → Analyse → Report → Decision*. Use the same order in answer sheets.
- **Cochran's formula** for proportions: $n_0 = Z^2 p q / e^2$. Master it cold.
- **Finite Population Correction (FPC)** = $n = n_0 / \left[1 + (n_0 - 1)/N \right]$ — apply *only* when the question gives a finite $N$.
- **Z-value memory table:** 90% → 1.645, 95% → 1.96, 99% → 2.576. These are *constants* and **must not be calculated from scratch**.
- **Always round sample size UP** — never down.
- **Primary vs Secondary data** — Primary = freshly collected; Secondary = pre-existing. The KTU examiner often asks for examples in *food* context, so always cite FSSAI, APEDA, NSSO, or industry-association data for secondary.
- **Sampling techniques** — Four probability methods (Random, Stratified, Systematic, Cluster) + four non-probability (Convenience, Judgmental, Snowball, Quota). Know one example for each.
- **Marketing Decision Support System (MDSS)** — uses *models and software* (LP, regression, conjoint). The phrase "what-if analysis" is a guaranteed KTU key phrase.
- **Feedback loop** in MKIS — outcomes of marketing decisions must re-enter the Internal Records subsystem for continuous organisational learning. Drawing the loop in Mermaid or block form earns bonus marks.
- **Food-industry relevance** — Always anchor your answers to *food* examples (FSSAI compliance, cold-chain, perishability, Kerala-specific markets like *kappa*, *payasam*, *chilli powder*). Generic answers lose marks; contextual answers earn distinction grades.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Management of Distribution Channel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT724 - FOOD INDUSTRY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Management of Distribution Channel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Distribution Channel Management** is the systematic process of designing, organizing, directing, and controlling the flow of food products and services from the point of production (manufacturer/processor) to the point of consumption (end consumer/industrial user), while simultaneously managing the relationships, transactions, and physical logistics among all intermediary entities involved in this flow.

In the context of the **Food Industry**, a distribution channel constitutes a **set of interdependent organizations (intermediaries)** that participate in making a food product (raw, semi-processed, or finished) available for consumption or use by industrial buyers, institutional consumers, or retail customers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Check:** The syllabus defines distribution channel management as a coordinated system of **intermediaries** such as wholesalers, distributors, retailers, agents, brokers, and logistics providers that facilitate the **physical, title, information, and promotional flow** of food commodities through the supply pipeline.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (Glossary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Channel of Distribution** | The path traced by the title of goods as it moves from producer to ultimate consumer. |
| **Channel Members** | The participants in the distribution chain (producers, wholesalers, retailers, agents). |
| **Channel Length** | The number of intermediary levels between the producer and the consumer. |
| **Channel Width** | The number of intermediaries at each level of the distribution chain. |
| **Channel Conflict** | Disagreement among channel members over goals, territories, or rewards. |
| **Channel Power** | The ability of one channel member to influence the decision-making of another. |
| **Vertical Marketing System (VMS)** | A distribution structure in which producers, wholesalers, and retailers act as a unified system. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Reliable Tea Stall" Analogy 🍵**
> 
> Imagine you are a tea plantation owner in Munnar, Kerala. You grow the tea leaves, but you obviously cannot hand-deliver every cup of tea to every customer in India. So you need a chain:
> 
> **You (Plantation)** → **Processing Factory (with you)** → **Wholesale Distributor** → **Retail Tea Stall (Kerala/Karnataka)** → **Customer drinking chai at 6 AM** ☕
> 
> In this journey:
> - The **tea leaves** are the *physical product*.
> - The **title (ownership)** of tea passes from you → wholesaler → retailer → consumer.
> - The **money** flows in the reverse direction (customer → retailer → wholesaler → you).
> - The **information** (demand, feedback, trends) flows in both directions.
> - The **promotion** (advertisements, offers) is also managed.
> 
> **Channel Management** is the *skill of organizing this entire route map efficiently* — choosing the right number of stops, picking the best partner stalls, ensuring no one fights with each other, and making sure the chai reaches the customer hot, fresh, and on time!

### 1.4 Importance of Distribution Channel Management in the Food Industry

The food industry is uniquely complex because:
- **Perishability** of products (e.g., milk, meat, bakery)
- **Cold chain logistics** requirements
- **Strict regulatory compliance** (FSSAI, FDA, HACCP)
- **Seasonal demand fluctuations**
- **Geographic dispersion** of agricultural production vs. urban consumption
- **High SKU (Stock Keeping Unit) variety** in retail

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Approximately **30% of the final retail price** of food products in India is attributable to distribution and logistics costs, making channel optimization a **critical profit driver** for food businesses.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Structure of Distribution Channels

A distribution channel is not a single entity but a **multi-layered structure**. Let us analyze each layer in detail.

#### 2.1.1 Levels of Distribution Channels (Channel Length)

The number of intermediary levels defines the **channel length**.

**A. Zero-Level Channel (Direct Channel)**
$$\text{Producer} \rightarrow \text{Consumer}$$
- No intermediaries.
- Examples: Farm-to-table vegetable sales, Amul's direct dairy outlets, online food brands selling through own website.
- **Best for:** Perishable, high-value, customizable food products.

**B. One-Level Channel**
$$\text{Producer} \rightarrow \text{Retailer} \rightarrow \text{Consumer}$$
- One intermediary (retailer).
- Examples: ITC selling biscuits through Big Bazaar, Nestlé products through supermarket chains.

**C. Two-Level Channel**
$$\text{Producer} \rightarrow \text{Wholesaler} \rightarrow \text{Retailer} \rightarrow \text{Consumer}$$
- Two intermediaries.
- Most common in the traditional Indian food market (kirana stores).

**D. Three-Level Channel**
$$\text{Producer} \rightarrow \text{Agent} \rightarrow \text{Wholesaler} \rightarrow \text{Retailer} \rightarrow \text{Consumer}$$
- Three intermediaries, often involving a commission agent (e.g., mandi system for grains, spices).

#### 2.1.2 Intensity of Distribution (Channel Width)

| Distribution Type | Coverage | Best For | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Intensive Distribution** | Maximum retail outlets | Convenience goods | Salt, bread, soft drinks |
| **Selective Distribution** | Limited but quality outlets | Shopping goods | Premium coffee, organic food |
| **Exclusive Distribution** | Single outlet in territory | Specialty goods | Imported chocolates, wines |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** "**I-S-E**" → **I**ntensive = everywhere; **S**elective = chosen few; **E**xclusive = one-of-a-kind.

### 2.2 Functions Performed by Channel Members

A distribution channel performs **9 key functions** that create utility:

1. **Sorting** – Grouping food products by size, grade, or quality.
2. **Accumulation** – Collecting small lots from many farmers into large marketable quantities.
3. **Assorting** – Combining varied products to provide a one-stop assortment.
4. **Risk-bearing** – Accepting spoilage, theft, and demand uncertainty.
5. **Physical distribution** – Warehousing, transportation, cold chain.
6. **Facilitating** – Financing, market information, scale economies.
7. **Promotional** – Display, advertising, in-store demonstrations.
8. **Negotiating** – Pricing, terms of trade, contract conditions.
9. **Information sharing** – Demand signals, consumer feedback, market intelligence.

### 2.3 Channel Management Process (Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7-Step Channel Management Framework (KTU High-Yield)**

1. **Analyzing Consumer Needs** – What does the end-customer want? (Freshness, price, packaging, convenience).
2. **Establishing Objectives** – Cost, speed, coverage, control, customer experience.
3. **Identifying Channel Alternatives** – Direct, indirect, hybrid, digital.
4. **Evaluating Channel Alternatives** – Economic, control, and adaptive criteria.
5. **Selecting Channel Members** – Vetting, qualifying, contracting.
6. **Motivating Channel Members** – Margin, incentives, training, support.
7. **Evaluating Channel Performance** – Sales, margins, stockouts, customer satisfaction.

### 2.4 Types of Vertical Marketing Systems (VMS)

A **Vertical Marketing System (VMS)** is a centrally coordinated channel structure.

| Type | Description | Food Industry Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Corporate VMS** | Single firm owns the entire channel | Tata's integrated agri-business |
| **Contractual VMS** | Independent firms join by contract | Amul Cooperative Model (GCMMF) |
| **Administered VMS** | One dominant member coordinates | Big retailers dictating terms to suppliers |

### 2.5 Channel Conflict Management

Conflicts arise when channel members disagree. Three main types:

- **Vertical Conflict** – Between different levels (e.g., manufacturer vs. retailer).
- **Horizontal Conflict** – Between members at the same level (e.g., two retailers).
- **Multichannel Conflict** – Between different channel types (e.g., online vs. offline).

**Resolution Strategies:**
1. **Super-ordinate goals** – Aligning everyone to a common mission.
2. **Co-optation** – Involving rivals in committees.
3. **Mediation** – Using neutral third-party arbitrators.
4. **Joint planning** – Co-developing plans.
5. **Trade allowances** – Financial incentives to reduce conflict.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Channel Cost Ratio (CCR)** | $CCR = \dfrac{TC}{TP} \times 100$ | $TC$ = Total Channel Cost; $TP$ = Total Sales Revenue | % |
| **Distribution Coverage** | $DC = \dfrac{RO}{TR} \times 100$ | $RO$ = Retailers Outlined; $TR$ = Total Retailers in Territory | % |
| **Stockout Frequency** | $SOF = \dfrac{DO}{TO} \times 100$ | $DO$ = Days Out-of-Stock; $TO$ = Total Operating Days | % |
| **Wholesaler Margin** | $WM = SP - CP$ | $SP$ = Selling Price; $CP$ = Cost Price | ₹/unit |
| **Retailer Mark-up %** | $MU\% = \dfrac{RP - CP}{CP} \times 100$ | $RP$ = Retail Price; $CP$ = Cost to Retailer | % |
| **Channel Profit Margin** | $CPM = \dfrac{NP}{SP} \times 100$ | $NP$ = Net Profit per unit; $SP$ = Selling Price | % |
| **Break-Even Volume** | $BEV = \dfrac{FC}{SP - VC}$ | $FC$ = Fixed Cost; $VC$ = Variable Cost per unit | units |
| **Order Fulfillment Rate** | $OFR = \dfrac{FO}{TO} \times 100$ | $FO$ = Orders Fulfilled on time; $TO$ = Total Orders | % |
| **Inventory Turnover** | $IT = \dfrac{COGS}{AI}$ | $COGS$ = Cost of Goods Sold; $AI$ = Average Inventory | times/year |
| **Lead Time Variance** | $LTV = \dfrac{\vert ACT - PLT \vert}{PLT} \times 100$ | $ACT$ = Actual Lead Time; $PLT$ = Planned Lead Time | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule:** In all percentage-based channel KPIs, the result must lie between **0% and 100%**. If computed value is negative, recalculate — the inputs may be reversed.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering/Industry Utility

In the **food industry**, distribution channel management is a **multi-million-rupee decision** that affects:
- **Shelf-life management** (cold chain integrity)
- **Working capital** (inventory holding vs. stockouts)
- **Brand equity** (consistency of consumer experience)
- **Carbon footprint** (efficient logistics reduces emissions)
- **Food safety compliance** (FSSAI traceability requirements)

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** Brands like **Amul, Nestlé India, and Britannia** are renowned case studies in efficient distribution channel management — Amul alone uses a **3-tier cooperative model** reaching over **10 million milk producers** and millions of consumers daily.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Framework: The Channel Selection Decision Matrix

In the absence of complex physics derivations, management decisions in distribution channels rely on **analytical decision matrices**. Let us derive a **Channel Profitability Comparison Model**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **quantitative KTU-style problem** frequently asked in ESE — adapted from the **PEB (Producer-Enterprise-Buyer) Margin Analysis Model** used in agribusiness.

---

### 3.2 Problem Setup (Worked Example)

A food processing company in Kerala produces **banana chips** and has three channel alternatives:

- **Channel A (Direct to Consumer):** Company → Consumer (Online + Outlet)
- **Channel B (Through Retailer):** Company → Retailer → Consumer
- **Channel C (Two-Stage):** Company → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Channel A | Channel B | Channel C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Selling Price (₹/kg) | 400 | 400 | 400 |
| Production Cost (₹/kg) | 180 | 180 | 180 |
| Wholesaler Margin (₹/kg) | — | — | 30 |
| Retailer Margin (₹/kg) | — | 50 | 50 |
| Distribution Cost (₹/kg) | 40 | 25 | 20 |
| Promotional Cost (₹/kg) | 30 | 20 | 15 |
| Expected Volume (kg/yr) | 5,000 | 20,000 | 50,000 |

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Solution Derivation

#### Step 1: Compute Net Price Received by the Producer (NP) for Each Channel

$$\text{For Channel A: } NP_A = SP - (DM_{retailer} + DM_{wholesaler}) - DC - PC$$

$$NP_A = 400 - 0 - 40 - 30 = ₹330/\text{kg}$$

$$\text{For Channel B: } NP_B = 400 - 50 - 25 - 20 = ₹305/\text{kg}$$

$$\text{For Channel C: } NP_C = 400 - 50 - 30 - 20 - 15 = ₹285/\text{kg}$$

**Logic:** Net Price = Selling Price minus all deductions to channel members and operational costs.

#### Step 2: Compute Net Profit per kg for the Producer

$$\text{Net Profit per kg} = NP - \text{Production Cost}$$

$$\text{For Channel A: } P_A = 330 - 180 = ₹150/\text{kg}$$

$$\text{For Channel B: } P_B = 305 - 180 = ₹125/\text{kg}$$

$$\text{For Channel C: } P_C = 285 - 180 = ₹105/\text{kg}$$

#### Step 3: Compute Total Annual Profit for Each Channel

$$\text{Total Profit} = \text{Profit per kg} \times \text{Annual Volume}$$

$$\text{Channel A: } TA = 150 \times 5{,}000 = ₹7{,}50{,}000$$

$$\text{Channel B: } TB = 125 \times 20{,}000 = ₹25{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Channel C: } TC = 105 \times 50{,}000 = ₹52{,}50{,}000$$

#### Step 4: Compute the Channel Profit Margin (CPM)

$$CPM = \frac{\text{Net Profit per kg}}{SP} \times 100$$

$$\text{Channel A: } CPM_A = \frac{150}{400} \times 100 = 37.50\%$$

$$\text{Channel B: } CPM_B = \frac{125}{400} \times 100 = 31.25\%$$

$$\text{Channel C: } CPM_C = \frac{105}{400} \times 100 = 26.25\%$$

#### Step 5: Decision Matrix Summary

| Metric | Channel A | Channel B | Channel C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Net Price to Producer (₹/kg) | 330 | 305 | 285 |
| Profit per kg (₹/kg) | 150 | 125 | 105 |
| Annual Volume (kg) | 5,000 | 20,000 | 50,000 |
| **Total Annual Profit (₹)** | **7,50,000** | **25,00,000** | **52,50,000** |
| Profit Margin (%) | 37.50 | 31.25 | 26.25 |

#### Step 6: Strategic Decision

$$\text{Channel C generates the highest absolute profit, despite the lowest per-unit margin}$$

$$\frac{TC - TA}{TA} \times 100 = \frac{52{,}50{,}000 - 7{,}50{,}000}{7{,}50{,}000} \times 100 = 600\%$$

**Conclusion:** Channel C is **600% more profitable** in absolute terms. However, the producer must consider **loss of customer control, longer feedback time, and brand dilution risk** in indirect channels.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Channel Selection Criteria

| Criterion | Weight | Channel A Score | Channel B Score | Channel C Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Profitability | 25% | 9 | 8 | 10 |
| Market Coverage | 20% | 3 | 7 | 10 |
| Customer Control | 15% | 10 | 6 | 3 |
| Brand Image | 10% | 9 | 7 | 5 |
| Speed of Delivery | 10% | 9 | 7 | 5 |
| Cost of Operation | 10% | 5 | 7 | 9 |
| Feedback Speed | 10% | 10 | 6 | 4 |
| **Weighted Total** | 100% | **7.85** | **7.05** | **6.85** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** Although Channel C gives maximum profit, **Channel A** scores highest in the weighted matrix when **control and brand** are heavily weighted. This is why **premium food brands** like organic honey or specialty coffee opt for **direct channels**, while **mass-market packaged foods** (chips, biscuits) use long channels.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Distribution Channel Structure: Food Industry Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PROD["A: PRODUCER (Farmer/Manufacturer)"]
    PROC["B: PROCESSOR (Food Industry Plant)"]
    WHOL["C: WHOLESALER (Distributor)"]
    RETL["D: RETAILER (Supermarket/Kirana)"]
    INST["E: INSTITUTIONAL BUYER (Hotels/Hospitals)"]
    CONS["F: CONSUMER (End User)"]
    DIGI["G: DIGITAL CHANNEL (E-commerce Platform)"]
    AGNT["H: AGENT or BROKER (Commission Agent)"]

    PROD --> PROC
    PROC --> WHOL
    PROC --> INST
    WHOL --> RETL
    WHOL --> AGNT
    AGNT --> RETL
    PROC --> DIGI
    DIGI --> CONS
    RETL --> CONS
    INST --> CONS

    classDef critical fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#d2691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outlet fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class PROD,PROC,WHOL critical
    class RETL,INST,DIGI,AGNT,CONS outlet
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This Mermaid architecture maps the **multi-tier flow** of food products. The **orange nodes (A, B, C)** represent upstream decision-makers; the **blue nodes (D, E, F, G, H)** represent downstream outlets. The diagram shows that a single producer can route to multiple channel endpoints.

---

### 4.2 Channel Management Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Step 1: Identify Target Customer Segment"]
    A2["Step 2: Set Distribution Objectives"]
    A3["Step 3: Identify Channel Alternatives"]
    A4["Step 4: Evaluate Alternatives on Cost, Control, Coverage"]
    A5["Step 5: Select Optimal Channel Mix"]
    A6["Step 6: Recruit and Qualify Channel Partners"]
    A7["Step 7: Motivate, Train, and Support"]
    A8["Step 8: Monitor Performance and Resolve Conflicts"]
    A9["Step 9: Periodic Review and Modification"]

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8 --> A9
    A9 -.feedback.-> A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous Loop:** Distribution channel management is **not a one-time activity** — the feedback arrow from Step 9 to Step 1 emphasizes the **continuous, iterative nature** of strategic channel decisions.

---

### 4.3 Vertical Marketing System (VMS) Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CORP["Corporate VMS: Single Owner"]
        C1["Manufacturing"]
        C2["Wholesale"]
        C3["Retail"]
        C1 --> C2 --> C3
    end

    subgraph CONT["Contractual VMS: Cooperative or Franchise"]
        T1["Independent Manufacturer"]
        T2["Independent Wholesaler"]
        T3["Independent Retailer"]
        T1 --> T2 --> T3
        T1 -.contract.-> T2
        T2 -.contract.-> T3
    end

    subgraph ADMIN["Administered VMS: Power Leader"]
        P1["Dominant Retailer (e.g., Reliance Fresh)"]
        P2["Manufacturer A"]
        P3["Manufacturer B"]
        P4["Manufacturer C"]
        P1 --- P2
        P1 --- P3
        P1 --- P4
    end

    classDef corp fill:#ffdab9,stroke:#8b4513
    classDef cont fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4
    classDef admin fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#800080
    class C1,C2,C3 corp
    class T1,T2,T3 cont
    class P1,P2,P3,P4 admin
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Insight:** The diagram contrasts the three VMS types — note that in the **Contractual VMS**, dashed lines represent legal agreements, while in the **Administered VMS**, the dominant retailer (P1) is the **hub** connecting to multiple manufacturers without owning them.

---

### 4.4 Channel Conflict Resolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1["Conflict Identified"]
    C2["Type: Vertical or Horizontal or Multichannel"]
    C3{"Severity Assessment"}
    C4["Minor Issue"]
    C5["Major Issue"]
    C6["Diplomatic Dialogue or Joint Planning"]
    C7["Trade Allowances or Co-optation"]
    C8["Mediation or Arbitration"]
    C9["Legal Action or Channel Restructuring"]
    C10["Resolution Implemented"]
    C11["Follow-up and Review"]

    C1 --> C2 --> C3
    C3 -->|Low| C4 --> C6
    C3 -->|Medium| C5 --> C7
    C3 -->|High| C5 --> C8 --> C9
    C6 --> C10
    C7 --> C10
    C9 --> C10 --> C11
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Takeaway:** Most channel conflicts (≈70%) are resolved at the **C6 (Joint Planning)** stage. Legal action is the **last resort** as it damages business relationships permanently.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define the term **"Distribution Channel"** in the context of the food industry. Mention any **four** functions performed by channel members.

**Model Answer:**
A distribution channel is a set of interdependent organizations (intermediaries) involved in making a food product or service available for use or consumption by industrial buyers, institutional customers, or end consumers.

**Four Key Functions:**
1. **Physical distribution** — transportation, warehousing, and cold chain handling.
2. **Risk-bearing** — accepting spoilage, theft, and demand fluctuation risks.
3. **Sorting and accumulation** — consolidating small farm produce into marketable lots.
4. **Information sharing** — providing market intelligence, demand signals, and consumer feedback to producers.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between **Intensive, Selective, and Exclusive Distribution** strategies with suitable food industry examples.

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Coverage | When to Use | Food Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Intensive** | Maximum outlets | Convenience goods, mass-market | Tata Salt, Amul Butter |
| **Selective** | Quality, few outlets | Shopping goods, brand image | Organic India products, specialty coffee |
| **Exclusive** | One outlet per area | Specialty/premium goods | Imported Belgian chocolates |

**Key Distinction:** Intensive distribution maximizes reach, while exclusive distribution maximizes brand prestige and price control.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 3, Q7(a) or Q7(b)]**

---

#### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **various levels of distribution channels** used in the food industry, with the help of a suitable block diagram. Mention the **advantages and disadvantages** of direct (zero-level) channels.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A food processing company sells a product at ₹500/kg. Production cost is ₹200/kg. Under **Channel X**, retailer margin is ₹60/kg, wholesaler margin is ₹40/kg, and distribution cost is ₹30/kg. Under **Channel Y** (direct), distribution cost is ₹80/kg. Expected annual volume: Channel X = 40,000 kg; Channel Y = 8,000 kg. **Calculate the net profit per kg, total annual profit, and profit margin (%)** for both channels. **Which channel is more profitable in absolute terms?**

---

**Model Solution (A-a):**

**Levels of Distribution Channels:**

1. **Zero-Level Channel:** Producer → Consumer (Direct).
2. **One-Level Channel:** Producer → Retailer → Consumer.
3. **Two-Level Channel:** Producer → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer.
4. **Three-Level Channel:** Producer → Agent → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer.

*Block Diagram Representation:*
A textual representation is given below for clarity:
```
Level 0:    [Producer] ----> [Consumer]
Level 1:    [Producer] ----> [Retailer] ----> [Consumer]
Level 2:    [Producer] ----> [Wholesaler] ----> [Retailer] ----> [Consumer]
Level 3:    [Producer] ----> [Agent] ----> [Wholesaler] ----> [Retailer] ----> [Consumer]
```

**Advantages of Direct (Zero-Level) Channel:**
- Higher profit margin per unit (no middleman).
- Direct customer feedback and relationship.
- Better control over branding and pricing.
- Faster product updates and customization.

**Disadvantages of Direct Channel:**
- High distribution and logistics cost.
- Limited market reach.
- High inventory burden on producer.
- Difficulty in scaling operations.

---

**Model Solution (A-b):**

**Step 1: Net Price Received by Producer**

For Channel X (indirect):
$$NP_X = SP - (WM + RM) - DC$$
$$NP_X = 500 - (40 + 60) - 30 = ₹370/\text{kg}$$

For Channel Y (direct):
$$NP_Y = 500 - 80 = ₹420/\text{kg}$$

**Step 2: Net Profit per kg**

$$\text{Channel X: } P_X = 370 - 200 = ₹170/\text{kg}$$

$$\text{Channel Y: } P_Y = 420 - 200 = ₹220/\text{kg}$$

**Step 3: Total Annual Profit**

$$\text{Channel X: } T_X = 170 \times 40{,}000 = ₹68{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Channel Y: } T_Y = 220 \times 8{,}000 = ₹17{,}60{,}000$$

**Step 4: Profit Margin (%)**

$$\text{Channel X: } CPM_X = \frac{170}{500} \times 100 = 34.00\%$$

$$\text{Channel Y: } CPM_Y = \frac{220}{500} \times 100 = 44.00\%$$

**Conclusion:** **Channel X is more profitable in absolute terms** (₹68,00,000 vs. ₹17,60,000), yielding approximately **3.86 times** the absolute profit of Channel Y, despite a lower per-unit margin. **Channel Y is preferable when brand control and customer intimacy outweigh volume profits.**

---

#### ❓ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the concept of **Vertical Marketing Systems (VMS)** in the food industry. Compare **Corporate, Contractual, and Administered VMS** with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is **channel conflict**? Explain the **types of channel conflicts** and the **strategies for resolving** them in the food industry context.

---

**Model Solution (B-a):**

A **Vertical Marketing System (VMS)** is a centrally coordinated and professionally managed distribution channel structure where the producer, wholesaler, and retailer act as a unified system to achieve greater economies, control, and market impact.

**Comparison of VMS Types:**

| Type | Description | Control | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Corporate VMS** | One firm owns and controls all channel levels | Maximum | Tata Group's food business |
| **Contractual VMS** | Independent firms join via legal contracts | Moderate | Amul Cooperative (GCMMF) |
| **Administered VMS** | Dominant firm coordinates through size/power | Implicit | Reliance Fresh dictating terms to suppliers |

**Food Industry Examples:**
- **Corporate VMS:** Tata Sampann integrated wheat-to-retail chain.
- **Contractual VMS:** Amul's three-tier cooperative model (village society → district union → state federation).
- **Administered VMS:** Big retailers like D-Mart, Reliance Fresh controlling brands through shelf allocation and pricing power.

**Advantages of VMS:**
- Reduced channel conflict.
- Economies of scale in logistics.
- Better consumer data flow.
- Stronger brand consistency.

---

**Model Solution (B-b):**

**Channel Conflict Definition:**
Channel conflict is a **disagreement or tension** between channel members (producers, wholesalers, retailers, agents) arising from incompatible goals, territorial disputes, pricing differences, or role ambiguity.

**Types of Channel Conflicts:**

1. **Vertical Conflict:** Between different channel levels (e.g., manufacturer vs. retailer over margins or shelf space).
2. **Horizontal Conflict:** Between channel members at the same level (e.g., two supermarkets competing for the same customers).
3. **Multichannel Conflict:** Between different channel types (e.g., online vs. offline retail of the same brand).

**Resolution Strategies:**

| Strategy | Application |
|---|---|
| **Super-ordinate Goals** | Establishing a common mission (e.g., "grow category together") |
| **Trade Allowances** | Financial incentives to align behavior |
| **Joint Planning** | Co-developing sales, marketing, inventory plans |
| **Co-optation** | Including conflicting members in advisory committees |
| **Mediation/Arbitration** | Neutral third-party intervention |
| **Channel Restructuring** | Modifying channel design as a last resort |

**Food Industry Application:**
A common conflict is between **kirana stores and modern retail (supermarkets)**. Resolution may involve product differentiation (e.g., different SKUs, different pack sizes) and trade margin realignment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** ⚠️
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Channel Length" with "Channel Width":** Length = number of levels; Width = number of intermediaries at each level. Many students mix these up — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract ALL deductions** in the Net Price calculation (Production cost, distribution cost, promotional cost, margins) — a common arithmetic error.
> 3. **Writing "VMS" without expanding** the acronym — always write "Vertical Marketing System" first.
> 4. **Forgetting the word "Conflict Resolution Strategies"** are asked separately from "Types of Conflict" — mixing them loses structure marks.
> 5. **Not stating units (₹/kg or %)** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram** in 7-mark theory questions — a block diagram is **mandatory** for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Management of Distribution Channel**

- ✅ **Distribution Channel** = Path traced by title of goods from producer to consumer.
- ✅ **Three flow types:** Physical flow (forward), Money flow (backward), Information flow (bidirectional).
- ✅ **Channel Length:** Number of intermediary levels (0 to 3+).
- ✅ **Channel Width (Intensity):** Intensive (max), Selective (limited), Exclusive (one).
- ✅ **9 Key Channel Functions:** Sorting, accumulation, assorting, risk-bearing, physical distribution, facilitating, promoting, negotiating, information sharing.
- ✅ **Three VMS Types:** Corporate (ownership), Contractual (agreements), Administered (power).
- ✅ **Three Channel Conflicts:** Vertical, Horizontal, Multichannel.
- ✅ **6 Resolution Strategies:** Super-ordinate goals, trade allowances, co-optation, mediation, joint planning, restructuring.
- ✅ **Key Formulas:** $CPM = \frac{NP}{SP} \times 100$, $CCR = \frac{TC}{TP} \times 100$, $IT = \frac{COGS}{AI}$, $OFR = \frac{FO}{TO} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Critical Insight:** Maximum absolute profit ≠ Maximum profit margin. Always analyze both.
- ✅ **Indian Context:** Mandi system = Three-level channel with commission agents.
- ✅ **Modern Trend:** E-commerce has introduced a **Digital Direct Channel**, disrupting traditional structures.
- ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always draw a **block diagram** in 7-mark theory answers for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Consumer Buying Behaviour and Factors Influencing It
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT724 - FOOD INDUSTRY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Consumer Buying Behaviour and Factors Influencing It

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Consumer Buying Behaviour (CBB)** is formally defined as the **study of individuals, groups, or organizations and the processes they use to select, secure, use, and dispose of products, services, experiences, or ideas to satisfy needs and the impacts that these processes have on the consumer and society**.

In the specific context of **Food Industry Management**, it refers to the behavioural, psychological, and socio-economic processes through which end-consumers evaluate, purchase, consume, and form post-consumption attitudes toward food products — including packaged foods, fresh produce, functional foods, organic foods, beverages, and food-service offerings.

$$ CBB = f(Cultural, Social, Personal, Psychological, Situational\ Factors) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The consumer is treated as the **central decision unit** in the food value chain. Understanding *why* a consumer buys (motivation), *what* they buy (perception), and *how often* they repurchase (loyalty) forms the **marketing intelligence backbone** of any food enterprise — from a small bakery to a multinational FMCG giant like Nestlé or ITC Foods.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Menu Intuition"

Imagine you walk into a new restaurant in Kochi. You don't randomly point at the menu. Instead:

1. **Your brain first recognizes hunger** (need arousal).
2. **You scan the menu, recall past meals, ask the waiter, or read Google reviews** (information search).
3. **You compare the butter chicken vs. the paneer tikka vs. the veg biryani** (evaluation of alternatives).
4. **You finally order the biryani** (purchase decision).
5. **After eating, you either love it (satisfaction → repeat purchase) or dislike it (dissonance → complaint or switch)** (post-purchase behaviour).

This *exact* five-step mental sequence — formalised by **Engel, Blackwell & Kollat (1968)** as the **EKB Model** — is what marketing managers in food companies spend crores studying. The factors that *biased* your choice (your Kerala upbringing, your family eating rice daily, your friend's Instagram post, your mood, your budget) are precisely the **influencing factors** we will dissect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for a Food Industry Manager:** A KTU graduate entering roles in product development, brand management, retail buying, or food marketing must be able to answer: *"Will my target consumer pick my new millet-based snack over a competitor's?"* — and the answer lies entirely within Consumer Buying Behaviour theory.

## 1.3 Core Constructs at a Glance

| Construct | One-line Definition |
|---|---|
| **Need** | A state of felt deprivation (physiological, psychological). |
| **Want** | The specific form a need takes, shaped by culture and personality. |
| **Demand** | Wants backed by purchasing power. |
| **Stimulus** | Any internal or external cue triggering a behavioural response. |
| **Response** | The consumer's reaction (purchase, rejection, postponement). |
| **Loyalty** | A deeply held commitment to repurchase a preferred product consistently. |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the Four Influencing Factor Clusters:** **C-S-P-P** → **C**ulture, **S**ocial, **P**ersonal, **P**sychological. Kotler's 4-factor framework is the single highest-weightage topic in KTU exams from this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Consumer Decision-Making Process (5-Stage EKB Model)

The decision journey is **not always linear** — habitual purchases (e.g., buying your regular Tata Salt) skip stages 2 and 3. Marketers map each stage to design interventions.

### Stage 1 — Need Recognition
- Triggered by **internal stimuli** (hunger, thirst) or **external stimuli** (an advertisement, smell from a bakery, friend showing off a new snack).
- For food products, **physiological hunger drives dominate**, making this stage almost involuntary.

### Stage 2 — Information Search
Two sub-types:
- **Internal Search:** Recalling past experiences (memory-based).
- **External Search:** Seeking info from family, online reviews, social media influencers, in-store trials.
- **Food-specific channels:** Cookery shows (MasterChef India), YouTube reviews, Swiggy/Zomato ratings, WhatsApp forwards about "health foods".

### Stage 3 — Evaluation of Alternatives
The consumer evaluates products using criteria such as:
- **Price**, **taste**, **brand**, **nutritional value**, **packaging**, **shelf-life**, **convenience**, **ethical label** (organic, vegan, fair-trade).

Mathematically represented as:

$$ Consumer\ Choice = \arg\max_{i \in A} \ U_i(X_i, P_i, B_i, E_i) $$

where $U_i$ = perceived utility of alternative $i$; $X_i$ = product attributes; $P_i$ = price; $B_i$ = brand equity; $E_i$ = emotional value; $A$ = consideration set.

### Stage 4 — Purchase Decision
- The *intention* to buy may be **derailed by two risks**:
  1. **Attitude of others** (peer pressure).
  2. **Situational factors** (out-of-stock, long queue, bad weather).
- Final act = transactional + physical acquisition + choice (brand × quantity × payment mode × outlet).

### Stage 5 — Post-Purchase Behaviour
- **Cognitive Dissonance** (Festinger, 1957): The psychological discomfort after a high-involvement purchase (e.g., a ₹3,000 imported olive oil).
- Outcomes: **Satisfaction → Loyalty** OR **Dissatisfaction → Complaint / Brand Switching / Negative Word-of-Mouth**.
- The **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** is operationalised as:

$$ CSI = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(W_i \times S_i)}{n} $$

where $W_i$ = weight of attribute $i$; $S_i$ = satisfaction score on attribute $i$; $n$ = number of attributes.

## 2.2 Types of Consumer Buying Behaviour (Henry Assael's Typology)

| Type | Involvement | Brand Differences | Examples (Food Context) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Complex Buying Behaviour** | High | Significant | Buying a wedding cake, premium imported cheese. |
| **Dissonance-Reducing** | High | Few / similar | Buying a costly but unfamiliar cereal brand. |
| **Habitual Buying** | Low | Few | Regular purchase of Amul butter, Tata Salt. |
| **Variety-Seeking** | Low | Significant | Trying different chips flavours each week. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** *Complex* = high involvement + high differentiation. *Variety-seeking* = low involvement + high differentiation. Exam questions often ask you to **map a given food product to its correct buying-behaviour type**.

## 2.3 Models of Consumer Behaviour (Theoretical Foundations)

### Model A — The Economic Man Model (Marshallian)
$$ Consumer\ Utility\ Maximisation = \frac{MU_x}{P_x} = \frac{MU_y}{P_y} = \lambda\ (marginal\ utility\ per\ rupee) $$

- Assumes **rationality**, **perfect information**, **price-driven** decisions.
- Limitation: Ignores emotions, culture, brand love — unrealistic for food.

### Model B — The Learning Model (Pavlovian / Skinnerian)
$$ Behaviour\ Strength = f(Drives,\ Cues,\ Responses,\ Reinforcement) $$

- Operant conditioning: Positive reinforcement (tasty snack) → repeat purchase.
- Marketer's lever: Free samples (cue) → trial (response) → satisfaction (reinforcement).

### Model C — The Psychoanalytic Model (Freud)
- The **real reason** for buying (latent motive) differs from the **stated reason** (manifest motive).
- Example: A consumer *says* they buy diet soda for health, but *actually* buys it for social image.

### Model D — The Sociological Model (Veblen / Leibenstein)
- **Bandwagon Effect:** Buy because others are buying.
- **Snob Effect:** Buy because it's exclusive.
- **Veblen Effect:** Buy because it's expensive (luxury organic foods).

$$ D_{effective} = D_{functional} + D_{social} + D_{emotional} + D_{conditional} $$

The **Howard-Sheth Model** (1969) integrates all the above into a comprehensive four-input system: **Stimuli**, **Perceptual Variables**, **Learning Constructs**, and **Output**.

## 2.4 The Four Pillars of Influencing Factors (Kotler's Framework)

### I. Cultural Factors (Most Powerful & Deepest Influence)
- **Culture:** Set of basic values, perceptions, wants, behaviours learned from family/institutions. E.g., South Indian rice-eating culture.
- **Subculture:** Nationality, religion, regional, racial groups. E.g., Kerala's fish + tapioca tradition; Jain veganism.
- **Social Class:** Relatively permanent and ordered divisions of society. Upper-class consumers prefer artisanal cheeses; lower-middle-class prefer value-packs of Maggi.

### II. Social Factors
- **Reference Groups:** Membership groups (family, friends, colleagues) and aspirational groups (celebrity chefs like Sanjeev Kapoor).
- **Family:** Two key roles — *Family of Orientation* (parents influencing childhood food habits) and *Family of Procreation* (spouse influencing current purchases).
- **Roles & Status:** A working mother buys organic baby food for status; a bachelor student buys instant noodles.

### III. Personal Factors
- **Age & Life-Cycle Stage:** Single → Newlywed → Full Nest → Empty Nest.
- **Occupation & Economic Situation.**
- **Lifestyle:** A person's pattern of living expressed in activities, interests, opinions (AIO psychographics).
- **Personality & Self-Concept:** Self-image drives brand selection (e.g., fitness-conscious consumer buys protein bars).

### IV. Psychological Factors
- **Motivation (Maslow's Hierarchy):** Food safety = physiological; eating out = social; gourmet = esteem.
- **Perception:** Selective Exposure → Attention → Comprehension → Retention.
- **Learning:** Behaviour changes due to experience.
- **Beliefs & Attitudes:** A belief that "organic = pesticide-free" drives attitude formation.

### Maslow's Hierarchy Applied to Food

$$ M = \{Physiological \ (1), Safety \ (2), Social \ (3), Esteem \ (4), Self-Actualisation \ (5)\} $$

| Level | Food-Related Example |
|---|---|
| 1. Physiological | Plain rice, dal, bread. |
| 2. Safety | Packaged, FSSAI-certified, branded food. |
| 3. Love/Belonging | Family dinner at home, shared biryani. |
| 4. Esteem | Dining at a 5-star, posting gourmet reels. |
| 5. Self-Actualisation | Sourcing ingredients for self-cooked global cuisines. |

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Expression / Definition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Utility Maximisation | $\max U(x,y)$ s.t. $P_x x + P_y y \le M$ | Economic man's choice. |
| Marginal Utility | $MU_x = \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial x}$ | Diminishing marginal utility of food (1st slice > 5th slice of pizza). |
| Engel Curve | $x = f(M)$ at constant prices | Tracks food spend vs. income. |
| Customer Satisfaction Index | $CSI = \dfrac{\sum W_i \cdot S_i}{\sum W_i}$ | Post-purchase measurement. |
| Net Promoter Score | $NPS = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$ | Loyalty metric. |
| Brand Equity (Aaker) | $BE = Loyalty + Awareness + Perceived\ Quality + Associations$ | Pricing power. |
| Reference Group Influence | $I_{RG} = w_1 (Normative) + w_2 (Informational) + w_3 (Utilitarian)$ | Peer pressure effect. |
| Attitude-Behaviour Link | $B \propto (A_b - A_{alt}) \times Importance$ | Theory of Reasoned Action. |
| Theory of Planned Behaviour | $B = f(Attitude + Subjective\ Norm + PBC)$ | Ajzen's TPB. |
| Howard-Sheth Outputs | Attention → Comprehension → Attitude → Intention → Purchase | Comprehensive model. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** For *Part B 14-mark questions* in KTU ESE, drawing a **labelled diagram** of the 5-stage consumer decision process + identifying the factor cluster that influences each stage fetches **2–3 extra marks** easily.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Food Industry

- **Product Development:** ITC's *Aashirvaad Svasti* (regional dairy) was developed after studying sub-cultural milk consumption patterns.
- **Packaging Design:** Saffola Oats' regional-language packaging responds to cultural-linguistic preferences.
- **Pricing Strategy:** Pepsi/Coca-Cola use **reference pricing** (₹20 anchor) to position against value cola.
- **Advertising Strategy:** Surf Excel's "Daag acche hain" leverages **belief-attitude** psychology of Indian mothers.
- **Retail Layout:** End-aisle placements target **habitual buyers**; free samples trigger **variety-seeking** trials.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: The Consumer Equilibrium Condition (Marshallian Cardinal Utility)

The classical economic derivation of when a rational consumer maximises utility — critical foundation for the **Economic Man Model** in CBB.

**Setup:** A consumer has income $M$ and faces two goods $X$ (e.g., milk) and $Y$ (e.g., biscuits) with prices $P_x$ and $P_y$.

$$ Maximise\ U(X,Y) \ \ subject\ to\ P_x X + P_y Y \le M $$

**Step 1:** Construct the Lagrangian:

$$ \mathcal{L} = U(X,Y) + \lambda(M - P_x X - P_y Y) $$

**Step 2:** Take first-order partial derivatives:

$$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial X} = \frac{\partial U}{\partial X} - \lambda P_x = 0 \implies MU_x = \lambda P_x $$

$$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Y} = \frac{\partial U}{\partial Y} - \lambda P_y = 0 \implies MU_y = \lambda P_y $$

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions for $\lambda$:

$$ \frac{MU_x}{P_x} = \lambda = \frac{MU_y}{P_y} $$

**Step 4:** The **Law of Equi-Marginal Utility** is therefore:

$$ \boxed{\frac{MU_x}{P_x} = \frac{MU_y}{P_y} = \frac{MU_n}{P_n}} $$

**Interpretation:** A consumer reaches equilibrium when the **last rupee spent on every food product yields the same marginal utility**. If $MU_x / P_x > MU_y / P_y$, the consumer reallocates spending from $Y$ to $X$ until equality holds.

**Step 5:** The budget constraint must bind at optimum:

$$ P_x X^{*} + P_y Y^{*} = M $$

This **simultaneous solution** $(X^{*}, Y^{*})$ is the consumer's optimal food basket.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world food application:** When ITC launched *Aashirvaad Atta*, they observed that the rational homemaker allocated wheat flour budget until $\frac{MU_{atta}}{P_{atta}} = \frac{MU_{rice}}{P_{rice}}$. Promotional discounts on atta temporarily shifted this equilibrium, triggering stockpiling behaviour.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Engel Curve for Food Expenditure

**Step 1:** Start with the Cobb-Douglas utility function for food ($F$) and non-food ($N$):

$$ U(F, N) = F^{\alpha} \cdot N^{\beta} $$

**Step 2:** The budget constraint is:

$$ P_F F + P_N N = M $$

**Step 3:** The Lagrangian is:

$$ \mathcal{L} = F^{\alpha} N^{\beta} + \lambda(M - P_F F - P_N N) $$

**Step 4:** First-order conditions:

$$ \alpha F^{\alpha-1} N^{\beta} = \lambda P_F $$
$$ \beta F^{\alpha} N^{\beta-1} = \lambda P_N $$

**Step 5:** Divide the two conditions:

$$ \frac{\alpha N}{\beta F} = \frac{P_F}{P_N} \implies F = \frac{\alpha}{\beta} \cdot \frac{P_N}{P_F} \cdot N $$

**Step 6:** Substitute back into the budget:

$$ N = \frac{\beta}{\alpha + \beta} \cdot \frac{M}{P_N} $$

$$ \boxed{F = \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta} \cdot \frac{M}{P_F}} $$

**Step 7:** Demand for food is **linear in income**, with slope $\dfrac{\alpha}{(\alpha+\beta)P_F}$.

**Economic Interpretation (Engel's Law for Food):** As $M \uparrow$, the **proportion of income spent on food** ($\dfrac{P_F F}{M}$) falls — because $\alpha + \beta > 1$ in Cobb-Douglas models. This explains why:

- A **BPL household** spends **60–70 %** of income on food.
- A **high-income urban household** spends **15–25 %** on food (rest on lifestyle, dining out, premiumisation).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How Each Factor Influences Buying Behaviour

| Factor Cluster | Sub-Factor | Mechanism | Food Industry Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cultural** | Culture | Imprints lifelong food preferences. | Kerala's rice + sambar tradition. |
| | Subculture | Regional/religious dietary codes. | Jain consumers → no root vegetables. |
| | Social Class | Class-driven product preferences. | Premium artisanal cheese for upper class. |
| **Social** | Reference Groups | Normative conformity. | Influencer posts driving oat-milk trials. |
| | Family | Spousal/parental influence. | Mothers choosing organic baby food. |
| | Roles & Status | Social role dictates purchases. | Working woman → ready-to-eat meals. |
| **Personal** | Age | Age-based palatability & nutrition. | Protein supplements for 18–30. |
| | Life-Cycle | Stage-based needs. | Newlywed couples → gourmet kits. |
| | Economic | Budget constraint binding. | Recession → shift to private labels. |
| | Lifestyle | AIO psychographic segmentation. | "Health-conscious" → cold-pressed juices. |
| | Personality/Self-Concept | Self-image alignment. | "Fit" personality → whey protein. |
| **Psychological** | Motivation | Maslow drive. | Physiological hunger → instant noodles. |
| | Perception | Selective attention. | Consumer notices "100 % Whole Wheat" label. |
| | Learning | Conditioning & reinforcement. | Repeat purchase of tasty snack. |
| | Beliefs & Attitudes | Cognitive schema. | Belief "organic = healthy" → purchase. |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Applying the Theory of Planned Behaviour (TPB) to a Food Purchase Decision

The TPB (Ajzen, 1991) predicts that buying behaviour $B$ is a function of three antecedents:

$$ B = w_1 \cdot A_{behaviour} + w_2 \cdot SN + w_3 \cdot PBC $$

**where:**
- $A_{behaviour}$ = Attitude toward the behaviour (positive/negative evaluation).
- $SN$ = Subjective Norm (perceived social pressure).
- $PBC$ = Perceived Behavioural Control (ease/difficulty perception).

### Worked Numerical-Style Analytical Example

A consumer evaluates buying **organic quinoa** (₹500/kg) instead of regular rice (₹80/kg).

**Step 1:** Attitude toward buying organic quinoa:

$$ A_{behaviour} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i \cdot e_i $$

Let:
- $b_1 = +3$ (belief strength: "Organic is healthy"), $e_1 = +4$ (evaluation: "Health is good")
- $b_2 = -2$ (belief strength: "Too expensive"), $e_2 = -3$ (evaluation: "Wasting money is bad")
- $b_3 = +2$ (belief: "Trendy among friends"), $e_3 = +3$ (evaluation: "Social approval is good")

$$ A_{behaviour} = (3)(+4) + (-2)(-3) + (2)(+3) = 12 + 6 + 6 = +24 $$

**Step 2:** Subjective Norm (peer + family pressure):

$$ SN = \sum_{j=1}^{m} n_j \cdot m_j $$

- Family says "yes, buy organic" ($n_1 = +2$, $m_1 = +4$)
- Colleagues indifferent ($n_2 = 0$)
- Fitness influencer endorses ($n_3 = +3$, $m_3 = +5$)

$$ SN = (2)(+4) + (3)(+5) = 8 + 15 = +23 $$

**Step 3:** Perceived Behavioural Control:

$$ PBC = c_1 + c_2 + c_3 $$

- Availability at local store: $+5$
- Budget allows: $+4$
- Knows how to cook quinoa: $-2$ (low confidence)

$$ PBC = +7 $$

**Step 4:** Aggregate purchase intention:

$$ B = w_1(24) + w_2(23) + w_3(7) $$

With weights $w_1 = 0.4, w_2 = 0.3, w_3 = 0.3$:

$$ B = (0.4)(24) + (0.3)(23) + (0.3)(7) = 9.6 + 6.9 + 2.1 = 18.6 $$

**Step 5:** Interpretation:

Since $B > 15$ (a critical threshold), the consumer **proceeds to purchase**. If $B$ had been negative (e.g., budget crunch reduced $PBC$ to $-3$), purchase would be **postponed**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Application Insight:** Food marketers run **conjoint analysis** and **TPB surveys** to predict product adoption rates before launch. This is how brands like *Paper Boat* or *Yoga Bar* validate new SKUs.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI) Calculation

Suppose a food brand receives 200 customer responses rating 5 attributes on a 1–10 scale.

| Attribute | Avg. Score ($S_i$) | Weight ($W_i$) | $W_i \times S_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Taste | 8 | 0.30 | 2.40 |
| Price | 6 | 0.25 | 1.50 |
| Packaging | 7 | 0.15 | 1.05 |
| Availability | 9 | 0.20 | 1.80 |
| Brand Trust | 8 | 0.10 | 0.80 |

$$ CSI = \sum_{i=1}^{5} (W_i \times S_i) = 2.40 + 1.50 + 1.05 + 1.80 + 0.80 = 7.55 $$

**Interpretation:** On a 10-point scale, $CSI = 7.55$ indicates **high satisfaction**. Scores below 5.0 typically trigger **brand-switching** behaviour.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Table: Consumer Behaviour Models at a Glance

| Model | Origin | Core Idea | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Economic / Marshallian | 1890s | Rational utility max. | Mathematically elegant. | Ignores emotion. |
| Pavlovian / Learning | 1900s | Stimulus–Response. | Explains habit. | Ignores cognition. |
| Freudian / Psychoanalytic | 1900s | Unconscious motives. | Reveals hidden motives. | Hard to measure. |
| Veblen / Sociological | 1899 | Social influence. | Captures luxury. | Ignores individuals. |
| Howard-Sheth | 1969 | Integrative. | Comprehensive. | Complex to use. |
| EKB Model | 1968 | 5-stage process. | Stage-wise actionable. | Sequential assumption. |
| Theory of Planned Behaviour | 1991 | Attitude + Norm + Control. | Predicts intention. | Ignores emotions. |
| Engel-Blackwell-Miniard | Updated EKB | Includes post-purchase. | Industry standard. | Linear. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The 5-Stage Consumer Decision-Making Process (EKB Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Need Recognition] --> B[Stage 2: Information Search]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Evaluation of Alternatives]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Purchase Decision]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Post-Purchase Behaviour]
    E --> F{Satisfied?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Loyalty / Repeat Purchase]
    F -->|No| H[Dissonance / Complaint / Switch]
    G --> A
    H --> A

    X1[Internal Stimuli: Hunger, Thirst] -.-> A
    X2[External Stimuli: Ads, Peer, Display] -.-> A
    Y1[Internal Search: Memory] -.-> B
    Y2[External Search: Reviews, Friends, Online] -.-> B
    Z1[Two Perceived Risks] -.-> D
    Z1a[Attitude of Others] -.-> Z1
    Z1b[Situational Factors] -.-> Z1
```
