# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
Define **Consumer Buying Behaviour**. List the **four major factors** that influence it as per Kotler's framework.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Consumer Buying Behaviour refers to the **decision processes and acts of people involved in selecting, buying, using, and disposing of goods, services, ideas, or experiences to satisfy needs and wants**.

**Four factors (2 marks):**

| Factor | Brief Description |
|---|---|
| **Cultural** | Culture, subculture, social class. |
| **Social** | Reference groups, family, roles and status. |
| **Personal** | Age, life-cycle, occupation, lifestyle, personality. |
| **Psychological** | Motivation, perception, learning, beliefs and attitudes. |

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Writing the **C-S-P-P** mnemonic alone (without explanation) may fetch only 1 mark. Always add one-line justification for each factor to secure full marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
Differentiate between **Complex Buying Behaviour** and **Variety-Seeking Buying Behaviour**. Give **one food-product example** for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Dimension | Complex | Variety-Seeking |
|---|---|---|
| Involvement Level | **High** | **Low** |
| Brand Differences | **Significant** | **Significant** |
| Decision Time | Long | Short |
| Risk Perception | High | Low |
| **Food Example** | Wedding cake, premium cheese | Different chips flavours tried weekly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Clarity:** Both types share *high brand differentiation*, but complex buying is **high involvement**, while variety-seeking is **low involvement**. Examiners often test this distinction.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice between two full questions is provided.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model QP Module 3]**

> *"A leading organic food brand wants to launch a new range of millet-based snacks in Kerala. Explain the **complete consumer buying decision process** and discuss how **cultural, social, personal, and psychological factors** will influence the buying behaviour of the target consumer."*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Consumer Decision-Making Process**

**Model Solution:**

The five-stage **Engel-Blackwell-Kollat (EKB) model** governs the decision journey:

**Stage 1: Need Recognition (1 mark)**
- Triggered by internal cues (health concern, doctor advice) and external cues (Instagram reels on millets, supermarket displays). For organic millets, the *health-conscious* urban consumer experiences dissonance with regular fried snacks.

**Stage 2: Information Search (1 mark)**
- *Internal Search:* Recalls taste experiences of ragi, jowar.
- *External Search:* Searches Google, watches YouTube reviews, consults nutritionist friends, checks Amazon ratings.

**Stage 3: Evaluation of Alternatives (1.5 marks)**
- Compares brands on taste, price (₹80–₹150), packaging, certifications (India Organic, Jaivik Bharat), shelf-life, and brand trust.
- Mathematically: $Choice = \arg\max_i U_i(taste, price, health, brand)$.

**Stage 4: Purchase Decision (1 mark)**
- Two perceived risks: *Attitude of others* (family approval) and *Situational factors* (availability in local store). Online ordering via BigBasket mitigates availability risk.

**Stage 5: Post-Purchase Behaviour (1 mark)**
- If the snack tastes good and aligns with the *health* belief, **satisfaction + repeat purchase** occurs. If not, dissonance triggers **negative word-of-mouth** or brand switching.

**Stage-wise marketer interventions (1.5 marks):**

| Stage | Marketer Action |
|---|---|
| Need Recognition | Influencer marketing, in-store aroma triggers. |
| Information Search | SEO-optimised content, WhatsApp nutrition guides. |
| Evaluation | Free samples, comparison tables on website. |
| Purchase | Discount codes, easy EMI, quick delivery. |
| Post-Purchase | Loyalty points, follow-up feedback forms. |

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Four Factor Clusters Influencing Behaviour**

**(i) Cultural Factors (1.75 marks)**
- *Culture:* Kerala's traditional *kanji* and millet porridge tradition supports millet adoption.
- *Subculture:* Ayurveda emphasises *pathyam* (millet-based diets) during monsoon → natural fit.
- *Social Class:* Premium organic millets target upper-middle-class urban consumers.

**(ii) Social Factors (1.75 marks)**
- *Reference Groups:* Fitness influencer endorsements and WhatsApp health groups.
- *Family:* Mothers as primary household purchasers; husbands approving premium purchases.
- *Roles & Status:* Working professionals seeking *guilt-free snacking* tied to self-image.

**(iii) Personal Factors (1.75 marks)**
- *Age:* Millennials (25–40) more open to millets than Gen Z preferring chips.
- *Lifestyle:* Health-conscious, yoga-practising consumers (AIO psychographic cluster).
- *Economic:* Disposable income must exceed ₹5,000/month for premium pricing acceptance.

**(iv) Psychological Factors (1.75 marks)**
- *Motivation:* Maslow's safety and esteem levels — health, longevity, social prestige.
- *Perception:* Selective attention to "100 % whole grain" labels.
- *Learning:* Trial packs create positive reinforcement (tasty → repeat).
- *Beliefs & Attitudes:* Strong belief that "millet = diabetes control" drives favourable attitude.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Examiner's Note:** Students often *only* list the four factors in part (b) without giving **mechanism + food example + marketer implication**. Each factor must be paired with a **real food illustration** and an **actionable marketing intervention** to score full marks.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP Module 3]**

> *"Explain the **Theory of Planned Behaviour** with reference to a **consumer's decision to purchase functional probiotic yogurt**. Discuss the **types of consumer buying behaviour** and how they apply to the Indian dairy industry."*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Theory of Planned Behaviour Applied**

**Model Solution:**

The **Theory of Planned Behaviour (TPB)**, proposed by **Icek Ajzen (1991)**, predicts consumer behaviour from three antecedents:

$$ B = w_1 A_{behaviour} + w_2 SN + w_3 PBC $$

**Component 1: Attitude (2 marks)**

$$ A_{behaviour} = \sum_{i} b_i \cdot e_i $$

- *Belief 1:* "Probiotic yogurt improves gut health" ($b_1 = +4$) × "Gut health matters" ($e_1 = +5$) = $+20$.
- *Belief 2:* "It tastes sour" ($b_2 = +3$) × "I dislike sour taste" ($e_2 = -3$) = $-9$.
- *Belief 3:* "It is expensive" ($b_3 = +3$) × "Spending is bad" ($e_3 = -2$) = $-6$.

$$ A_{behaviour} = 20 - 9 - 6 = +5\ (positive\ but\ weak) $$

**Component 2: Subjective Norm (2 marks)**

- Family approves ($n_1 = +3$, $m_1 = +4$).
- Friends neutral ($n_2 = 0$).
- Doctor recommends ($n_3 = +5$, $m_3 = +5$).

$$ SN = 12 + 0 + 25 = +37 $$

**Component 3: Perceived Behavioural Control (2 marks)**

- Available at supermarket: $+5$.
- Affordable: $+3$.
- Familiar with brand: $+4$.

$$ PBC = +12 $$

**Component 4: Aggregate Intention (1 mark)**

$$ B = 0.4(5) + 0.3(37) + 0.3(12) = 2 + 11.1 + 3.6 = 16.7 $$

Since $B > 15$ threshold, **purchase is likely**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners reward **numerical/quantitative working** over pure theory narration. Always substitute dummy values to demonstrate you *understand* the TPB mechanism.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Types of Buying Behaviour in Indian Dairy Industry**

**Henry Assael's Four Types (7 marks):**

| Type | Characteristics | Indian Dairy Examples (2024) |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Complex Buying Behaviour** | High involvement + significant brand differences. | Buying imported Greek yogurt (Epigamia) for the first time. |
| **2. Dissonance-Reducing** | High involvement + few perceived differences. | Choosing between two premium paneer brands at a Kirana store. |
| **3. Habitual Buying** | Low involvement + few brand differences. | Daily Amul Taaza milk, regular Mother Dairy curd. |
| **4. Variety-Seeking** | Low involvement + significant brand differences. | Trying different yogurt flavours each week (Strawberry → Blueberry → Mango). |

**Implications for Indian Dairy Marketers (2 marks):**

1. **Mass commodities** (milk, curd) → Habitual → invest in **distribution + availability**.
2. **Premium innovations** (Greek yogurt, A2 milk) → Complex → invest in **education + sampling**.
3. **Flavoured yogurt** → Variety-seeking → invest in **flavour innovation + impulse placement**.
4. **Paneer/cheese** → Dissonance-reducing → invest in **brand reassurance + return guarantees**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common mistake:** Confusing *Complex* with *Dissonance-Reducing* — both are high involvement. The difference is in **brand differentiation** (high vs. low).
> 2. **Common mistake:** Forgetting to give an **Indian-specific food example** in each type. Generic global examples (e.g., Tesla cars) lose marks.
> 3. **Common mistake:** Writing only definitions without **drawing or labelling** the EKB or Assael diagrams.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> **Important Things to Remember — High-Density Bullet Summary**

- **Definition:** Consumer Buying Behaviour = decision + act of selecting, buying, using, and disposing of products to satisfy needs.
- **Five-Stage EKB Model:** Need Recognition → Information Search → Evaluation of Alternatives → Purchase Decision → Post-Purchase Behaviour.
- **Four Factor Clusters (C-S-P-P):** Cultural, Social, Personal, Psychological.
- **Cultural Factors (deepest influence):** Culture, Subculture, Social Class.
- **Social Factors:** Reference Groups, Family, Roles & Status.
- **Personal Factors:** Age & Life-Cycle, Occupation & Income, Lifestyle, Personality & Self-Concept.
- **Psychological Factors:** Motivation, Perception, Learning, Beliefs & Attitudes.
- **Maslow's Hierarchy (food adaptation):** Physiological (rice) → Safety (branded) → Social (family meal) → Esteem (fine dining) → Self-Actualisation (exotic self-cooked cuisine).
- **Assael's Four Buying Behaviour Types:**
  - Complex (high inv. + high diff.)
  - Dissonance-Reducing (high inv. + low diff.)
  - Habitual (low inv. + low diff.)
  - Variety-Seeking (low inv. + high diff.)
- **Three Levels of Decision-Making:** Extensive Problem Solving → Limited Problem Solving → Habitual/Routine Response.
- **TPB Equation:** $B = w_1 A + w_2 SN + w_3 PBC$.
- **Attitude Calculation:** $A = \sum b_i \cdot e_i$ (belief strength × outcome evaluation).
- **Economic Man Equilibrium:** $\dfrac{MU_x}{P_x} = \dfrac{MU_y}{P_y} = \lambda$.
- **Engel's Law:** As income rises, the **share** of income spent on food falls.
- **Models to Remember:** Economic (Marshallian), Pavlovian (Learning), Freudian (Psychoanalytic), Veblenian (Social), Howard-Sheth (Integrative), EKB, TPB.
- **Post-Purchase Outcomes:** Satisfaction → Loyalty; Dissatisfaction → Dissonance → Complaint or Switch.
- **CSI Formula:** $CSI = \sum (W_i \times S_i)$; high CSI indicates strong brand health.
- **Veblen Effects on Food:** Bandwagon (everyone's buying keto snacks), Snob (exclusive imported cheese), Veblen (luxury saffron rice).
- **Cognitive Dissonance (Festinger, 1957):** Post-purchase mental discomfort; marketers counter with reassurance messaging.
- **KTU 2024 Hot Tip:** Always pair theory with **at least one Indian food example** (e.g., Amul, ITC, Britannia, Nestlé India, local Kerala brands) to score full marks in part (b).

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Consumer Buying Behaviour and Factors Influencing It** ✅

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Export Trade: Government Regulations, GAIT, WTO Regulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT724 - FOOD INDUSTRY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Export Trade: Government Regulations, GAIT, WTO Regulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Export Trade — Formal Academic Definition

**Export Trade** refers to the commercial transaction in which goods or services produced in one country (the *domestic economy*) are sold and shipped to a buyer located in another country (the *foreign economy*), with the objective of earning foreign exchange (forex), expanding market reach, and contributing to the nation's Gross Domestic Product (GDP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Export trade is the legal, financial, and logistical framework that enables a domestic producer to ship goods across international borders in compliance with the **Foreign Trade Policy (FTP)**, the **Customs Act, 1962**, the **Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA), 1999**, and the multilateral rules enforced by the **World Trade Organization (WTO)**.

In the specific context of the **food industry**, export trade covers the cross-border movement of:
- **Agri-commodities** (rice, spices, tea, coffee, marine products)
- **Processed foods** (ready-to-eat meals, fruit pulps, dairy derivatives)
- **Beverages and alcohol**
- **Edible oils and oleoresins**
- **Livestock, poultry, and animal by-products**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Regulatory Bodies in India for Export Trade**
> - **DGFT** – Director General of Foreign Trade (issues IEC, advances EPCG)
> - **CBIC** – Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs
> - **RBI** – Reserve Bank of India (forex remittance)
> - **FSSAI** – Food Safety and Standards Authority of India
> - **EIC** – Export Inspection Council
> - **APEDA / MPEDA** – Commodity Boards (Agriculture / Marine exports)
> - **ECGC** – Export Credit Guarantee Corporation

---

## 1.2 GAIT (General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade) — Definition

**GATT** is a multilateral treaty negotiated in **1947** by 23 original contracting parties under the auspices of the United Nations to regulate international trade by reducing tariffs, eliminating quantitative restrictions (quotas), and establishing a rules-based trading system. Although it was originally intended as a temporary arrangement pending the formation of a full **International Trade Organization (ITO)**, it continued to function for nearly **48 years (1947–1994)** and was finally replaced by the WTO on **1 January 1995**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GAIT is often typed as "GATT"** — *General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade*. The acronym is the same. The syllabus uses "GAIT" interchangeably.

---

## 1.3 WTO (World Trade Organization) — Definition

The **WTO** is the only global international organization that deals with the **rules of trade between nations**. Established on **1 January 1995** under the **Marrakesh Agreement (signed 15 April 1994)**, the WTO provides a framework for negotiating trade agreements, settling disputes, and implementing multilateral trade rules. As of 2024, the WTO has **164 member nations** representing over **98% of world trade**.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "International Trade Airport"

Imagine a country is a **giant international airport**:

- **Export Trade** is the **departure terminal** where goods board international "flights."
- **GAIT / WTO** is the **Air Traffic Control (ATC)** that makes sure planes (goods) from different countries don't crash into each other, follow common routes (rules), and pay predictable fees (tariffs).
- **Government Regulations (DGFT, Customs, FSSAI)** are the **immigration counters, security checks, and baggage screening** that ensure no dangerous, illegal, or sub-standard goods board the plane.
- **Tariffs** are the **landing and takeoff fees** charged at each country's airport.
- **The Dispute Settlement Body (DSB)** is the **International Court of Aviation Disputes** that resolves conflicts when a country accuses another of breaking the rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway for KTU Students**
> Without GAIT/WTO, every country would set its own rules, and exporters would face chaos — like a pilot trying to land at an airport with no runway lights, no tower, and no shared signals. WTO provides the **runway lights, the radio frequency, and the runway itself**.

---

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in the Food Industry

The food industry is one of the **most regulated** sectors globally because food is a matter of **public health, cultural sensitivity, and national security**. A single violation — pesticide residue, microbial contamination, or non-declared allergens — can lead to:
- **Consignment rejection** at the destination port
- **Importer blacklisting** of the entire country
- **WTO dispute** under the **SPS Agreement**

Therefore, KTU 2024 Scheme mandates that every Food Industry Management graduate understand the **legal architecture of export trade** before they can even consider taking a brand global.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Government Regulations Governing Export Trade in India

India does **not** have a single "Export Act." Instead, export trade is governed by a **layered regulatory framework**:

### 2.1.1 The Foreign Trade (Development & Regulation) Act, 1992 (FTDR Act)
- This is the **umbrella law** for all export-import operations in India.
- Administered by the **Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT)** under the Ministry of Commerce and Industry.
- The Act mandates that every exporter obtain an **IEC (Importer-Exporter Code)** — a **10-digit** unique identifier issued by DGFT. No IEC, no export.

### 2.1.2 The Customs Act, 1962
- Governs the **physical movement** of goods across the customs frontier.
- Levies **Basic Customs Duty (BCD)**, **Social Welfare Surcharge (SWS)**, and **IGST** on imports.
- Implements **Drawback** (refund of duties on re-export) and **EPCG (Export Promotion Capital Goods)** schemes.

### 2.1.3 The Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA), 1999
- Replaced the older **FERA, 1973**.
- Regulates **foreign exchange receipts and payments** by Indian exporters.
- Exporters must receive payment and repatriate it to India within **9 months** from the date of shipment (under RBI's *Master Direction on Export of Goods and Services*).

### 2.1.4 The Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006 (FSSAI)
- Mandatory for **all food exports** from India.
- The **FSSAI License / FSSAI Registration** is required for the manufacturing unit.
- Exports must comply with the standards of the **destination country** (e.g., **Codex Alimentarius**, **USFDA**, **EFSA**).

### 2.1.5 Export Inspection Council of India (EIC)
- Established under the **Export (Quality Control and Inspection) Act, 1963**.
- Mandatory pre-shipment inspection for specific notified food categories (fish, marine products, dairy, etc.).

### 2.1.6 Commodity Boards
- **APEDA** (Agricultural and Processed Food Products Export Development Authority) — for scheduled agri-products.
- **MPEDA** (Marine Products Export Development Authority) — for seafood.
- **Tea Board, Coffee Board, Spices Board, Tobacco Board** — for their respective commodities.

### 2.1.7 ECGC (Export Credit Guarantee Corporation)
- Provides **insurance cover** to exporters against buyer non-payment and commercial/political risks.
- The standard policy is the **Standard Policy (Shipments)** covering up to **90% of the invoice value**.

---

## 2.2 GAIT — Core Pillars and Principles

GAIT operated on **four foundational principles** that are now embedded in the WTO:

### Pillar 1: Most-Favoured-Nation (MFN) Treatment
Any trade concession (e.g., a tariff cut) granted to one member must be **immediately and unconditionally** extended to **all other members**.

> **Example:** If India lowers the import duty on French wine from 100% to 50%, that 50% rate must automatically apply to wine from the USA, Australia, Chile — every WTO member.

### Pillar 2: National Treatment
Once a foreign product has cleared the border and paid the import duty, it must be treated **no less favourably** than a domestically produced "like product" in terms of internal taxes, regulations, and distribution rules.

### Pillar 3: Tariff Binding
Members "bind" their tariffs — they commit to **not raise them above a specified ceiling** without compensating other members. The **bound rate** is the ceiling; the **applied rate** is what is actually charged.

### Pillar 4: Elimination of Quantitative Restrictions (QRs)
Members must phase out **quotas, embargoes, and licensing requirements** that restrict the *quantity* of imports. Tariffs are the **only** legitimate border protection allowed.

---

## 2.3 Evolution of GAIT — The Trade Rounds

| Round | Years | Key Outcome |
|:------|:------|:------------|
| Geneva | 1947 | Tariff reductions on ~45,000 tariff lines |
| Annecy | 1949 | Further tariff bindings |
| Torquay | 1950-51 | Tariff reductions |
| Geneva II | 1955-56 | Tariff revisions |
| **Dillon** | 1960-61 | **European Economic Community** (EEC) formation; problem-solving |
| **Kennedy** | 1964-67 | First round focused on **non-tariff barriers**; antidumping code |
| **Tokyo** | 1973-79 | Tariff cuts on industrial goods; **plurilateral codes** on subsidies, customs valuation |
| **Uruguay** | 1986-94 | Birth of **WTO**; included agriculture, textiles, services, IP, dispute settlement |

---

## 2.4 WTO — Structure and Key Agreements

### 2.4.1 Organizational Structure (Top-Down)
1. **Ministerial Conference** — Apex body, meets at least once every 2 years (e.g., MC13 in Abu Dhabi 2024).
2. **General Council** — Day-to-day decision-making body.
3. **Dispute Settlement Body (DSB)** — Adjudicates trade disputes between members.
4. **Trade Policy Review Body (TPRB)** — Reviews each member's trade policies every 2/4/6 years (depending on share of world trade).
5. **Council for Trade in Goods (CTG)**
6. **Council for Trade in Services (CTS)**
7. **Council for Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights (TRIPS Council)**

### 2.4.2 The Umbrella Agreements (Annexes of the Marrakesh Agreement)

| Annex | Agreement | Relevance to Food Industry |
|:------|:----------|:---------------------------|
| **Annex 1A** | **GATT 1994** (updated GAIT) | Tariffs, MFN, NT on goods |
| Annex 1A | **AoA** (Agreement on Agriculture) | Domestic support, market access, export subsidies for farm products |
| Annex 1A | **SPS** (Sanitary & Phytosanitary) | Food safety, pesticide limits, animal/plant health |
| Annex 1A | **TBT** (Technical Barriers to Trade) | Labelling, packaging, quality standards |
| Annex 1B | **GATS** (General Agreement on Trade in Services) | Food logistics, cold chain, restaurant services |
| Annex 1C | **TRIPS** | Geographical Indications (e.g., **Darjeeling Tea**, **Basmati Rice**) |
| Annex 2 | **DSU** (Dispute Settlement Understanding) | Litigation mechanism |
| Annex 3 | **TPRM** (Trade Policy Review Mechanism) | Peer review of national policies |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Logic | Notation & Units |
|:--------|:----------------|:-----------------|
| **FOB Value** (Free on Board) | $\text{FOB} = \text{Ex-Factory Price} + \text{Loading Charges}$ | In INR or USD |
| **CIF Value** (Cost, Insurance, Freight) | $\text{CIF} = \text{FOB} + \text{Insurance} + \text{Freight}$ | In INR or USD |
| **Effective Rate of Protection (ERP)** | $g_{j} = \dfrac{t_{j} - \sum_{i} a_{ij}\, t_{i}}{1 - \sum_{i} a_{ij}}$ | where $a_{ij}$ = input coefficient, $t$ = nominal tariff |
| **Tariff Binding Margin** | $\text{Binding Margin} = \text{Bound Rate} - \text{Applied Rate}$ | Expressed in % |
| **Drawback Rate** | $\text{Refunded} = \text{Rate} \times \text{FOB Value}$ | In INR |
| **Export Realization Period** | $\leq 9$ months from date of shipment | RBI mandate |
| **MSME MEIS Benefit** | Up to **5% of FOB value** (under earlier schemes) | Now replaced by **RoDTEP** |
| **RoDTEP Rebate** | Calculated as % of FOB — currently **0.3% – 4.3%** by HS code | Per Customs Notification |
| **Forex Conversion** | $\text{INR Equivalent} = \text{Foreign Currency} \times \text{TT Buying Rate (RBI)}$ | Conversion via AD bank |
| **Countervailing Duty (CVD)** | $\text{CVD} = \text{Value} \times \text{Ad Valorem Rate}$ | Equivalent to local excise |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CIF vs FOB is a **Killer Question** in KTU 2024 ESE.** Students frequently lose marks by stating the formula backward. Memorize: **C-I-F = C**ost + **I**nsurance + **F**reight.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility for Food Engineers

1. **Pricing decisions** — Knowing CIF/FOB helps the food company decide **who pays the freight**: Indian exporter (CIF) or foreign buyer (FOB).
2. **Export incentive claims** — RoDTEP and drawback are computed on the FOB value. Errors cost the company money.
3. **Market selection** — Companies select export markets based on **tariff differentials** (e.g., a **bound rate of 150%** in the EU makes fresh mango exports uncompetitive).
4. **Dispute preparedness** — A food contamination case can become a WTO dispute (e.g., the **EU–India Hormones case**).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Models

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Converting FOB to CIF (Step-by-Step)

**Problem Statement:** A food exporter in Kerala ships 100 metric tonnes of freeze-dried pineapple to Germany. The **Ex-Factory Price** is **₹2,00,000 per tonne**. Loading charges are **₹10,000 per tonne**. Freight to Hamburg is **₹25,000 per tonne**. Marine insurance is **1.25% of FOB**. Compute the CIF value.

### Step 1: Calculate FOB per tonne

$$
\text{FOB} = \text{Ex-Factory Price} + \text{Loading Charges}
$$

$$
\text{FOB} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}000 = \text{₹}\,2{,}10{,}000 \text{ per tonne}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate Freight per tonne

$$
\text{Freight per tonne} = \text{₹}\,25{,}000
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Insurance per tonne

Insurance is **1.25% of FOB**:

$$
\text{Insurance} = 0.0125 \times 2{,}10{,}000 = \text{₹}\,2{,}625 \text{ per tonne}
$$

### Step 4: Compute CIF per tonne

$$
\text{CIF} = \text{FOB} + \text{Freight} + \text{Insurance}
$$

$$
\text{CIF} = 2{,}10{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 2{,}625 = \text{₹}\,2{,}37{,}625 \text{ per tonne}
$$

### Step 5: Total CIF for the consignment (100 tonnes)

$$
\text{Total CIF} = 2{,}37{,}625 \times 100 = \text{₹}\,2{,}37{,}62{,}500
$$

> **Answer:** CIF Value = **₹ 2,37,62,500** (or ₹ 2.376 crores).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Rate of Protection (ERP) — A Critical Trade Concept

The **Effective Rate of Protection (ERP)** measures the **actual protection** given to a domestic value-added process after accounting for duties on its inputs.

### Formula

$$
g_{j} = \frac{t_{j} - \sum_{i} a_{ij}\, t_{i}}{1 - \sum_{i} a_{ij}}
$$

Where:
- $g_{j}$ = **Effective Rate of Protection** on the final product $j$
- $t_{j}$ = **Nominal tariff** on the final product $j$
- $t_{i}$ = **Nominal tariff** on the input $i$
- $a_{ij}$ = **Input coefficient** = the cost of input $i$ required to produce one unit of $j$, expressed as a fraction of the value of $j$

### Worked Example — Tomato Ketchup Industry

Suppose a domestic ketchup producer:
- Uses **fresh tomatoes** (input) which cost **60%** of the value of the ketchup produced → $a_{ij} = 0.60$
- Government levies a **tariff of 40%** on imported ketchup → $t_{j} = 0.40$
- Government levies a **tariff of 20%** on imported tomatoes → $t_{i} = 0.20$

### Step 1: Substitute values into the ERP formula

$$
g_{j} = \frac{0.40 - (0.60 \times 0.20)}{1 - 0.60}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the numerator

$$
0.60 \times 0.20 = 0.12
$$

$$
0.40 - 0.12 = 0.28
$$

### Step 3: Compute the denominator

$$
1 - 0.60 = 0.40
$$

### Step 4: Final division

$$
g_{j} = \frac{0.28}{0.40} = 0.70 = 70\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** Even though the **nominal tariff** on ketchup is only **40%**, the **effective protection** given to the value-added ketchup-making process is **70%**. This is because the imported raw tomatoes (an input) carry a *lower* tariff (20%), making domestic ketchup production doubly attractive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ERP matters in food industry strategy:** Indian food companies use ERP analysis to lobby the government for **input-side tariff reductions** (cheaper raw imports) while retaining **output-side protection** for finished goods — a strategy called **tariff escalation** in WTO parlance.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: RoDTEP Rebate Calculation

**RoDTEP** = Remission of Duties and Taxes on Exported Products — India's WTO-compliant export incentive scheme (replaced **MEIS** in 2021).

### Formula

$$
\text{RoDTEP Rebate} = \text{FOB Value} \times \text{RoDTEP Rate (per HS Code)}
$$

### Worked Example — Basmati Rice (HS Code 1006.30)

- FOB value of shipment = **USD 5,00,000**
- Applicable RoDTEP rate for HS 1006.30 = **1.0%**
- Exchange rate (RBI reference) = **₹ 83.20 per USD**

### Step 1: Compute rebate in USD

$$
\text{Rebate} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.01 = \text{USD}\,5{,}000
$$

### Step 2: Convert to INR

$$
\text{Rebate in INR} = 5{,}000 \times 83.20 = \text{₹}\,4{,}16{,}000
$$

> **Answer:** The exporter will receive **₹ 4,16,000** as a rebate. This is debited to a scrip that can be used to pay **Basic Customs Duty** on future imports.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: GATT/WTO — Most-Favoured-Nation (MFN) Logic

**The MFN Rule (Article I of GATT 1994):**

> *If a member grants any advantage, favour, privilege, or immunity to the product of any other country, that same treatment must be extended **immediately and unconditionally** to the like product of all other WTO members.*

### Mathematical Representation

$$
\forall \, M_{i}, M_{j}, M_{k} \in \text{WTOMembers} : \quad T_{i \to k} = T_{i \to j}
$$

Where $T_{i \to k}$ is the tariff rate applied by member $i$ to imports from member $k$.

### Worked Example
India charges **30%** duty on wine from France. India wishes to give a **special rate of 20%** to Australia under a **bilateral FTA**.

- **Without MFN exception:** All WTO members (including France, Chile, Italy) would automatically demand 20% — eroding the benefit.
- **Solution under WTO:** India must invoke an **MFN exception** — either via a **Regional Trade Agreement (RTA) / FTA** notified under **Article XXIV of GATT 1994**, or under the **Enabling Clause** (for developing countries).

> **Result:** FTAs are the only legal way to grant preferential tariffs **without** extending them to the entire WTO membership.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How Indian Food Exports Are Governed

| Regulation | Authority | Mandate | Penalty for Non-Compliance |
|:-----------|:----------|:--------|:---------------------------|
| FTDR Act 1992 | DGFT | IEC mandatory | Cancellation of IEC |
| Customs Act 1962 | CBIC | Duty assessment, drawback | Seizure, confiscation |
| FEMA 1999 | RBI | Forex repatriation in 9 months | Penalty up to **3x the sum** involved |
| FSSAI Act 2006 | FSSAI | License for food units | Imprisonment up to **6 months** + fine |
| EIC Act 1963 | EIC | Pre-shipment inspection | Rejection of consignment |
| APEDA Act 1985 | APEDA | Registration for scheduled products | Cancellation of registration |
| ECGC | ECGC | Credit risk insurance | Premium forfeiture |
| RoDTEP / Drawback | CBIC | Rebate of embedded taxes | Refusal of scrip |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Indian food export governance is **multilayered**. A single shipment of mango pulp to Japan may require (a) IEC, (b) APEDA registration, (c) FSSAI license, (d) Phytosanitary Certificate from Plant Quarantine, (e) Certificate of Origin from Chamber of Commerce, (f) EIC pre-shipment inspection, (g) Customs clearance, (h) ECGC insurance, and (i) FEMA-compliant forex repatriation.

## 4.1 Diagram A: Indian Food Export — End-to-End Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C[Obtain FSSAI License and APEDA Registration]
    C --> D[Receive Export Order from Foreign Buyer]
    D --> E[Negotiate Terms: FOB or CIF]

    J --> K[Shipping Bill Filed and Let Export Order]
    K --> L[Bill of Lading or Airway Bill Issued]
    L --> M[Foreign Exchange Realization within 9 Months]
    M --> N[AD Bank Credits Export Proceeds to Exporter]
    N --> O[Claim RoDTEP Rebate and Drawback]

```

> **Reading Guide:** The flow moves from **left to right**. The exporter (left) progressively accumulates **statutory permissions and documentation** until the goods physically leave the Indian customs frontier. Post-shipment (right side), the focus shifts to **forex realization, bank reconciliation, and incentive claims**.

## 4.2 Diagram B: GATT-to-WTO Evolution Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[1947 GATT Signed] --> B[1948 Havana Charter and ITO Failure]
    B --> C[Kennedy Round 1964 to 1967]
    C --> D[Tokyo Round 1973 to 1979]
    D --> E[Uruguay Round 1986 to 1994]
    E --> F[1994 Marrakesh Agreement Signed 15 April]
    F --> G[1995 1 January WTO Officially Launched]
    G --> H[Doha Round 2001 to Present]
    H --> I[MC13 Abu Dhabi 2024]
```

> **Insight:** The single biggest conceptual shift happened between **1994 and 1995** — what was a *provisional treaty* (GATT) became a *permanent organization* (WTO) with binding dispute settlement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> F[Council for Trade in Services]
    B --> G[TRIPS Council]

```

> **Functional Reading:** Trade disputes in food exports typically originate at the **Committee on SPS Measures** (e.g., India's basmati rice being challenged for pesticide residue) and escalate to the **DSB** if bilateral consultations fail.

    B --> B2[Progressive Tariff Reduction]

```

> **Interpretation for India:** India has used the **Green Box** (e.g., MSP-based public stockholding, RKVY, PM-KISAN) to defend its farm support policies within the **5% de minimis** ceiling for developing countries, avoiding WTO challenge.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Member Raises Grievance] --> B[Consultations 60 Days]
    B --> C{Resolved?}

    E --> F[Panel Report within 6 Months]
    F --> G{Appealed?}

The **Multi-Party Interim Appeal Arbitration Arrangement (MPIA)** is a temporary workaround used by ~50+ members, including the EU and China — but **India and the USA are NOT members** of MPIA as of 2024.
